blurb: Cannon Mountain is an 8,652-foot granite summit in the Stuart Range (Wenatchee Mountains) of the Cascade Range, set within the Enchantments core of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness in Chelan County, Washington, about one mile north of Enchantment Peak. It has no maintained trail to its summit and is reached entirely by off-trail travel through the Enchantment Lakes basin; the peak's first ascent is recorded as 1921, though the climbing party is not documented in available sources.
hierarchyNote: None — the given name, coordinates (47.5028, -120.8025), and elevation (8,652 ft) match Cannon Mountain, Chelan County WA (confirmed by Peakbagger and Wikipedia at 47.5029, -120.8013 / 8,652 ft), and the Stuart Range / Enchantments parent grouping is correct: Cannon sits within the Stuart Range subrange of the Cascades, in the core Enchantments basin of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. Note this is a distinct peak from the well-known Cannon Mountain in New Hampshire (Franconia Notch) and Mount Cannon in Glacier NP, MT — search results frequently mix these in in the same result set, so care was taken to use only WA-specific sources.

routes.overview: The South Slopes is Cannon Mountain's standard, non-technical scramble line: it climbs through the Enchantment Lakes core basin to Prusik Pass, contours onto the Druid Plateau below the summit, then finishes up an exposed friction-slab ramp on the south side of the summit block.
routes.beta: There is no trail to the Cannon Mountain summit. Parties reach it via off-trail travel from the Enchantment Lakes basin, most commonly approached from the Snow Lakes Trailhead (Southeast Approach, ~12.8 mi RT / ~4,500 ft gain to a high point around 8,600 ft) or from the Stuart/Colchuck Lake Trailhead via Aasgard Pass. From Perfection Lake, a side trail climbs to Prusik Pass (~7,497 ft); from there the route contours northwest around 7,300-7,400 ft past the Lorelei Lakelets, climbs a rib to roughly 7,900-8,000 ft, then turns northwest up steep slopes onto the Druid Plateau (~8,350 ft). The route crosses the plateau to the peak's western slopes and finishes with Class 2-3 scrambling and a short, exposed friction-slab ramp on the summit block's south side. Because of the long, brushy, trail-less approach, most parties treat this as a 2-3 day trip with a camp in the core Enchantments (permit required) rather than a day climb.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Road (FR 7600) roughly 8.4 miles and turn left on FR 7601 (Eightmile Road), then continue about 3.7-4 miles on a steep, washboarded dirt road to the Stuart Lake Trailhead (47.5278, -120.8205, ~3,400 ft) — high-clearance strongly recommended. Alternatively continue on Icicle Road (FR 7600) about 4.2-4.3 miles from US 2 to the Snow Lakes Trailhead (47.5441, -120.7095, ~1,300 ft). From either trailhead, hike into the Enchantment Lakes core basin (via the Colchuck Lake Trail and Aasgard Pass, or via the Snow Lakes Trail and Snow Creek), passing Lake Viviane, Leprechaun, Sprite and Perfection Lakes, then take the side trail/use-path up to Prusik Pass before leaving all tread behind for the final off-trail push described above.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route: down-climb the summit ramp, cross the Druid Plateau, descend to Prusik Pass, and retrace the approach back through the Enchantment Lakes basin to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the friction-slab ramp on the south side of the summit block (exposed — take care if wet or icy), cross back over the Druid Plateau, and descend the steep slopes and rib to the basin below Prusik Pass. Reverse the northeast contour past the Lorelei Lakelets back to Prusik Pass, then drop to Perfection Lake and follow the outbound trail/use-path back to the Snow Lakes or Stuart/Colchuck trailhead. There are no rappels or fixed anchors on this route; all descent is by downclimbing and reversed hiking.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Snow Lakes or Stuart Lake Trailhead, hike in through the Enchantment Lakes basin to a permitted camp near Perfection Lake or the core lakes. Day 2: Alpine start from camp, climb via Prusik Pass and the Druid Plateau to the Cannon Mountain summit, return to camp. Day 3 (if used): hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: There is no fixed retreat infrastructure on this route. Below the summit block, simply reverse the scramble back onto the Druid Plateau. From the plateau or the approach slopes, retreat is by reversing the ascent line back to Prusik Pass and down to the Enchantment Lakes basin — there is no faster technical bail option, so turn around early if weather, snow conditions, or daylight are marginal.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, off-trail approach and full-value scramble finish, set a hard turnaround time before starting that accounts for the return trip in daylight; do not commit to the exposed summit block scramble if the party is already behind schedule, since down-climbing it in failing light or in a storm meaningfully raises the risk.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through October is the practical window; the approach usually carries lingering snow into late June in high-snow years, and FR 7601 typically doesn't open until May. Route-finding is easiest, and the summit ramp driest, from mid/late July through September; October brings stable weather but also heavy larch-season foot traffic to the lower basin.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard scramble; the crux is the short, exposed friction-slab ramp on the summit block, which most parties solo. Less experienced or exposure-averse parties sometimes carry a short rope, harness, and a sling/small rack to belay or protect that final section.
routes.proNeeds: Not a roped climb for most parties; if protecting the summit block ramp, natural gear (slings over horns/chockstones) is more useful than cams given the low-angle, featureless granite.
whatToBring: Ice axe and microspikes/crampons for lingering snow through early-mid summer
Overnight wilderness/Enchantment Permit Area paperwork if camping in the core
Bear canister or approved food storage
Extra water — reliable sources are limited on the Druid Plateau late season
Sun protection for extended time on open granite and plateau terrain
Optional short rope/harness for parties wanting protection on the summit block ramp
proTips: Break the long approach into two days by permitted camp near Perfection Lake rather than attempting Cannon as a car-to-car day trip.
Go in a stable, lower-snow window (late July-September) — June and early-July trip reports describe icy, avalanche-scoured couloirs and bulletproof ice on the approach slopes.
The summit block's friction-slab ramp is on its south side; other faces of the summit boulder are harder and not the standard line.
Overnight Enchantment permits are highly competitive (lottery, <10% success rate) — apply early or plan around the daily walk-up lottery.
watchOut: No maintained trail exists past the Enchantment Lakes basin — GPS/map and solid route-finding experience are effectively mandatory.
Early-season snow can obscure the correct rib/gully line up to the Druid Plateau, increasing the chance of a route-finding error onto steeper, more exposed terrain.
The summit block's exposed friction slab is significantly harder and riskier when wet, icy, or rimed.
knownHazards: Loose talus and blocky rock on the upper approach slopes and rib below the Druid Plateau.
Avalanche and steep snow/ice hazard on approach gullies into early summer.
Thick brush, deadfall, and cliff bands on off-trail sections below the basin.
High wind exposure on the open Druid Plateau and summit block.
Exposed, holdless friction-slab scrambling on the final summit block.

pitchDetail.notes: Trail to the Enchantment Lakes basin via Snow Lakes Trail or Colchuck Lake Trail/Aasgard Pass; typically an overnight backpack given permit rules.
Trail/use-path past Inspiration, Perfection, Leprechaun and Sprite Lakes to the Prusik Pass side trail (~7,497 ft).
Contour NW around 7,300-7,400 ft past the Lorelei Lakelets, climb a rib to ~7,900-8,000 ft, then turn NW up steep slopes to the ~8,350 ft Druid Plateau.
Cross the plateau (seasonal meltwater pools early season) to the peak's western slopes below the summit block.
Friction-slab ramp on the south side of the summit boulder leads to the true summit; low-angle but exposed and holdless in places.

routes.face: South side of the summit block, via the Druid Plateau approach

road.name: Icicle Road (FR 7600) to FR 7601 (Eightmile Road) for Stuart Lake TH; Icicle Road (FR 7600) for Snow Lakes TH
road.status: As of late May 2026, FR 7601/Stuart Lake Trailhead was reported open; Icicle Road (FR 7600) had multiple landslides and clogged culverts with debris affecting the Eightmile/Ida/Johnny Creek Campgrounds area — check current Forest Service alerts before driving, since this can add several miles of road-walking if impassable to vehicles.
road.seasonalGate: FR 7601 is gated and closed each winter, typically reopening by May.
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth: Icicle Road (FR 7600) ~8.4 mi, then left on FR 7601, ~3.7-4 mi of steep, washboarded dirt road to Stuart Lake Trailhead (high-clearance recommended). For Snow Lakes Trailhead, continue on FR 7600 ~4.2-4.3 mi from US 2.

climate.typical: Dry, sunny summers with afternoon thunderstorm potential; a heavy winter snowpack can linger on shaded approach slopes into July.
climate.spring: Significant avalanche and steep-snow hazard on approach gullies into June; access road (FR 7601) usually still gated through April.
climate.summer: Prime window from late June through September, though shaded gullies can still hold snow/verglass into early July per trip reports.
climate.fall: Often stable, dry weather through October; larch season (early-mid October) brings heavy hiker traffic to the lower Enchantment basin.
climate.winter: Road closed, deep snowpack; only attempted as technical winter mountaineering (a possible first winter ascent via a west-ridge line has been reported, distinct from this standard route).

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District — Alpine Lakes Wilderness / Enchantment Permit Area
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30 annual Northwest Forest Pass at the trailhead.
access.permit: Free self-issued day-use wilderness permit at the trailhead for day trips. Overnight camping anywhere in the Enchantment Permit Area (including the core basin used to access Cannon) requires a reserved overnight permit, required May 15-October 31, allocated mostly via a competitive advance lottery (roughly <10% success rate) plus a smaller daily walk-up lottery; the core zone is capped at 24 overnight campers/night.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (or equivalent day-use fee) for parking; separate overnight Enchantment Permit Area permit for camping.
access.closures: FR 7601 closed seasonally each winter. Icicle Road (FR 7600) has been subject to 2026 landslide/washout damage near Eightmile/Ida/Johnny Creek — verify current Forest Service road-condition alerts before the trip. Dogs are prohibited on Enchantment Permit Area trails including the Snow Lakes approach.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start from camp given the long, technical off-trail final push to the summit and return.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or RiverCom 911 dispatch (509) 663-9911; Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (509) 667-6851
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Leavenworth, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-5815, 24-hour ED; higher-level trauma care at Central Washington Hospital in Wenatchee.
emergency.notes: The core Enchantments basin and Cannon's summit are remote and off-trail with no cell coverage in most of the basin; expect long SAR response times given the trail-less approach terrain.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (8,652 ft) and coordinates (47.5028, -120.8025) match Peakbagger/Wikipedia closely and required no correction. Secondary sources vary slightly on summit elevation (8,638-8,656 ft) and prominence (838-922 ft) — these were not used to override the existing, better-sourced 8,652 ft figure. No route-specific first-ascent party could be sourced, so 'fa' was left null rather than attributing the 1921 peak first ascent (party undocumented) to this specific route.

blurb: Buckhorn Mountain (6,988 ft) is a double-summit peak in the Buckhorn Wilderness of the northeastern Olympic Mountains, Olympic National Forest, just outside Olympic National Park in Jefferson County, Washington. Like most of the Olympics it is built of steep, loose sedimentary/basaltic rock; its two summits, separated by a shallow saddle about 1,200 feet apart, were named for their resemblance to deer antlers. It is the most popular scramble objective in the wilderness, reached via the Big Quilcene/Tubal Cain trail system over Marmot Pass; no notable technical first-ascent record exists, consistent with its history as a walk-up/scramble peak.

routes.overview: A non-technical but sustained Class 2-3 scramble up Buckhorn's southwest ridge and summit block, reached by a long subalpine approach over Marmot Pass -- the classic and by far most popular way up Buckhorn Mountain.
routes.beta: From Marmot Pass, a boot path heads north along the Tubal Cain Trail for about 100 meters before a use trail branches uphill onto Buckhorn's southwest ridge, crossing a minor ~6,800' knob before reaching the southwest (true) summit at 6,988'. The final approach involves loose talus and a short bit of hands-on Class 3 scrambling up onto the summit boulders; a Class 2-3 downclimb on the east side leads to the saddle toward the slightly lower east summit (about 1,200 ft away, ~32 ft lower).
routes.approach: Drive to the Upper Big Quilcene Trailhead (2,477 ft) via Penny Creek Rd and FS Roads 27/2750 from US-101 near Quilcene. Hike the Upper Big Quilcene Trail #833 about 5.3 miles, gaining roughly 3,500 ft through old-growth forest, past Shelter Rock/Boulder Shelter and Camp Mystery (~4.5 mi, last reliable water) to Marmot Pass (~5,900-6,100 ft). From the pass, continue about 100 m north on the Tubal Cain Trail to a boot-path junction, then follow the way trail up the ridge roughly 1 more mile and 900 ft to the southwest summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route: downclimb to the ridge, follow the boot path back to the Tubal Cain Trail junction, then descend over Marmot Pass and out the Upper Big Quilcene Trail #833 to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the Class 2-3 boot path/talus back to the ridge saddle and knob, rejoin the descending use trail down to the Tubal Cain Trail junction just north of Marmot Pass, cross the pass, and retrace the Upper Big Quilcene Trail #833 approximately 5.3 miles back to the trailhead. No rappels or technical gear are needed in summer conditions; move carefully on the loose talus near the summit and, in early season, on any residual snow near the pass.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a long day hike/scramble (roughly 8.5-9.5 hrs round trip) or as a summit side-trip from an overnight base camp at Boulder Shelter (~1.7 mi below Marmot Pass) or Camp Mystery (~4.5 mi up-trail); many parties backpack in on day 1, scramble Buckhorn (and often the neighboring unnamed 6,599' peak) on day 2, and hike out on day 3.
routes.bail: The route follows a single approach trail with no technical commitment -- retreat at any point simply means returning downhill along the same boot path/ridge to Marmot Pass and out Trail #833; no fixed anchors or rappels are involved.
routes.turnaround: Because the upper mountain is fully exposed above treeline with several miles of ridge and trail to descend, start early enough to be off the ridge before typical afternoon thunderstorms or deteriorating weather develop, and turn back at any sign of incoming lightning, high wind, or whiteout conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed mid-summer through early fall (roughly July-September) once snow has cleared from Marmot Pass and the upper ridge; the approach trail can hold snow into June in a heavy year, and use continues through September before fall storms arrive.
routes.proNeeds: None required -- this is a Class 2-3 scramble with no fixed protection; a helmet is recommended given the loose rock and the chance of other parties above on the boot path.
gear: layered clothing for the exposed ridge
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass (required for trailhead parking)
water filter/treatment (last reliable water at Camp Mystery)
extra water for the dry upper ridge
bear-resistant food storage for overnight camping
headlamp if starting or finishing in the dark
proTips: Fill water at Camp Mystery (~4.5 mi up-trail) -- it's the last reliable source before the dry, exposed ridge to the summit.
Start early to be off the summit ridge before typical Olympic afternoon thunderstorms build.
A Northwest Forest Pass ($5/day or $30/year) is required to park at the Upper Big Quilcene Trailhead.
Basing at Boulder Shelter or Camp Mystery turns the trip into an easy overnight and makes the summit a short side-scramble rather than one long day push.
watchOut: Loose, small talus on a steep grade near the summit makes footing unreliable and creates rockfall risk from parties above.
Snow and cornices can linger on the pass and upper ridge into early summer.
The final approach to the true summit requires brief Class 3 hand/foot scrambling with some exposure.
Marmot Pass and the summit meadows see heavy weekend traffic; camp only in existing, durable sites -- fires are prohibited above 3,500 ft, including at Camp Mystery and Marmot Pass.
knownHazards: Loose rock/talus on the final summit scramble
afternoon thunderstorms on the fully exposed summit ridge
residual snow and cornices near Marmot Pass in early season
faint boot path and route-finding difficulty above treeline

pitchDetail.notes: ~4.5 mi, gaining most of the ~3,500 ft to the pass through old-growth forest; last reliable water at Camp Mystery.
~0.8 mi of steeper, more open trail gaining the final few hundred feet to the pass (~5,900-6,100 ft).
Boot path heads north on the Tubal Cain Trail, then a use trail climbs the ridge over a minor knob.
Short scramble with some hand use over talus/boulders to the true summit; downclimb east side (Class 2-3) reaches the saddle toward the lower east summit.

road.name: Forest Road 27 / FS Road 2750 (Big Quilcene Rd)
road.status: Gravel Forest Service road, generally passable in dry season; check Olympic NF alerts for washout closures
road.driveNote: From US-101 near Quilcene, take Penny Creek Rd ~1.5 mi, turn right onto FS Road 27, continue ~9-9.5 mi, then turn left onto FS Road 2750 for ~4.7-5 mi to the Upper Big Quilcene Trailhead (2,477 ft). High clearance is not required but the road can be potholed/washboarded.

climate.typical: Wet maritime climate typical of the Olympics; snow lingers into early summer at Marmot Pass, with drier, clearer weather July-September and returning fall storms by October.
climate.spring: Trail to Marmot Pass is often still snow-covered into June, with postholing and avalanche runout risk on the upper trail.
climate.summer: Best conditions July-September; wildflower meadows around Marmot Pass peak in late July-August.
climate.fall: Cooler, drier air but shorter days and increasing storm frequency from October onward.
climate.winter: Heavy snow with the access road often unplowed/impassable; a winter objective only for experienced, self-sufficient parties.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service - Olympic National Forest (Quilcene Ranger District)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful pass required at the trailhead ($5/vehicle/day or $30/year)
access.permit: No permit required for day use; overnight camping in Buckhorn Wilderness is subject to group-size limits (12 people/8 head of stock max) and no-campfire rules above 3,500 ft
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: No open fires or wood gathering above 3,500 ft elevation (includes Camp Mystery and Marmot Pass); trailhead road and trail are subject to seasonal snow and washout closures -- check Olympic National Forest alerts before going

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (pre-dawn/sunrise) given the length of the approach and the afternoon-thunderstorm risk on the exposed summit ridge

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Jeffcom 911 (Jefferson County Communications) -- dial 911 for emergencies; non-emergency line 360-344-9779
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Forest, Quilcene Office, 295142 Highway 101 S, Quilcene, WA 98376, (360) 765-2200
emergency.nearestHospital: Jefferson Healthcare, 834 Sheridan St, Port Townsend, WA (25-bed critical access hospital)
emergency.notes: Search and rescue in this area (outside Olympic National Park) is coordinated by the Jefferson County Sheriff's Office with volunteer Jefferson Search and Rescue (JSAR).

routes.corrections: Minor cross-source elevation variance only: Wikipedia and SummitPost give 6,988 ft, matching the provided value; one Mazamas trip listing cites 6,996 ft. Treated as normal survey/measurement tolerance -- no change made to the provided elevation. Provided coordinates (47.825501, -123.121713) also closely match Wikipedia's cited coordinates (47.825574, -123.121867), well within GPS/survey rounding.

blurb: Cashmere Mountain (8,514 ft) is a massive, pyramid-shaped summit of Late Cretaceous tonalite rising roughly 6,500 ft above the Icicle Creek drainage in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Leavenworth, WA, forming the southern terminus of the Chiwaukum Range just west of the Stuart Range/Enchantments across the valley. Its distinctive multi-toothed west ridge — a series of gendarmes separated by five main gaps — is the mountain's classic scramble line. The peak itself was first climbed in 1900 by USGS surveyor Robert A. Farmer during a topographic survey.
hierarchyNote: Name, coordinates (47.5586, -120.8472 vs. Peakbagger/Wikipedia's 47.5587, -120.8470), and elevation (8,514 ft) all check out against Peakbagger, Wikipedia, and SummitPost — this is unambiguously the correct peak. One nuance: Cashmere Mountain sits in the Wenatchee Mountains, at the southern tip of the Chiwaukum Range, on the Icicle Creek (west) side of the valley — it is geographically adjacent to but not part of the Stuart Range/Enchantments massif proper (those lie across Icicle Creek to the southeast). Bucketing it under "Stuart-Enchantments" is defensible since climbers reach it via the same Icicle Creek/Eightmile corridor and Enchantment Permit Area used for Stuart/Enchantments approaches, but it is not topographically part of that range — flagging for awareness, not as an error requiring a fix.

routes.overview: Cashmere's standard route: a long valley-and-lake approach through the Alpine Lakes Wilderness followed by an off-trail ridge scramble that weaves through a series of rock gendarmes to the summit, switching from the ridge's north side to its south side and back as it threads five major gaps.
routes.beta: After roughly 8-9 miles of largely gentle trail past Little Eightmile Lake and Lake Caroline to Windy Pass, the route leaves the maintained trail, climbs open basins to gain the crest of Cashmere's west ridge near 7,500 ft, then follows the ridge north and traverses talus east to the col between the West Peak and main summit. From there parties scramble the upper ridge's gendarmes, generally staying below Class 4 by weaving around towers on the north or south side as needed. Standard conditions are Class 3 with short Class 4 moves; rime, verglas, or lingering snow (common into July on north-facing sections) can push the climbing to Class 4-5 and require a rope/light rack.
routes.approach: From the Eightmile Trailhead (3,300 ft, reached via Icicle Road/FR 7600 then FR 7601 from Leavenworth), follow the Eightmile Lake Trail up the Eightmile Creek drainage to Little Eightmile Lake (~2.8 mi, 4,415 ft). Continue on the spur trail toward Lake Caroline and Little Caroline Lake, then climb to Windy Pass/Windy Basin (~6,500 ft) where the maintained tread ends. From Windy Basin, go off-trail northeast through meadows, brush, and creek crossings to a small flat meadow near 7,100 ft, cross it, and continue northeast to gain a rocky south ridge around 7,500 ft; follow this ridge crest north to about 8,000 ft, then traverse east over talus to the col between Cashmere's West Peak and main summit.
routes.descent: Descend the West Ridge by the same route in reverse, reversing the gendarme-avoidance line used on ascent.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb back to the col below the West Peak and retrace the ridge crest south, weaving around (rather than over) the gendarmes to stay near Class 3. Drop off the ridge crest near 7,500 ft back into the basin, then follow the off-trail descent through meadows and talus back to the Windy Pass trail. Some parties instead descend moderately steep, crusty north-facing snowfields between rock outcrops (using snow pickets in early season) when conditions favor it, rejoining the standard approach lower down; this variation requires current snow/rime information and is not the default line.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat this as a 2-day (occasionally 3-day) trip: Day 1 hike from the Eightmile Trailhead to a camp near Lake Caroline or in Windy Basin (~5-5.5 hrs); Day 2 climb the west ridge to the summit and return to camp (roughly 4-5 hrs up, 5 hrs down); Day 3 (if used) hike out to the trailhead. Very fit parties complete it car-to-car in a single long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward along the ascent line: downclimb to the col and follow the ridge crest back to the basin and Windy Pass trail; there is no technical rappelling built into the standard descent, so bailing simply means reversing the scramble at any point before committing to a gendarme.
routes.turnaround: Because the upper ridge is long, exposed, and mostly off-trail with no fixed retreat except reversing the ridge, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight and margin for deteriorating weather to regain the col and descend the full basin-to-trailhead approach before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September offers the driest, most snow-free conditions on the upper ridge. Snow and rime can linger on north-facing sections into July in heavy-snow years, and shoulder-season attempts (May-June, or September-October) commonly find hard snow or rime-covered rock on the ridge that raises the climbing to Class 4 and warrants an ice axe and crampons.
routes.rockGrade: Class 3-4 (occasional low 5th class on gendarmes/variations, avoidable with route-finding)
objHaz: Loose, exfoliating tonalite rock on the ridge gendarmes
Rime ice and verglas on rock in shoulder-season conditions
Lingering steep snowfields on north-facing slopes near the summit block
Long off-trail route-finding above Windy Pass with no cairned tread
Afternoon thunderstorm exposure on the open ridge crest
routes.detailedRack: Not a technical rope route in standard summer conditions; the ridge is scrambled unroped by most parties. In early/late season or when rime/verglas coats the rock, some parties carry a rope and light rack (a few slings and small-to-medium chocks) for running belays across the more exposed gendarme sections.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection or bolts; any protection is trad gear placed opportunistically in the ridge's crack systems, used mainly as a safety net for exposed shoulder-season scrambling rather than for sustained technical climbing.
gear: Ice axe (early/late season or after fresh snow)
Light rack (slings, small-medium chocks) for shoulder-season variations
Map/compass or GPS for off-trail navigation above Windy Pass
whatToBring: Overnight camping gear if splitting the approach into 2 days (common camps near Lake Caroline or Windy Basin)
Extra water — the ridge itself is dry once off the lake-basin approach
Layers for wind and exposure on the open ridge crest
Sun protection — much of the route is above treeline
Northwest Forest Pass (day use) or overnight wilderness permit
proTips: Break the long approach into two days by camping near Lake Caroline or in Windy Basin, then climb the ridge with a light summit pack.

The ridge's gendarmes can be passed on either the north or south side — study route photos beforehand and stay flexible in the moment to keep the climbing at Class 3 rather than committing to Class 4 towers.

Check recent trip reports for current snow/rime conditions on the upper ridge before committing to a shoulder-season attempt; conditions swing the route from Class 3 scramble to Class 4-5 mixed climbing.
watchOut: Loose, exfoliating rock typical of the Wenatchee Mountains tonalite — test every hold on the gendarmes.
The route above Windy Pass is entirely off-trail with no cairns or maintained tread, making route-finding the crux for many parties.
Rime-ice-covered rock in shoulder-season conditions can turn a Class 3 scramble into low 5th-class mixed climbing.
The open ridge crest offers no shelter from afternoon thunderstorms or wind.
knownHazards: Loose rock and exfoliating tonalite on the ridge gendarmes increases rockfall risk to parties below.
Rime ice and verglas coat the rock in shoulder-season conditions, raising the technical difficulty and fall consequences.
Steep snowfields on north-facing slopes near the summit persist into early summer in heavy-snow years.
Long, mostly off-trail travel above Windy Pass raises navigation and time-management risk, especially in fog or whiteout.

pitchDetail.pitch: Eightmile Trailhead to Little Eightmile Lake
pitchDetail.notes: ~2.8 mi maintained trail, gains from 3,300 ft to ~4,415 ft.

pitchDetail.pitch: Little Eightmile Lake to Windy Pass/Windy Basin
pitchDetail.notes: Spur trail past Lake Caroline and Little Caroline Lake to ~6,500 ft where maintained tread ends.

pitchDetail.pitch: Windy Basin to base of west ridge (~7,500 ft)
pitchDetail.notes: Cross-country through meadows, brush, and creeks to a rocky south ridge, then north along the crest.

pitchDetail.pitch: Ridge crest to col below West Peak (~8,000 ft)
pitchDetail.notes: Ridge walking with a talus traverse east to the col between West Peak and the main summit.

pitchDetail.pitch: Col to main summit via upper ridge gendarmes
pitchDetail.notes: Weave around or over a series of gendarmes separated by five main gaps; route-finding keeps most parties at Class 3.

road.name: Icicle Road (FR 7600) to Eightmile Creek Road (FR 7601)
road.status: Gravel forest road; high-clearance vehicle recommended, becomes washboarded later in the season
road.seasonalGate: FR 7601 closed to vehicles in winter (roughly November-May) to protect the roadbed
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow Icicle Road (FR 7600) about 8 miles, then turn left on FR 7601 near Bridge Creek Campground and follow it about 3 miles to the Eightmile Trailhead.

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades climate: drier and sunnier than the west-side crest, with hot summer days and cold, snowy winters.
climate.spring: Snow lingers on north-facing ridge sections into June; access road may still be gated into May.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Sep); afternoon thunderstorms possible on the exposed ridge.
climate.fall: Stable weather often into September-October, but early snow can return by mid-October.
climate.winter: Deep snow and road closure make the standard route impractical without ski/snowshoe mountaineering.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District — Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30 annual Northwest Forest Pass for day use at the Eightmile Trailhead.
access.permit: Free self-issued day-use wilderness permit at the trailhead; paid overnight camping permit required May 15-Oct 31 through Recreation.gov's Enchantment Permit Area lottery/walk-up system.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass (day use)
access.closures: Access road FR 7601 closed to vehicles in winter; dogs are prohibited within the Enchantment Permit Area; group size limited to 8.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/alpine start if attempting car-to-car in one long day; most parties instead split the trip and start the summit push at first light from a basin camp.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff (509) 667-6851 (non-emergency); 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — 24/7 Emergency Department, (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness route with a long, mostly off-trail approach; self-rescue capability and a filed trip plan are important given the distance from the trailhead to the upper ridge.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (8,514 ft) and coordinates (47.5586, -120.8472) are confirmed accurate against Peakbagger and Wikipedia (47.5587, -120.8470) — no correction needed. Note that secondary sources disagree modestly on round-trip elevation gain/distance depending on route variation taken (Mazamas: ~5,300 ft gain; a GPS-tracked trip report logged 6,014 ft total ascent/5,201 ft net gain over 18.2 mi; another multi-day TR logged ~5,600 ft gain/loss over ~18 mi). I used the Mazamas-cited round figure (5,300 ft) as the representative gainFt value and flag the ~700-900 ft spread here rather than presenting false precision.

blurb: Pinto Rock's West Face is the western-most wall of a ~400-ft welded-tuff breccia crag rising to roughly 5,123 ft in the southern Washington Cascades, inside the Gifford Pinchot National Forest (Skamania County). It's the aspect seen first while driving up Forest Road 77, with knob-studded volcanic rock climbed on a mix of bolts and, on older lines, hitched knobs/slings; the wall carries only two documented routes, including the area classic Wild Wild West.

routes.season: Jun-Sep (snow lingers above ~4,000 ft into early summer per local trip reports)
routes.approach: From Randle, WA: head south on Hwy 131, follow NF-25 for 19.2 miles; after milepost 21, turn left at the Mosquito Meadows sign onto NF-28 and go 2.7 miles, then turn left onto NF-77 (rough gravel, drive slow) for about 2 miles to the main pullout, where the climber's trail up to the West Face is visible.
routes.turnaround: Standard alpine-rock practice applies: set a firm turnaround time before committing to the upper wall so descent/rappel is completed well before dark, and retreat early if weather or rockfall risk increases.
routes.bestSeason: Snow can linger on the approach and wall above ~4,000 ft into early summer; best conditions come after a sustained stretch of warm, dry weather has cleared the crag, typically mid-summer into early fall.
whatToBring: helmet (loose knobs/cobbles reported on the formation)
sun protection - the West Face is fully sun-exposed
watchOut: Rock quality: knobs and cobbles embedded in the welded-tuff can peel off under body weight - test holds before committing.
NF-77 is rough gravel; go slow and expect high-clearance conditions.
knownHazards: Welded-tuff breccia rock is studded with loose knobs/cobbles that can detach.
Remote National Forest location with limited services.

routes.corrections: Could not verify a route named 'Nest Route' on Pinto Rock's West Face in any required primary source. Mountain Project's West Face area page explicitly lists only 2 total documented climbs there (Pony Express 5.8 and Wild Wild West 5.8); SummitPost, theCrag, and PeakBagger were also checked and returned no match for this name. Route-specific stats (grade, FA, pitches, length, etc.) are left null; only general Pinto Rock/West Face approach, access, and area facts are included above, which apply to the wall regardless of the specific line.

routes.overview: A four-pitch adventure sport route up the lowest toe and open face of Pinto Rock's West Face, mixing knobby face climbing on lower pitches with an exposed, bolt-protected headwall finish near the summit.
routes.beta: Fully bolted 4-pitch line starting on a short prow at the base of the West Face. P1 climbs the opening face past ramps to a ledge belay; P2 works a shallow groove with a traverse; P3 follows bolts up an exposed face to a hanging belay; P4 climbs up and left from the hanging belay with good exposure onto the face and the summit. Rated a 3.5-star classic with wild exposure and summit-register access.
routes.fa: Jim Kimball, Jim Yoder, Brenden Sullivan (ground-up)
routes.rappels: Rappel down the back (east) side of the formation near a stand of fir trees
routes.approach: From Randle, WA: south on Hwy 131, NF-25 for 19.2 miles, left at the Mosquito Meadows sign onto NF-28 for 2.7 miles, then left onto NF-77 (rough, drive slow) for about 2 miles to the main pullout with the climber's trail. Walk up the road and directly to the wall; the route starts on the lowest toe of the West Face - look for the first two bolts on a short prow.
routes.descent: Rappel the back (east) side near a stand of fir trees, or hike around the north end of the formation back to the base.
routes.descentText: From the summit, either rappel down the back side of the formation near a stand of fir trees, or descend eastward and hike around the north end of Pinto Rock back to the base of the West Face - climbers report this walk-off descent as the more common option.
routes.itinerary: Single-day outing: drive to the NF-77 pullout, approach directly to the wall base (short walk), climb 4 pitches (Grade II, a few hours round trip for a competent team), then rappel or walk off.
routes.bail: Fully bolted anchors exist at each pitch's belay; a party can lower/rappel from any stance back to the ground on the bolted line if needed.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that keeps descent (rappel or walk-off) well clear of dark and of any incoming weather; the sun-exposed face and loose knobs make an unhurried, daylight descent important.
routes.bestSeason: Snow lingers above roughly 4,000 ft into early summer, so the wall is best climbed after a sustained warm spell has dried it out - typically mid-summer through early fall. The West Face is fully sun-exposed and can be hot in midsummer.
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted sport route - no trad rack needed. Bring alpine draws to manage rope drag on the wandering line; roughly a dozen draws total is recommended for all 4 pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Bolts only on all 4 pitches; carry about a dozen quickdraws/alpine draws and a 60-70m rope for leading and lowering/rappelling.
whatToBring: helmet (rock is knobby and pieces have been reported to break off)
approach shoes suitable for the walk-off descent
proTips: Route starts on the lowest toe of the West Face - identify the first two bolts on a short prow before racking up.
Bring a full dozen alpine draws; the line wanders enough that rope drag is a real concern without them.
Sign the summit register at the top of P4.
Descend by walking off around the north end rather than rappelling if you're unfamiliar with the back-side raps.
watchOut: Be careful weighting 'jugs'/knobs - pieces of the welded-tuff have been reported to peel off.
P3's hanging belay and P4's exposure mean a confident second is important.
Fully sun-exposed face - can be very hot in summer with no shade.
knownHazards: Welded-tuff breccia is studded with knobs/cobbles that can detach under load.
Sustained exposure on the P3-P4 headwall.
Remote, high-elevation crag with limited shade and no on-site water.

pitchDetail.notes: Opening face past ramps to a ledge belay, ~120 ft
Shallow groove with a traverse, ~110 ft
Bolted, exposed face to a hanging belay, ~80 ft
Clip a bolt up and left from the hanging belay, exposed face move onto the summit push, ~90 ft

routes.corrections: Peak elevation was null in the given data. Cross-checked sources disagree on exactly which figure represents the true Pinto Rock summit vs. the crag/wall base: PeakBagger and ListsOfJohn give the true summit at 5,123 ft (also cited elsewhere as 5,118 ft), while Mountain Project's Pinto Rock area page lists 4,821 ft (likely the crag approach/base elevation rather than the true high point) and its West Face sub-area page lists 4,747 ft at the wall's toe. Set highPointFt to 5,123 ft (summit, cross-confirmed by PeakBagger + ListsOfJohn) and used 4,747 ft as the West Face base-of-route waypoint elevation (Mountain Project). Also corrected county: some general web sources associate this area with Lewis County (nearby Randle, WA is in Lewis County), but Pinto Rock's actual summit sits in Skamania County per PeakBagger/TopoZone/HomeTownLocator/Northwest Waterfall Survey.

blurb: Enchantment Peak (8,538 ft) is a granite summit in the Stuart Range within the Enchantments basin of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Chelan County, Washington, first climbed by a Fred Beckey party in May 1948. It has two closely-spaced summits set amid the glacier-carved granite slabs and alpine lakes of the Enchantments — a broader northeast summit (confirmed by a modern ground survey to be the true high point) reached by an easy class 2-3 scramble from Prusik Pass, and a narrow, highly exposed southwest summit fin requiring class 4-to-low-5th-class climbing.

objHaz: sustained exposure on the SW summit fin
routes.face: South side of the SW summit block; NE (true) summit is reached from the west via Prusik Pass
routes.overview: A high alpine scramble to the twin summits of Enchantment Peak in the heart of the Enchantments basin: an easy, low-class-3 walk-up to the true (NE) summit, plus an optional, far more serious class 4-to-low-5th scramble out to the exposed SW summit fin that gives the route its name.
routes.beta: From Prusik Pass — reached either via Aasgard Pass and the upper Enchantments basin from Colchuck Lake, or via the Snow Lakes/Prusik Pass trail from the north — head west/northwest and work up talus and granite slabs toward the NE summit: mostly class 2 with one short class-3 step to a flat, roomy summit block. The connecting ridge runs about half a mile to the SW summit, where the route crosses talus on the south side of the summit block before a short but heavily exposed class-4 (some rate it low 5th class) scramble up a narrow rock fin reported to be only about 18 inches wide and several feet long.
routes.approach: Most parties now approach via the Stuart Lake Trailhead (3,400 ft; end of FS Road 7601 off Icicle Road): hike the Stuart/Colchuck Lake Trail roughly 4-5 miles to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft), then climb the steep, loose talus/scree of Aasgard Pass (about 2,300 ft of gain in roughly one mile) to 7,841 ft. From the pass, cross the upper Enchantments basin past the Isolation/Tranquil/Perfection Lakes area toward Prusik Pass, then head west up talus and slab toward the Enchantment Peak summits. An alternative, longer northern approach follows the Snow Lakes Trail from the Snow Lakes Trailhead (1,300-1,350 ft, Icicle Road) past Nada and Snow Lakes to Prusik Pass directly, which avoids Aasgard Pass but adds significant mileage and elevation gain.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back to Prusik Pass, then either back over Aasgard Pass to Colchuck Lake or out the Snow Lakes Trail, depending on which approach was used.
routes.descentText: From either summit, downclimb the same class 2-4 terrain used on the ascent back to Prusik Pass. If you came via Aasgard Pass, descend the loose talus/scree back to Colchuck Lake with care — it is notably steep and easy to dislodge rock onto parties below; some climbers glissade snow-filled sections in early season if conditions allow, otherwise it's a slow, careful scree descent. From Colchuck Lake, follow the Colchuck/Stuart Lake Trail back to the Stuart Lake Trailhead. If you approached via Snow Lakes, reverse the Prusik Pass area down past Nada/Snow Lakes and out the Snow Lakes Trail to Icicle Road.
routes.itinerary: Typically done either as a very long single day (12-16+ hrs car-to-car) from the Stuart Lake or Snow Lakes trailhead, or as a 2-3 day backpack with a permitted camp in the Core Enchantment Zone (e.g., near the upper lakes), allowing an unladen day to tag both the NE and SW summits plus neighboring peaks such as Little Annapurna and McClellan Peak.
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent line at any point below the SW summit's exposed fin — no fixed rappel or anchor is reported on this route, so parties without a rope for the final SW moves should simply turn back to the NE summit or down into the basin.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm, conservative turnaround time before committing to the SW summit's exposed class-4 fin — descending the loose Aasgard Pass talus or the exposed summit terrain in the dark, in a storm, or while fatigued materially raises accident risk on this peak; if conditions or time run short, the NE (true) summit alone is a satisfying, far safer objective.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is roughly July through October, once the Aasgard Pass talus and summit slabs have shed most seasonal snow. Access is possible into early July in low-snow years or as late as October, when larch color peaks in the basin but overnight freezes and early-season snow/ice become more likely on the approach and summit block.
routes.detailedRack: Most parties carry no technical rack for the NE (true) summit, which is a class-2-3 walk-up. For the SW summit — an exposed, narrow rock fin rated class 4 to low 5th class by different observers — many strong scramblers solo it, but some parties carry a 30m rope, a few slings, and carabiners to belay or rope-solo the final exposed moves given the lack of any reported fixed protection.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed gear is reported on the SW summit fin; any protection is opportunistic (slung horns/chockstones). A short, light rack (a few nuts/slings) is enough for parties who choose to protect the final exposed moves.
gear: 30m rope (optional, for the SW summit fin)
Small rack: a few slings and carabiners
whatToBring: Overnight wilderness permit if camping in the Core Enchantment Zone
Sun protection (extended time on exposed granite)
Extra warm/wind layers for the exposed ridge
proTips: The NE summit — confirmed by a 2020s theodolite/LiDAR ground survey to be 6-7 ft taller than the SW summit — is the true high point and is a mellow, low-class-3 walk-up from Prusik Pass; most peakbaggers only need to tag this summit.
The SW summit's narrow, exposed fin is optional and significantly harder (class 4 to low 5th); rope up if you're not comfortable soloing highly exposed, unprotected rock.

Approaching via Colchuck Lake and Aasgard Pass is the faster, more direct option in summer; the Snow Lakes/Prusik Pass approach from the north is longer but avoids Aasgard Pass's notoriously loose, steep talus.
October brings peak larch color but also the season's first snow and ice on the talus approaching Aasgard Pass and the summit block — carry microspikes/an axe if climbing late.
watchOut: Aasgard Pass is a steep, loose talus/scree slope (about 2,300 ft of gain in roughly a mile) that is dangerous to ascend or descend when wet, snow-covered, or icy.

The SW summit fin is reported to be only about 18 inches wide and highly exposed on multiple sides — falls here would likely be serious or fatal; confirm which summit you actually need before committing.
No dogs are allowed anywhere in the Enchantment Permit Area.
Afternoon thunderstorms and sudden weather changes are common on this exposed granite terrain above 8,000 ft.
knownHazards: Loose talus and scree on the Aasgard Pass approach and on the ridge toward the summit.
Sustained, unprotected exposure on the SW summit's narrow rock fin.
Early- and late-season snow and ice on the approach slopes and summit block (reported as late as early October).
Route-finding difficulty in the maze of granite slabs and lakes of the Enchantments basin, especially in poor visibility.

pitchDetail.notes: ~4-5 mi from Stuart Lake Trailhead, gaining to the 5,570 ft lake
~2,300 ft of gain in about 1 mile; steep, loose, exposed to rockfall from other parties above
Crosses the upper Enchantments basin past the Isolation/Tranquil/Perfection Lakes area
Walk-up with one short class-3 step to the flat summit block (true high point per ground survey)
~0.5 mi connecting ridge; drops onto talus on the south side of the SW summit block
Narrow, ~18-inch-wide exposed rock fin; no fixed protection reported, some parties rope up

road.name: Icicle Road / FS Road 7601 (to Stuart Lake TH) or FS Road 7600 (to Snow Lakes TH)
road.status: Paved to gravel; FS 7601 is steep, washboarded, high-clearance/4x4 recommended
road.seasonalGate: FS Road 7601 to the Stuart Lake Trailhead is gated and closed to vehicles every winter
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth via US 2, drive Icicle Road ~8.4-8.5 miles then turn onto FS Road 7601 and follow it ~3.7-4 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead; the Snow Lakes Trailhead is 4.3 miles up Icicle Road/FS 7600 directly.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Central Cascades zone (avalanche forecasts issued in winter/spring only; NWAC does not forecast during the summer climbing season)
climate.typical: Maritime-influenced climate with heavy winter snowfall; granite terrain at this 8,500+ ft elevation holds snow into early summer.
climate.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack on approach slopes and summit terrain; avalanche danger and difficult access if Icicle Road/FS 7601 is still gated.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); dry granite and warm days, but afternoon thunderstorms and sudden weather shifts are possible at elevation.
climate.fall: Larch season (late Sep-early Oct) brings good weather windows but also the season's first snow/ice on the approach and summit block; nights turn cold.
climate.winter: Deep snow, gated access roads, and high avalanche hazard make this essentially a non-climbing-season objective.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District; Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Core Enchantment Permit Area
access.fees: $5 per vehicle per day (or $30 annual Recreation Pass) at the Stuart Lake or Snow Lakes trailheads; day-use wilderness permits are free and self-issued at the trailhead.
access.permit: Overnight camping within the Core Enchantment Permit Area (which covers this approach and basin) requires an advance-reservation overnight permit for May 15-Oct 31, allocated via recreation.gov. Day trips without an overnight camp only need the free self-issued day-use wilderness permit.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest/Recreation Pass or $5 day-use fee at the trailhead; a separate Enchantment overnight permit is required to camp.
access.closures: FS Road 7601 to the Stuart Lake Trailhead is closed to vehicles every winter; Icicle Road has had seasonal camping restrictions in recent years — check current Wenatchee River Ranger District alerts before a trip. Dogs are prohibited throughout the Enchantment Permit Area.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start — Aasgard Pass and the summit terrain are best tackled with cool rock and a long daylight margin

waypoints.name: Enchantment Peak (NE summit, true high point)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 (Chelan County emergency dispatch) — call 911, or (509) 663-9911; Chelan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (509) 667-6851
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA — (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA — 24-hour ER, (509) 548-3420
emergency.notes: The Enchantments basin is remote backcountry with generally poor/no cell coverage; Chelan County Volunteer Search & Rescue supports mountain SAR operations here — activate via 911.

routes.corrections: The given peak elevation (8,538 ft) and coordinates (47.4875, -120.806389) correspond to the NE summit per Wikipedia/USGS data and are consistent with sourced data - no numeric correction needed. However, a 2020s ground survey using a theodolite plus LiDAR cross-check (countryhighpoints.com) found the NE summit is actually 6-7 ft taller than the SW summit, making the NE summit (an easy class 2-3 walk-up from Prusik Pass) the true high point - not the harder, narrower SW summit fin that gives this route ('Southwest Scramble') its name. Peakbagger also lists the SW summit as a separate subsidiary entry (pid 84173), reflecting this same ambiguity. Climbers relying on this route page should be aware the SW objective, while the more technically serious scramble, is very likely not the actual summit of the peak.

blurb: Eldorado Peak (8,872 ft) rises at the head of Marble Creek in North Cascades National Park, on the western edge of the largest contiguous non-volcanic ice sheet in the lower 48 states (the Eldorado–Inspiration–Klawatti glacier complex). Composed of Cretaceous biotite-granodiorite orthogneiss, it is famous for its dramatic knife-edge summit ridge and was dubbed "Queen of the Cascade River" by Fred Beckey. The peak was first climbed via its East Ridge on August 27, 1933 by Donald Blair, Norval Grigg, Arthur Winder, and Arthur Wilson.
hierarchyNote: None — peak name, elevation (8,872–8,873 ft), and coordinates (48.5374, -121.1345) are corroborated by Peakbagger and Wikipedia (48°32'15"N 121°08'04"W), and the North Cascades National Park / Marble Creek parent area is correct for this peak.

routes.fa: Donald Blair, Norval Grigg, Arthur Winder, Arthur Wilson — August 27, 1933 (first ascent of the peak)
routes.season: Jun-Sep (climbable most of the year with mountaineering experience, weather, and Cascade River Road access permitting)
routes.bestSeason: Early season (May-June) offers firmer, well-bridged snow over the glacier and fewer open crevasses but a longer, snowier approach; by August the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier opens up with visible crevasses and the upper ridge exposes hard névé and a bergschrund, requiring more careful route-finding and sometimes a talus detour on climber's left. The route can be attempted nearly year-round given stable weather, low avalanche danger, and an open Cascade River Road.
routes.overview: The standard and easiest route on Eldorado Peak: a glacier ascent up the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier to gain a moderate snow ridge, finishing on an airy, corniced, knife-edge summit crest with sweeping views of the Ptarmigan Traverse peaks.
routes.beta: A popular, technically easy climb with a classic finish on an exposed summit ridge. From the base of the East Ridge the route ascends the southern edge of the Inspiration Glacier to about 7,500 ft, then follows the ridge crest — narrowing to a true knife-edge near the top — to the summit. It is considered Class 2 glacier travel and snow climbing, with the crux being exposure and route-finding on the final ridge rather than technical difficulty.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road from Marblemount to the Eldorado parking area at Mile 20 (~2,100 ft; paved to mile 10, then gravel, washboard, and occasional washouts). From the trailhead, cross the Cascade River on logs or by wading, then follow a climbers' trail through forest and a boulder field (cairned) to about 4,000 ft. Continue up an open basin to roughly 5,400 ft (first campsites), traverse near 6,000 ft, then descend a third-class gully into the Roush Creek basin before ascending onto the Eldorado Glacier. Gain the glacier, climb to the ~7,500-7,800 ft ridge crest (common high camp), and continue up the East Ridge to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the East Ridge and glacier route to Roush Creek basin and the boulder field/trail back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the exposed knife-edge ridge back to the ~7,500-7,800 ft glacier crest, then descend the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier reversing the ascent line (watching for crevasses that open through the season). Climb back up and over the ~6,000 ft point into Roush Creek basin via the third-class gully, then descend through the basin, boulder field, and forest trail to the Cascade River crossing and trailhead. Late-season descents through the boulder field and Roush Creek basin can be loose, fast-melting, and require care on wet rock and dirt.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward by reversing the ascent line down the glacier and ridge in good visibility; in whiteout or high wind on the summit ridge, wait it out or downclimb carefully to the broader glacier below rather than risk cornice collapse or a fall off the crest. From high camp, the Roush Creek gully and boulder-field trail provide a walk-off exit in all but the worst weather.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before departing camp based on forecast weather, party pace on the glacier, and daylight remaining for the descent through Roush Creek basin and the boulder field — the exposed summit ridge and glacier crossing are the worst places to be caught by afternoon thunderstorms, whiteout, or softening snow bridges.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Eldorado backcountry; reception is fickle even from high points. A satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, SPOT, etc.) is recommended for all parties. If cell signal is found, North Cascades NP Dispatch can be reached at (360) 873-4500 ext. 37.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit: crampons, mountaineering ice axe, harness, glacier rope, 2-3 pickets for running protection or belays on the steeper ridge sections, crevasse-rescue hardware (prusiks/ascenders, pulley, locking carabiners), and a helmet for the summit ridge and any rockfall-prone sections.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets are the primary protection for the glacier and upper ridge; a rock rack is not needed on the standard line. A short section of fixed rope or hand line is sometimes placed by parties on the steepest névé near the top in low-snow years.
whatToBring: Bivy/camping gear for a high camp near 7,600-7,800 ft
Stove to melt snow for water (limited water sources at camp)
Blue bags for human waste (required, no burying/crevasse disposal)
IGBC-approved bear canister (required June 1-November 15 below vegetation line)
North Cascades NP overnight wilderness permit
proTips: Get an alpine-early start from high camp so the glacier and summit ridge are climbed on firm, refrozen snow before it softens.
Check current NPS climbing conditions blog posts for the Eldorado area before heading in — glacier crevasse and bergschrund status changes fast through the season.
Later in the season, some parties traverse talus on climber's left to avoid exposed ice on the upper glacier.
watchOut: The final summit ridge is a true corniced knife-edge with major exposure on both sides — a fall would be consequential.
Crevasses open on the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier as the season progresses; a rope and crevasse-rescue skills are essential.
Glide cracks and wet-slab avalanche danger on steep rollovers during warm/rainy periods.
knownHazards: Crevasse fall on the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier
Cornice collapse or fall from the exposed summit ridge
Glide-crack avalanches on steep snow slopes low on the route
Loose rock and dirt in the Roush Creek descent gully during late-season melt-out

pitchDetail.notes: Trailhead to boulder field (~4,000 ft) via forest trail and log/wade river crossing
Traverse near 6,000 ft and downclimb a short third-class gully into Roush Creek basin
Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier, roped travel, crevasse navigation to ~7,500-7,800 ft
Narrow, corniced knife-edge finish to the summit

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.seasonalGate: Typically fully open to the road end by late June/early July; can be gated or washed out after storms, especially the final ~10 gravel miles
road.driveNote: Paved to about mile 10, then gravel, washboarded, and narrow with occasional washouts to the Eldorado parking area at mile 20 (~2,100 ft); vehicles over 22 ft/8 ft or trailers prohibited beyond the NP boundary at mile 18

climate.forecastZone: NWS Zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades and Passes); NWAC 'West Slopes North' avalanche forecast zone
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate — Pacific storm fronts bring heavy precipitation, wet heavy snow, and rapid weather changes; conditions on the glacier can shift from clear to whiteout quickly.
climate.spring: Deep, consolidating snowpack; glacier mostly snow-covered with fewer visible crevasses but higher avalanche/glide-crack risk on warm days.
climate.summer: Best climbing window; glacier opens up with visible crevasses and a bergschrund forms near the ridge by August.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows, early-season snow can fall by late September; good firm névé conditions for the NE Face ice route.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall, high avalanche danger, and a much longer approach; Cascade River Road is typically not maintained/plowed to the trailhead.

access.fees: Backcountry/wilderness camping permit: $10 per person plus $6 reservation fee for overnight stays; no fee for day climbs
access.permit: Overnight parties must obtain a permit for the Eldorado Cross-Country Zone from the Wilderness Information Center or recreation.gov
access.closures: Cascade River Road can close seasonally (winter) or after storm washouts; check the NPS road conditions page before driving in

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start (2-4am) from high camp so the glacier and summit ridge are climbed on firm, refrozen snow

waypoints.name: Eldorado Creek / Cascade River Road Trailhead (Mile 20)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 emergency; Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency (360) 428-3211
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Complex Dispatch (360) 873-4500 ext. 37; Marblemount Wilderness Information Center (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111 (Level III ED); PeaceHealth United General, Sedro-Woolley — (360) 856-6021
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage in the backcountry — carry a satellite communicator. Rescue requires helicopter or ground SAR coordinated by NPS and Skagit County SAR; remote glaciated terrain.

routes.fa: Dan Cauthorn and Bill Pilling (year not confirmed by available sources)
routes.season: Oct-Apr (best as a mixed snow/ice climb in cool, freeze-thaw-stable conditions; becomes a snow/névé climb in summer)
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed as an ice/mixed route in late fall through spring when the couloir is filled with consolidated névé or water ice (trip reports document good conditions in early November, late November, and March); midsummer conditions are typically softer snow/névé rather than ice.
routes.face: Northwest Couloir, above the Eldorado Glacier / West-facing cirque between Eldorado and Dean's Tower
routes.rappels: 2 rappels from the notch between Eldorado and Dean's Tower (roughly 33m and a full rope length reported) using fixed piton/sling anchors
routes.overview: An excellent, moderately technical alpine ice/mixed line up an obvious couloir on Eldorado's northwest side, reached via a rappel from near the East Ridge into a west-facing cirque, finishing just below the summit's knife-edge ridge.
routes.beta: From the East Ridge at about 8,100 ft, traverse north (maintaining altitude) to an obvious notch between the North Ridge and the first rock spire near Dean's Tower. Rappel from a fixed station into the west-facing cirque below, then travel south to the base of the couloir. Climb the obvious gully directly — depending on conditions this may be pure snow/névé, ice steps to WI3-4, or short sections of mixed rock — for roughly 3 simul-pitches to gain the ridge just below the summit, then follow the East Ridge crest to the top.
routes.approach: Same trailhead and lower approach as the East Ridge (Cascade River Road Mile 20, boulder field, Roush Creek basin) to the Eldorado Glacier and East Ridge high camp near 7,800 ft; from there, traverse and rappel into the NW cirque as described above rather than continuing up the standard ridge. Total approach to the base of the couloir runs roughly 5-5.5 hours from the trailhead.
routes.descent: Descend via the standard East Ridge/glacier route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, follow the East Ridge crest back down to the glacier and descend the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier and Roush Creek basin as on the standard route back to the trailhead; do not reverse the couloir.
routes.bail: Below the couloir's technical sections, downclimb or rappel back into the west-facing cirque and traverse back toward the East Ridge notch; above the crux, continuing to the ridge and descending via the East Ridge is usually faster and safer than reversing steep ice.
routes.turnaround: Because the route funnels climbers below overhanging ice and rock, set a firm early turnaround for any sign of warming, precipitation, or rockfall/icefall activity in the couloir, and avoid lingering below parties above you.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator. If cell signal is found, North Cascades NP Dispatch can be reached at (360) 873-4500 ext. 37.
routes.detailedRack: A single 60-70m rope (70m avoids an extra rappel), ice tools, crampons, 5-7 ice screws, 2 snow pickets, and a light rock rack (nuts, small-to-medium cams, occasionally knifeblade pitons) for variable mixed conditions.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws for the couloir's steeper/icier steps, pickets for the snow sections above and on the connecting glacier, plus a small rock rack for anchors and any exposed rock moves — exact mix depends heavily on current conditions.
whatToBring: Glacier travel and crevasse rescue kit for the approach
Bivy gear for a high camp
Extra layers — the route is often climbed in colder shoulder-season conditions
proTips: Conditions vary hugely by year and season — check recent trip reports (Cascade Climbers, Rock n' Rope Northwest) before committing, since the couloir can be anywhere from soft névé to fat WI3-4 ice.
A 70m rope avoids a third rappel on the descent into or out of the cirque.
Some parties shortcut the standard approach by rappelling into the couloir's cirque directly rather than descending all the way to Marble Creek.
watchOut: The couloir acts as a funnel for icefall and rockfall from above — leader- or party-triggered debris is the primary objective hazard.
Crevasses on the Eldorado Glacier approach, sometimes well-bridged even in early November.
Post-holing and loose, fast-melting terrain on the boulder-field descent in warm conditions.
knownHazards: Icefall/rockfall funneled through the couloir
Crevasse fall on the glacier approach
Avalanche danger on approach slopes and in the couloir itself after new snow
Whiteout and spindrift exposure high on the route

pitchDetail.notes: Enter couloir with a steeper ice/mixed step off the cirque floor

pitchDetail.grade: Up to AI3-4 (chockstone crux reported), simul-climbing above
pitchDetail.notes: Sustained névé/ice with occasional steeper steps to 70-80° depending on conditions

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.seasonalGate: Typically fully open to the road end by late June/early July; can be gated or washed out after storms
road.driveNote: Paved to about mile 10, then gravel to the Eldorado parking area at mile 20 (~2,100 ft); this route is often climbed in colder months when the road may have limited winter maintenance

climate.forecastZone: NWS Zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades and Passes); NWAC 'West Slopes North' avalanche forecast zone
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy Pacific storm precipitation; the NW-facing couloir holds ice/névé longer than sunnier aspects.
climate.spring: Variable — can still hold good névé/ice into March per trip reports, transitioning to wetter snow later.
climate.summer: Typically softens to a snow/névé climb rather than true ice.
climate.fall: Prime season — cool temperatures build ice/névé in the couloir (documented good conditions in early-to-late November).
climate.winter: Ice/mixed conditions possible but with high avalanche danger and difficult, possibly gated road access.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start; parties commonly climb the couloir in the afternoon/evening after establishing camp, or pre-dawn for a single push

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 emergency; Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency (360) 428-3211

routes.corrections: The given routeName 'Northwest Couloir / Eldorado Glacier' matches sourced route naming (Mountain Project's 'Northwest Ice Couloir'); no elevation/coordinate corrections needed.

routes.season: Best late Sep-mid Oct (early fall, before first snowstorms)
routes.bestSeason: Best in early fall (late September to mid-October) after summer melt-out but before the first significant snowstorms arrive, when the face holds consistent névé/ice; conditions are fickle and can be mostly ice, mostly snow, or mixed depending on recent weather.
routes.overview: A somewhat contrived but enjoyable alternate finish that climbs the steep snow/ice face just climbers'-right of the standard East Ridge, giving climbers with some ice experience a technical variation before rejoining the East Ridge below the summit crest.
routes.beta: Follow the standard East Ridge route from the Eldorado Glacier until able to traverse climbers' right above steep cliffs to gain the base of the Northeast Face. Climb several pitches of snow/ice up to about 50-60°, rejoining the East Ridge route just below the summit ridge, then finish up the exposed knife-edge crest to the top.
routes.approach: Same approach as the East Ridge: Cascade River Road to the Eldorado trailhead at Mile 20, boulder field, Roush Creek basin, and up onto the Eldorado Glacier to a high camp near the base of the East Ridge, from which the Northeast Face is gained by a rightward traverse.
routes.descent: Descend via the East Ridge/glacier route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the East Ridge crest to the glacier and descend the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier and Roush Creek basin back to the trailhead, as on the standard route — do not descend the Northeast Face itself.
routes.bail: Below the crux pitches, downclimb or rappel back toward the East Ridge traverse point and rejoin the standard glacier route; the face is steep enough that retreat is easier low on the route than high.
routes.turnaround: Because the route is a fickle mixed/ice objective on a steep, sun-exposed face, turn around if conditions are found to be thin, wet, or actively shedding snow/ice rather than pushing into deteriorating rock or snow quality.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator. If cell signal is found, North Cascades NP Dispatch can be reached at (360) 873-4500 ext. 37.
gear: Ice axe and ice hammer/second tool
routes.detailedRack: Helmet, ice axe, ice hammer, crampons, a single rope, 5-6 ice screws, and 2 snow pickets; rack composition should flex toward more rock protection if the face is found to be mostly bare/mixed rather than snow/ice.
routes.proNeeds: Ice screws and pickets running protection through the snow/ice pitches; conditions dictate whether more rock gear is warranted for mixed sections.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a high camp on the glacier
Glacier travel and crevasse rescue kit for the approach
Extra layers for an early-fall alpine start
proTips: Best climbed in the narrow early-fall window (late Sept-mid Oct) after summer melt-out but before winter storms.
Conditions are fickle — one documented ascent a week and a half after fresh snowfall found the route mostly snow rather than ice, requiring only a couple of screws and pickets in a running belay.
watchOut: Route character (snow vs. ice vs. mixed rock) can vary significantly with recent weather — assess conditions before committing to the traverse onto the face.
The exposed summit knife-edge ridge at the top is shared with the East Ridge route and carries the same cornice/fall exposure.
knownHazards: Variable/thin ice or snow conditions on a steep, sun-exposed face
Rockfall from the cliffs below the face during the traverse in
Cornice/exposure hazard on the shared summit ridge finish

pitchDetail.notes: Right-trending traverse above steep cliffs from the East Ridge glacier line to the base of the face
Several pitches of moderate snow/ice climbing, character varies by conditions
Rejoins East Ridge route below the top

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.seasonalGate: Typically fully open by late June/early July; can be gated or washed out after storms
road.driveNote: Paved to about mile 10, then gravel to the Eldorado parking area at mile 20 (~2,100 ft)

climate.forecastZone: NWS Zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades and Passes); NWAC 'West Slopes North' avalanche forecast zone
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; a northeast aspect holds snow/ice later into the season than sun-exposed faces.
climate.summer: Face may be mostly bare rock/dry by mid-late summer.
climate.fall: Prime season — early fall freeze-thaw builds consistent névé/ice before winter storms bury the face in unconsolidated snow.
climate.winter: High avalanche danger and difficult access; not a typical winter objective.
