routes.corrections: First-ascent party/year for this specific route could not be sourced from the required primary sources or supplementary research and is left null rather than guessed; do not confuse with the 1933 peak/East Ridge first ascent.

blurb: Little Annapurna (8,485 ft) is a granite peak in the Stuart Range within the Enchantments/Alpine Lakes Wilderness of Chelan County, Washington, immediately southeast of Dragontail Peak. It was named by the Sherpa Climbing Club of Ellensburg (first ascent recorded 1947) for the resemblance of its south face to the Himalayan Annapurna. One of the most photographed and most frequently climbed summits in the Enchantments, it is normally a class 2 walk-up/scramble from the upper basin, though its south face also holds a multi-pitch 5.5-5.6 rock route described in Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide.
hierarchyNote: None — coordinates (47.468226, -120.814335) and elevation (8,485 ft) match Wikipedia/Peakbagger exactly, and the Stuart-Enchantments parent area is correct (Little Annapurna sits in the Stuart Range/Enchantments, Alpine Lakes Wilderness).

routes.overview: A long, scenic approach via Colchuck Lake and Aasgard Pass leads into the upper Enchantment basin, from which a class 2 talus/boulder scramble climbs Little Annapurna's south-facing slopes to one of the most photographed summit viewpoints in the range.
routes.beta: The standard (and by far most common) route to Little Annapurna is a non-technical scramble, not a climb: from the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trailhead, hike to Colchuck Lake, ascend the steep talus of Aasgard Pass into the Core Enchantments, then pick up a well-worn, cairned boot path that switchbacks up the peak's southern/left-hand slopes to the summit plateau. Snow can linger on the approach and summit slopes into July, in which case an ice axe (and sometimes microspikes) is recommended; otherwise no rope or technical gear is needed. A separate, rarely-climbed South Face rock route (5.5-5.6, multiple pitches, described in Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide) climbs the peak's namesake face directly and is a distinct line from this scramble route.
objHaz: exposure near Nightmare/Knitting Needles on the south edge
routes.face: South Slopes (standard scramble route, distinct from the technical South Face rock route)
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive US-2 to Icicle Road (FR 76) and follow it about 8.5 miles, then turn left onto Eightmile Road (FR 7600); stay right onto FR 7601 and continue about 4 miles to the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trailhead (elev. ~3,400 ft; day-use requires a Northwest Forest Pass, overnight requires an Enchantment Permit Area reservation). Hike the Lake Stuart Trail 2.5 miles to a fork, go left onto the Colchuck Lake Trail for another 1.8 miles to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft). Follow the trail around the lake's south/west shore into a talus field, then ascend the steep, loose boulder drainage between Dragontail Peak and Little Annapurna to Aasgard Pass (7,841 ft), gaining roughly 2,000 ft in under a mile. From the pass, descend slightly into the grey alpine Upper Enchantments basin, pass small tarns and meadows, and pick up an unsigned but well-cairned climbers' path branching right/uphill toward Little Annapurna's southern slopes, switchbacking to the broad summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent scramble down to the Upper Enchantments basin, then retrace the route back over Aasgard Pass and down to Colchuck Lake and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the same cairned boot path down the mountain's southern/left-hand slopes back into the Upper Enchantment basin. Regain Aasgard Pass and descend its steep, loose talus and boulder field carefully — this is the most hazard-prone section of the whole outing, especially with fatigue or in the wet — back down to Colchuck Lake, then follow the Colchuck Lake and Lake Stuart trails back to the trailhead. Some parties with ski or mountaineering experience descend the peak's snow-covered north-facing slopes toward Upper Enchantment Lakes in spring corn-snow conditions, but the standard descent for hikers/scramblers is to reverse the ascent route.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-day itinerary: Day 1 — hike from the Stuart Lake Trailhead to Colchuck Lake (~4.3 mi) and continue over Aasgard Pass into the Enchantments to make camp near the upper lakes (permit-dependent zone); Day 2 — day-hike/scramble Little Annapurna from camp, then descend back over Aasgard Pass and out to the trailhead. Strong parties do the whole loop, including the summit, as a long single-day push (~15-16 miles round trip, 5,000-5,800 ft gain).
routes.bail: This is a non-technical walk-up scramble with no fixed rappel stations — retreat at any point simply by reversing the cairned path down to the Upper Enchantments basin. The only sustained hazardous section to downclimb is Aasgard Pass itself; if conditions deteriorate (thunderstorm, whiteout, exhaustion) it is safer to shelter in the basin and wait it out than to descend the pass's loose talus in poor visibility.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that gets you off the exposed upper slopes and back over Aasgard Pass before afternoon thunderstorms typical of Cascades summers develop, and before failing light makes the loose talus on Aasgard Pass more hazardous to descend.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through October is the typical window, dependent on snowpack; the Enchantment basin and Aasgard Pass often hold snow into July in high-snow years, in which case an ice axe (and sometimes traction devices) is advisable. Early autumn (late Sept-early Oct) brings larch colour and is a popular period, but nights get cold and early-season storms can bring snow.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through most of the Colchuck Lake basin, Aasgard Pass, and the Enchantments; do not rely on a phone for emergency communication. Some isolated high points (e.g., nearby Dragontail's summit) have reported sporadic Verizon signal, but this is not consistent. A satellite communicator/messenger is recommended for this trip.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack is needed for the standard South Slopes scramble — it is a hiking/scrambling route on trail and talus. In early season or when snow lingers on the approach or summit slopes, carry an ice axe and consider microspikes/traction devices; a helmet is a reasonable precaution given loose rock and rockfall potential on Aasgard Pass and the summit talus.
routes.proNeeds: None — this is an unroped scramble route with no fixed or removable protection used.
whatToBring: Enchantment Permit Area reservation (overnight) or day permit
plenty of water/purification (limited reliable water on the upper slopes)
layers for wind/weather exposure on the summit plateau
bear canister if camping in the Enchantments
proTips: Most parties combine Little Annapurna with a broader Enchantments trip (backpack in over Aasgard Pass, day-scramble the peak from camp) rather than a single monster day, given the 15-16 mile round trip and 5,000+ ft of gain.
The summit plateau is one of the best panoramic viewpoints in the Enchantments — bring a camera for Dragontail, Prusik Peak, the Enchantment Lakes basin, and on clear days Mt. Rainier and Mt. Stuart.
Snow can linger on the ascent gully and summit slopes well into July; check recent trip reports before assuming a bare-ground scramble.
watchOut: Aasgard Pass is steep, loose talus/scree in both directions and is the most injury-prone section of the trip, especially descending tired or in the wet.
Cairns on the upper mountain can be obscured or relocated by rockfall/shifting talus — practice careful route-finding rather than blind cairn-following.
The south edge of the peak drops off toward the technical Nightmare and Knitting Needles formations; stay well back from unmarked edges.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in Cascades summer — the exposed upper mountain and Aasgard Pass are poor places to be caught in one.
knownHazards: Loose, shifting talus and scree on Aasgard Pass and the upper mountain create significant rockfall and ankle-injury risk.
Lingering snow and ice in the approach gully and on summit slopes into early summer can require an ice axe and produce slip-fall hazard.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common Cascades summer hazards on this exposed, high-alpine route.
Cell phone coverage is unreliable through most of the route, complicating emergency communication.

road.name: Forest Road 7601 (via Icicle Road/FR 76 and Eightmile Road/FR 7600)
road.status: Open seasonally; steep with washboarded, worsening surface through the season — high-clearance recommended, 4x4 advisable
road.seasonalGate: Closed to vehicles every winter to protect the roadbed and prevent hazardous travel; typically reopens mid-to-late April depending on snowpack
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth take Icicle Road (FR 76) ~8.5 miles, turn left on Eightmile Road (FR 7600), stay right onto FR 7601, and continue ~4 miles to the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trailhead. Parking is extremely limited at this heavily-trafficked trailhead; arrive early or arrange a drop-off.

climate.forecastZone: East Slopes Central Cascades / Alpine Lakes Wilderness (NWS Spokane/Pendleton mountain zones; NWAC does not forecast this zone — check mountain-forecast.com for point forecasts)
climate.typical: Dry, warm summers with cool nights typical of the east-slope Cascades rain shadow; afternoon thunderstorm risk in summer; snow possible any month above treeline.
climate.spring: Snow typically covers the approach and upper basin into June; access road (FR 7601) usually opens mid-to-late April.
climate.summer: Warm, mostly dry days (often 70s-80s F at the trailhead), cool alpine nights, and a real risk of afternoon thunderstorms building over the peaks.
climate.fall: Crisp, popular larch-viewing season (late Sept-early Oct) with cold nights and increasing chance of early-season snow.
climate.winter: Access road closed; deep snowpack makes this a ski-mountaineering/winter travel objective only for experienced parties.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Leavenworth Ranger District), Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass ($5/day or annual) for day-use parking at the trailhead; Enchantment Permit Area overnight permit fee ($6 non-refundable lottery application + $5/person/day if awarded) for overnight camping
access.permit: Enchantment Permit Area reservation required for overnight camping May 15-Oct 31, allocated via an advance lottery (applications open mid-February) and a limited daily walk-up lottery; day hiking/climbing does not require an overnight permit but does require a free self-issued wilderness day permit at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (day use) or Enchantment Permit Area reservation (overnight)
access.closures: FR 7601 access road closed seasonally each winter; wilderness travel restrictions apply within the Enchantment Permit core zone (group-size and camping-location rules)

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/early morning start recommended given the long approach and to be off Aasgard Pass and the exposed upper mountain before afternoon thunderstorms

emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable through most of the Colchuck Lake/Aasgard Pass/Enchantments corridor; carry a satellite communicator. The Enchantments see frequent SAR callouts, particularly on Aasgard Pass and among under-prepared day hikers.

routes.corrections: None to the given peak data — elevation (8,485 ft) and coordinates (47.468226, -120.814335) are corroborated by Wikipedia and match the DMS coordinates (47°28'06"N 120°48'52"W) cited there. Note some secondary sources (WTA, a few trip reports) round the summit to 8,440 ft; 8,485 ft (Peakbagger/Wikipedia/USGS-derived) is treated as authoritative here per the given data.

blurb: Mount Anderson rises near the hydrographic center of Olympic National Park in Washington's Olympic Mountains (Jefferson County); its glaciated massif is the source of the Dosewallips, Quinault, and (via the Hayes River) Strait-of-Juan-de-Fuca watersheds. The range's rock is obducted Eocene sandstone, turbidite, and basaltic oceanic crust uplifted along the Cascadia subduction zone, and Anderson still carries three named glaciers (Anderson, Eel, and Hanging). The peak (East Peak, the true/named summit) was first climbed in 1920 by Fairman B. Lee's party of settlers approaching via Hayden Pass; the taller, non-technical West Peak (7,365 ft) is a separate sub-summit of the same massif.
hierarchyNote: Coordinates and elevation for Mount Anderson (47.721, -123.332, ~7,321 ft) check out against Wikipedia/USGS-derived data and multiple independent climbing sources -- this is genuinely the well-known Mount Anderson in the central Olympic Mountains, Olympic National Park. However, the given parent area "North-Central Olympic Mountains (Deception-Gray Wolf)" looks geographically questionable: Anderson sits near the hydrographic center of the range, at the headwaters of the Dosewallips/Quinault/Duckabush-adjacent drainages close to Anderson Pass, roughly 13-15 miles south of Mount Deception and the Gray Wolf River area (a distinct northeastern subrange in the Buckhorn Wilderness), separated by substantial intervening terrain (Mt. Constance, Warrior Peak, etc.). Recommend double-checking whether Anderson belongs in a "Central Olympics / Anderson-Dosewallips-Quinault" bucket rather than grouped with Deception-Gray Wolf.

objHaz: glacier crevasses on the Eel Glacier
bergschrund/moat at the head of the glacier below Flypaper Pass
steep snow finger (estimated 40°+ in places, per recent reports) below Flypaper Pass
wet-snow avalanche potential on approach slopes
unbridged/hazardous ford of the West Fork Dosewallips at Honeymoon Meadows in spring or high water
routes.season: Jun-Aug (best in spring/early summer before the Flypaper Pass snow finger melts out)
routes.face: Eel Glacier (northwest cirque) and Flypaper Pass, the notch between Mt. Anderson and Echo Rock
routes.overview: A glacier-and-scramble line up Mount Anderson's northwest side: from Anderson Pass climbers cross into the Anderson Glacier basin, climb a steepening snow finger to 6,500-ft Flypaper Pass, then drop onto and cross the upper Eel Glacier before finishing on snow and short 3rd-class rock to the summit.
routes.beta: Rated a Grade I, class 3 basic glacier climb, this is the standard route on Mount Anderson's East Peak (the true, named summit) and the line used for Mountaineers-club basic glacier courses. It's usually done as a 3-day trip from the Dosewallips trailhead (trailhead-to-camp 7-9 hrs, camp-to-summit round trip 5-7 hrs), or compressed to 2 days by camping on the Anderson Glacier moraine. Glacier travel gear (rope, crampons, axe, crevasse-rescue kit) is required for the Eel Glacier crossing even though the terrain is otherwise moderate.
routes.approach: From US-101 at Brinnon, turn onto Dosewallips Road (milepost 306). The road has been washed out roughly a mile in since 2002 flood damage and is closed to vehicles indefinitely (foot/bike only beyond the washout -- a mountain bike is commonly used to speed this section). Continue on the old roadbed and then the Dosewallips/West Fork Dosewallips River Trail past Dose Forks, Diamond Meadows, and Honeymoon Meadows camps (the West Fork ford at Honeymoon Meadows can be hazardous in spring/high water) to Anderson Pass (4,464 ft, roughly 16 trail miles from the road-end washout). From the pass, leave the maintained trail and climb north on talus/moraine into the Anderson Glacier basin to Anderson Tarn (~5,100 ft), a common high camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down the upper Eel Glacier, back over Flypaper Pass, and down the snow finger/talus into the Anderson Glacier basin to camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the ascent line north across the summit snowfields to the saddle, then back onto the upper Eel Glacier, roped for crevasses. Recross the glacier to the base of Flypaper Pass and descend the steep snow/scree finger (which softens through the day and can be icy in early morning or crusty/loose by afternoon) back into the Anderson Glacier cirque. Some parties instead descend via Echo Col to reach snowfields west of the upper Anderson Glacier before rejoining the standard line back to camp, then follow the approach trail back out.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Bike/hike in from the Dosewallips Road washout past Dose Forks and Diamond Meadows to Honeymoon Meadows camp. Day 2: Continue over Anderson Pass to a high camp near Anderson Tarn/the Anderson Glacier moraine (~5,100 ft). Day 3: Climb via the Anderson Glacier, Flypaper Pass, and Eel Glacier to the summit and return to high camp. Days 4-5: Hike/bike back out to the trailhead. (Strong parties compress this to 2 days in by camping directly on the Anderson Glacier moraine.)
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent from anywhere below the summit ridge -- down the Eel Glacier to Flypaper Pass and down the snow finger to the Anderson Glacier basin. No technical/roped rappelling is required on this route in normal conditions, but crevasse hazard and poor-visibility navigation on the glacier call for a rope team and reliable map/GPS.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach alone is a multi-day undertaking, base the turnaround on conditions rather than a fixed clock time: the Flypaper Pass snow finger steepens and softens through the day, so it is safest climbed and descended while still firm; retreat if afternoon cloud, rain, or wet-snow avalanche activity develops on the pass or the upper glacier.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in spring through mid-summer (roughly June-August) while the snow finger below Flypaper Pass is still intact -- it is the key link between the Anderson Glacier basin and the pass, and it can melt out and steepen significantly later in the season; glacier recession has been reported to have pushed this slope to an estimated 40 degrees or more in recent years.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit for a rope of 2-3: one rope per rope team, plus a small crevasse-rescue rack (2-3 pickets or ice screws, prusiks/mechanical ascenders, a pulley, and cordage/slings). No rock rack is needed -- the summit rock is unroped 3rd-class scrambling.
routes.proNeeds: Crevasse-rescue protection (pickets/screws) for the Eel Glacier crossing rather than rock protection; the class 3 rock near the summit is climbed unroped.
gear: bear canister (required in the West Fork Dosewallips corridor)
whatToBring: mountain bike (to cover the washed-out lower road)
map/compass or GPS (glacier and whiteout navigation)
proTips: Bring a mountain bike for the washed-out lower Dosewallips Road -- it saves significant time and energy on the closed roadbed section.
Climb in spring/early summer while the Flypaper Pass snow finger is still intact and before it steepens with melt-out.
Camping on the Anderson Glacier moraine compresses the trip to 2 days in instead of the standard 3.
Start the glacier crossing early for firmer snow on the Flypaper Pass finger for both ascent and descent.
watchOut: The snow/scree finger below Flypaper Pass has steepened with glacier recession and has been measured at an estimated 40+ degrees in recent trip reports.
A bergschrund/moat where the pass meets the Eel Glacier can be difficult or impossible to cross by late summer.
Crevasses on the Eel Glacier's east side require route-finding and rope protection.
The West Fork Dosewallips ford at Honeymoon Meadows can be hazardous in spring or after heavy rain.
The permanent Dosewallips Road washout adds substantial mileage and requires biking or walking a closed roadbed.
knownHazards: Glacier travel with crevasse-fall potential on the Eel Glacier.
Steep, recession-exposed snow finger below Flypaper Pass (estimated 40°+ in places).
Wet, heavy Olympic Mountains snowpack that can produce avalanches across a range of conditions.
Unbridged river fords on the approach that are hazardous during high water.

road.name: Dosewallips Road
road.status: Permanently washed out roughly 1 mile from US-101; closed to vehicles indefinitely since 2002 flood damage
road.driveNote: From US-101 at Brinnon (milepost 306), turn onto Dosewallips Road and drive to the washout; continue on foot or by bike to the historic road-end trailhead and ranger station.

climate.typical: Wet Pacific-maritime climate typical of the Olympic Mountains, with heavy annual precipitation and a snowpack that tends to run wetter/denser than in the Cascades.
climate.summer: The primary climbing window; the snow finger to Flypaper Pass is most reliable earlier in this period.
climate.winter: Heavy snow plus the permanent road washout make winter approach and climbing impractical for this route.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (7,323 ft) differs slightly from the summit elevation most consistently cited across independent sources for the East Peak/true summit of Mount Anderson (7,321 ft, per multiple trip reports/guides); Wikipedia's rounded conversion of 2,234 m yields ~7,330 ft. The taller West Peak (7,365 ft) is a separate, non-technical sub-summit and is not the named 'Mount Anderson' summit -- do not conflate the two elevations. Also: the mountain's documented 1920 first ascent (Fairman B. Lee's party, via Hayden Pass) is the PEAK's first ascent on a different line, not a first ascent of this Eel Glacier/Flypaper Pass route -- no source documents who first climbed this specific line, so route 'fa' is left null rather than reusing the peak FA.

blurb: Mount Carrie (6,995 ft) is the highest summit in the Bailey Range, a remote, glaciated subrange in the heart of Olympic National Park's Daniel J. Evans Wilderness, Clallam County, Washington. Built of grey Eocene sandstone and carrying the Carrie Glacier (NE cirque) and Fairchild Glacier (E slope), it was named in 1899 by surveyor Theodore Rixon for his wife Caroline "Carrie" Rixon; it also serves as the opening summit of the classic multi-day Bailey Range Traverse.
hierarchyNote: Mount Carrie's name, coordinates (47.894, -123.649 matches Wikipedia/USGS-derived 47°53'39"N 123°38'57"W), elevation (6,995 ft), and parent area (it genuinely is the highest point of the Bailey Range) all check out — no data-integrity problem with this row. However, flagging for the requester: this is an Olympic Mountains peak, not a North Cascades/Cascades peak as the task framing described. If this batch is meant to be Cascades-only, this peak may be out of scope; if the batch is WA-statewide, it's fine as-is.

routes.routeName: Southeast Route via Hurricane Ridge / Cat Basin
routes.overview: A committing glacier line up Mount Carrie's southeast side, almost always climbed as the opening summit of a multi-day Bailey Range or Olympus-Bailey traverse rather than as a stand-alone day route: it gains a saddle above Carrie Glacier, rappels an icefall, crosses the upper glacier, and finishes with a short rocky scramble to the summit.
routes.beta: Reached cross-country through the Bailey Range rather than by maintained trail. Unlike the class 2-3 scrambling of the standard southwest-ridge/Catwalk route, this line involves real glacier travel on Carrie Glacier, including a roped rappel down an icefall, before a short scramble up the rocky southeast ridge to the summit.
routes.approach: Parties reach the mountain's east/southeast side cross-country through the Bailey Range — either continuing west along the Bailey Range crest from an Elwha-side approach (Whiskey Bend/Madison Falls) toward Stephen Lake, or linking in via the Sol Duc/High Divide trail to Cat Basin and the Catwalk from the west. From a high camp (e.g. Stephen Lake, ~4,998'), the route drops into Sickle Canyon and climbs to a roughly 6,600' snow saddle overlooking Carrie Glacier on the peak's southeast side.
routes.descent: Most parties continue over the summit and descend Carrie's southwest ridge (the standard route's line) rather than reversing the icefall rappel.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the southwest ridge (class 1-2) past Boston Charlie's camp toward the scree/snow basin and southwest ridge crest — the same terrain used by the standard High Divide route — instead of re-climbing and re-rappelling the southeast icefall.
routes.itinerary: Typically one day within a multi-day Bailey Range or Olympus-Bailey traverse rather than a stand-alone push. One documented ascent gained the summit in 6.6 hours from a camp at Stephen Lake (4,998'), for roughly 2,650 ft of gain on summit day.
routes.bail: Retreating back down the ascent line is complicated by the icefall rappel; parties in trouble more commonly continue over the summit and descend the easier southwest ridge rather than reversing the southeast glacier route.
routes.turnaround: Given the remote, multi-day nature of this line and the roped glacier travel involved, set a firm turnaround time that leaves ample daylight to complete the icefall rappel and glacier crossing before dark, and do not linger on the glacier in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July through August, once the icefall and glacier slopes have consolidated enough for safer travel; earlier in the season the icefall and crevasses are more open and hazardous, and by late September conditions become unpredictable.
objHaz: Crevasses on Carrie Glacier's upper slopes
Icefall near the ~6,600' saddle requiring a roped rappel
Off-trail, exposed scrambling on the southeast ridge
Steep snow travel in Sickle Canyon
knownHazards: Crevasse fall hazard while crossing Carrie Glacier's upper slopes.
Icefall below the 6,600' saddle requires a roped, picket-anchored rappel.
Route-finding through off-trail terrain (Sickle Canyon, the saddle) is difficult in poor visibility.
watchOut: This is genuine glacier terrain, not a scramble — crevasses and an icefall require roped travel and rappel gear.
Most ascents happen mid-traverse through the Bailey Range with a loaded pack, adding fatigue to the technical sections.
proTips: This route is far more committing than Carrie's standard southwest ridge — nearly everyone who climbs it is already mid-traverse through the Bailey Range, not treating it as a stand-alone objective.
Go mid-to-late season (mid-July onward) so the icefall and glacier are consolidated enough to travel safely.

routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit: rope, harness, crevasse-rescue rack, and snow pickets (used as the icefall rappel anchor), plus ice axe and crampons.
routes.proNeeds: Snow pickets for the icefall rappel anchor; full crevasse-rescue rack for glacier travel.

climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy precipitation, especially in winter
climate.spring: Deep snowpack and open crevasses make this line more hazardous
climate.summer: Best window, July-September, once the icefall/glacier consolidate
climate.fall: Shortening days and increasing storms by late September

routes.corrections: The DB route name pairs 'Hurricane Ridge' with 'Cat Basin,' but sourced trip reports show the eastern approach to this side of Carrie runs through the Elwha valley (Whiskey Bend/Madison Falls trailhead) and Stephen Lake, not literally up the Hurricane Ridge Road corridor — Hurricane Ridge is the well-known viewpoint from which Carrie's glaciated east side is visible, not the trailhead itself. Approach/timing/gain figures above are drawn from a single well-documented multi-day traverse trip report (not a dedicated stand-alone climbers'-guide entry for this line), so treat them as illustrative rather than definitive for all parties.

routes.overview: The standard, non-technical way up Mount Carrie: a long trail approach up the Sol Duc valley to Heart Lake and the High Divide, a cross-country stretch to Cat Basin, an exposed but non-technical scramble across 'the Catwalk' ridge, then the southwest ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: From the Sol Duc River road-end trailhead (2,000'), follow trail up through Sol Duc Park and Heart Lake to the High Divide crest, then continue along the High Divide to where the maintained trail ends near Cat Basin. Cross the exposed, narrow 'Catwalk' ridge connecting Cat Peak to the Bailey Range, then climb Mount Carrie's southwest ridge (class 1-2, with a scree/snow traverse near the true summit) to the 6,995' summit. Rated class 3 on the Catwalk, class 2 or easier elsewhere.
routes.approach: Drive US-101 west from Lake Crescent about 2 miles to Sol Duc Hot Springs Road; follow it roughly 14 miles to the road-end trailhead (2,000'). Hike the Sol Duc River/High Divide trail about 7 miles to Heart Lake (4,800'), then 1.5 miles to the 5,100' High Divide crest, then south along the High Divide about 3 miles to where the official trail ends near Cat Basin (a designated, bear-canister-required campsite).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: back across the Catwalk and out the High Divide/Sol Duc trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the southwest ridge back to Boston Charlie's camp, re-cross the exposed Catwalk to Cat Basin, then follow the High Divide trail back over the crest to Heart Lake and out the Sol Duc River trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties backpack in and camp at Heart Lake or Cat Basin, then day-climb the summit and return; the round trip is doable in a single very long day (~31 miles, ~9,800' gain) but is far more commonly split over 2-3 days. One trip report logged 8.2 hours from the trailhead to a Cat Basin camp on day one.
routes.bail: Straightforward retreat along the same trail/ridge line at any point before the Catwalk; once on the Catwalk, the safest bail is to reverse the crossing rather than attempt an alternate descent off the ridge.
routes.turnaround: Given the long trail approach and the exposed Catwalk crossing, set a turnaround time that guarantees recrossing the Catwalk and regaining the High Divide trail well before dark or the onset of afternoon thunderstorms/fog.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July through mid-September. Snow lingers on the High Divide and the Catwalk into early July; going earlier means postholing and a snow-covered, harder-to-follow Catwalk, while by mid-September the summit scree/snow basin and Catwalk are typically in their best, most stable shape.
routes.comms: Some parties have reported unreliable cell coverage at High Divide, likely line-of-sight to a tower across the valley near Hurricane Ridge; this should not be relied on for emergencies.
objHaz: Exposed class 3 scrambling on the Catwalk
Loose rock/scree on the upper southwest ridge
Lingering snow/ice near the summit into mid-summer
knownHazards: Exposed, low class 3 scrambling across the Catwalk between Cat Peak and Mount Carrie.
Loose rock and scree on the upper southwest ridge.
Lingering snow/ice in the summit basin into mid-summer, sometimes requiring an ice axe.
watchOut: The Catwalk is the route's crux: a narrow, exposed ridge crest with low class 3 moves and real fall consequences.
Trail can be snow-covered into early July, obscuring the unmaintained stretch past the end of the High Divide trail.
This is a very long day if done car-to-car (31 mi round trip, ~9,800' gain) — most parties backpack in rather than day-hike it.
proTips: Time the trip for mid-July through mid-September so the Catwalk is snow-free and the summit scree/snow basin only needs an ice axe, not crampons.
Cat Basin is a designated, bear-canister-required campsite and the natural base camp for the Catwalk and summit push.
Carry an ice axe even in summer for the snow patch in the summit basin.

routes.proNeeds: None — this is a non-technical scramble. Some parties carry a short rope for confidence on the Catwalk's exposure, but it is not standard practice.
whatToBring: overnight backpacking gear (most parties camp at Heart Lake or Cat Basin)
map/compass/GPS for the unmaintained stretch past the High Divide trail's end

climate.summer: Driest, most stable window, mid-July through September

routes.corrections: The DB route name pairs 'Carrie Glacier' with the High Divide approach, but sourced descriptions (SummitPost, multiple trip reports) show this southwest-ridge/Catwalk line only crosses scree and a snow patch in the summit basin near the top — the true Carrie Glacier occupies the peak's separate northeast cirque and is not crossed by this route (it is crossed by the southeast route instead). Distance/gain figures (31 mi RT / 9,800 ft) are for the full car-to-car climb of Carrie via this trail; a separate report cites 14 mi/7,200 ft for a shorter variant, likely reflecting a different start point or partial trip — treat the 31 mi/9,800 ft figure as the standard full ascent number.

blurb: Mount Baker (10,781 ft / 3,286 m) is a heavily glaciated andesite stratovolcano in the North Cascades of Whatcom County, Washington, roughly 31 miles east of Bellingham — Washington's third-highest peak and one of the most heavily glaciated volcanoes in the Cascade Range, with a persistently active fumarole field in Sherman Crater near the summit. It was first climbed August 17, 1868 by Edmund Coleman, John Tennant, Thomas Stratton, and David Ogilvy via what is now the Coleman Glacier route, after two earlier attempts by Coleman in 1866.

routes.fa: Fred Murley, Dave Musser, Klindt Vielbig, 1961
objHaz: Loose rock on the ridge, worse late season
routes.season: Spring-early summer, before rock loosens and ice bridges deteriorate
routes.face: Northeast side, between the Coleman/Roosevelt icefall and Park Glacier
routes.overview: A moderate mixed ridge climb up Mt. Baker's northeast side, threading between loose rock towers and the Coleman-Roosevelt icefall before joining the upper mountain near the Park Glacier headwall.
routes.beta: From Heliotrope Ridge, cross the Coleman and Roosevelt Glaciers and climb moderate snow to gain the ridge around 7,500 ft. The route bypasses a rock pinnacle on its right, then works left/east on low-angle ramps through the icefall to avoid loose towers and bergschrunds, passing the Cockscomb feature on its left via steep snow before rejoining upper-mountain terrain.
routes.approach: Start at the Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead (end of Glacier Creek Rd/FSR 39, 8 mi from Mt. Baker Hwy 542). Hike Heliotrope Ridge Trail roughly 2.25 mi to the Coleman Glacier fork, rope up, and cross the Coleman and Roosevelt Glaciers, gaining moderate snow slopes to reach the ridge crest near 7,500 ft (2-3 hrs from the glacier).
routes.descent: Coleman-Deming Glacier route
routes.descentText: Descend via the standard Coleman-Deming line: down the Roman Wall to the Colfax-Baker saddle, then down easy snow past the Black Buttes (move quickly through the serac zone) back to Hogsback camp and the Heliotrope Ridge Trail.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike to high camp (roughly 6,000-7,000 ft) below Heliotrope Ridge. Day 2: Alpine start, cross the Coleman/Roosevelt Glaciers, climb the ridge to the summit, and descend via Coleman-Deming.
routes.bail: Retreat onto the Coleman Glacier below the ridge is straightforward in most conditions; once committed above the icefall, continuing to the upper mountain and descending via Coleman-Deming is usually safer than reversing the loose terrain.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting given the loose rock and icefall exposure — deteriorating snow bridges or afternoon rockfall are reasons to bail, not just the clock.
routes.bestSeason: Best in spring to early summer while snow still consolidates the loose rock and bridges crevasses in the icefall; sources note the route becomes 'insanely loose and sketchy' by late summer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage above Kulshan Cabin/Hogsback camp; treat the upper mountain as out of range.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack: a few ice screws, 2-4 snow pickets, glacier rope(s), and standard crevasse-rescue kit; an extra picket or two for the icefall ramps.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection — mostly snow/ice anchors (pickets, screws) for glacier crossings and short steeper steps; not a rock-gear route.
proTips: Time the climb for spring/early summer when the icefall is still snow-bridged and rock is frozen together.
Route-finding through the icefall is the crux — be ready to reroute around opening crevasses.
Descending Coleman-Deming after topping out is generally easier than reversing the ridge.
watchOut: Loose rock towers along the ridge, worse late season
Bergschrund crossings within the icefall
Rockfall onto the approach slopes
knownHazards: Coleman/Roosevelt icefall crevasse hazard
Loose rock on the ridge and pinnacle bypass
Serac exposure similar to neighboring Black Buttes terrain

pitchDetail.notes: within the icefall, traverse left/east
loose towers, bypass on ramps
pass the pinnacle feature on the left

road.name: Glacier Creek Road (FSR 39)
road.seasonalGate: Generally open summer only; subject to washout closures
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA take Mt. Baker Hwy 542 east about 1 mi to Glacier Creek Rd (FSR 39), then 8 mi to the trailhead

objHaz: Weather/whiteout exposure on the broad summit plateau
Volcanic gas near Sherman Crater fumaroles
routes.face: South side, via the Railroad Grade moraine ridge and Easton Glacier
routes.overview: The standard south-side glacier route up Baker, following the Railroad Grade moraine ridge onto the Easton Glacier to the summit plateau; widely regarded as the mountain's easiest line.
routes.beta: From Schriebers Meadow, follow the trail west, cross the outlet creek near 3,600 ft, then climb north along the Railroad Grade lateral moraine above treeline. Around 8,000-9,000 ft, cut onto the Easton Glacier and ascend its moderate slopes, weaving around crevasses, to the summit plateau.
routes.approach: Park at the Park Butte/Schriebers Meadow trailhead (end of FSR 13, off Baker Lake Rd). Hike through Schriebers Meadow, crossing the creek at about 3,600 ft, then follow the Railroad Grade moraine crest above treeline to roughly 8,000-9,000 ft where the route breaks onto the glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent down the Easton Glacier and Railroad Grade
routes.descentText: Descend the same glacier slopes back to the Railroad Grade ridge, staying alert for softening crevasse bridges in the afternoon, then follow the moraine and trail back to Schriebers Meadow.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike to high camp (roughly 6,000 ft) on the Railroad Grade. Day 2: Alpine start, climb the Easton Glacier to the summit, descend same day.
routes.bail: Retreat down the Railroad Grade ridge is straightforward from almost any point on the glacier in good visibility.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before softening snow bridges and afternoon whiteout risk on the broad glacier make navigation difficult.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September gives the most consolidated crevasse bridges and stable weather; a Recreation Pass is required at the trailhead year-round.
routes.comms: Cell service is generally unavailable once above Schriebers Meadow; carry a PLB/satellite device.
routes.detailedRack: Standard 2-person glacier team kit: rope, 2-4 pickets, crevasse rescue kit, harness, ice axe, crampons; technical hardware is rarely needed.
routes.proNeeds: No rock protection needed; anchors are snow pickets/flukes for crevasse rescue and belayed crossings.
whatToBring: Layered clothing for wind on the plateau
proTips: Start early — the broad glacier gets confusing in flat light or afternoon cloud.
Watch for the Sherman Crater fumarole smell/steam near the summit plateau.
This and Coleman-Deming are the two standard routes; Easton has a longer but gentler approach.
watchOut: Crevasses opening later in the season
Afternoon whiteout on the broad summit plateau
Volcanic gas near Sherman Crater
knownHazards: Glacier crevasse fall
Weather-related routefinding difficulty on the plateau
Sherman Crater fumarole gases

routes.fa: Edmund Coleman, John Tennant, Thomas Stratton, David Ogilvy — August 17, 1868 (first ascent of Mt. Baker, via this line)
objHaz: Serac avalanche off Colfax Peak/Black Buttes
Crevasses on the Coleman and Deming Glaciers
Exposed, convex Roman Wall slope near 10,000 ft
routes.season: May-Aug; spring best before crevasses fully open
routes.face: North side, via Heliotrope Ridge and the Coleman/Deming Glaciers
routes.overview: Mt. Baker's original 1868 first-ascent line and its most popular route: a straightforward but glaciated climb up the Coleman and Deming Glaciers on the mountain's north side.
routes.beta: From Hogsback camp (about 6,000 ft), climb easy snow with Heliotrope Ridge on climber's right, traversing carefully beneath the Black Buttes/Colfax Peak serac band. Continue to the Colfax-Baker saddle (about 9,000 ft), then ascend the convex Roman Wall slope near 10,000 ft to the summit plateau; the true summit is a short walk east across a saddle.
routes.approach: From the Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead, hike about 3 mi through old-growth forest and moraine to Hogsback camp (about 6,000 ft) below the Coleman Glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent down the Roman Wall and Coleman-Deming Glaciers
routes.descentText: Down-climb the Roman Wall, cross back over the Colfax-Baker saddle, and descend past the Black Buttes serac zone (move quickly through this section) back to Hogsback camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to Hogsback camp. Day 2: Alpine start, climb via Coleman-Deming to the summit, descend the same day.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same glacier is possible from nearly any point below the Roman Wall in good visibility; cross the serac zone below the Black Buttes quickly in both directions rather than lingering.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before the Black Buttes serac band and Roman Wall are exposed to warming afternoon temperatures.
routes.bestSeason: Spring through mid-summer offers the most consolidated crevasse bridges; a classic training climb for Rainier and beginner glacier travel.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service above Hogsback camp.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier rope(s), harness, 2-3 snow pickets per rope team, crevasse rescue kit, ice axe, crampons.
routes.proNeeds: Snow/ice anchors only (pickets, occasionally a fluke); no rock gear needed.
whatToBring: Extra insulating layer for the saddle wind
proTips: Move efficiently under the Black Buttes — serac fall here is a real and recurring hazard.
Early-season trips avoid the worst crevasse exposure on the upper glacier.
A great warm-up/training climb for the bigger Cascade volcanoes.
watchOut: Serac avalanches from Colfax Peak/Black Buttes
Crevasse opening on the upper Coleman/Deming Glaciers
Convex, exposed Roman Wall slope near 10,000 ft
knownHazards: Recurring serac avalanche activity below the Black Buttes
Crevasse fall on both glaciers
Whiteout on the broad summit plateau

routes.fa: Joe Morovits and party, July 2, 1894
routes.season: Early season, before mid-summer rockfall increases
routes.face: East side, the cleaver dividing the Boulder and Park Glaciers
routes.overview: A remote east-side line up the rock-and-snow cleaver separating the Boulder and Park Glaciers, reaching the summit via a choice of a snow traverse toward Sherman Crater or a direct rock/snow finish near Grant Peak.
routes.beta: From a high bivy (about 5,500 ft) below the Boulder Glacier, cross the lower glacier northwest to the toe of the cleaver and follow it north (crevasse-free lower down). At about 9,300 ft choose either a southward traverse on 25-35° snow toward Sherman Crater, or a direct 4th-class rock buttress / north-edge snow-ice traverse toward Grant Peak.
routes.approach: From Baker Lake Rd, turn onto FSR 1130 then FSR 1131 to the Boulder Ridge trailhead. Hike Boulder Ridge Trail 605 about 2 mi to a clearing, traverse northwest to regain the trail, then scramble a 50-ft 3rd-class rock step to the ridge and continue to bivy sites at about 5,500 ft below the Boulder Glacier.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent line down the cleaver
routes.descentText: Down-climb the summit rock/snow option chosen on ascent, then reverse the cleaver back to high camp; some parties ski or board the descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Long approach via Boulder Ridge Trail to bivy (5,500-7,500 ft). Day 2: Alpine start, climb the cleaver to the summit, descend the same route.
routes.bail: The cleaver allows retreat to the bivy from most points below 9,300 ft; above that, committing to one of the two summit-finish options is generally easier than reversing exposed terrain.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before midday rockfall risk increases below the 9,300 ft buttress.
routes.bestSeason: Early season (spring-early summer) when snow still covers loose rock on the buttress and bridges glacier crevasses; a favorite for solitude on Baker.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage; a very remote approach.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier rope, one picket per person, crevasse rescue gear; helmet mandatory for the rockfall zone below the buttress.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly snow/glacier protection (pickets); minimal rock gear even on the 4th-class buttress option.
whatToBring: Sturdy approach boots for the 3rd-class scramble
Extra food/water for the long approach
proTips: The high camp below the cleaver is one of the most scenic bivies on Baker and sees very few parties.
Helmets are essential — rockfall below the 9,300 ft buttress is a known hazard.
Choose the summit-finish option (snow traverse vs. rock buttress) based on that day's conditions.
watchOut: Rockfall while traversing below the 9,300 ft buttress
Crevasses on the lower Boulder Glacier
Trail is faint and hard to follow above the maintained 2-mile section
knownHazards: Rockfall zone below the summit buttress
Glacier crevasse hazard
Long, arduous, poorly-maintained approach trail

routes.fa: Ed Cooper, Phil Bartow, Donald Grimlund, David Nicholson — August 1957
objHaz: Thin, unstable ice sheet over wet snow
routes.face: Northwest face, bounded by the Roman Nose on the right and serac walls on the left
routes.overview: Mt. Baker's classic ice face: a sustained northwest-facing headwall climbed via firm lower snow slopes into an 800-ft band of 60° water ice above two bergschrund crossings.
routes.beta: Approach onto the northwest face between the Roman Nose and the serac walls. Climb lower slopes of firm 50° snow, cross two bergschrunds, then engage the upper roughly 800 ft of 60° ice (WI3-4) — often only an inch-thick plate over wet snow, offering marginal protection. Simul-climb the lower section; pitch out the steep ice above.
routes.approach: Approach via the Heliotrope Ridge Trail to the Coleman Glacier, then traverse to the base of the northwest face below the Roman Nose.
routes.descent: Coleman-Deming Glacier route
routes.descentText: From the summit plateau, descend the standard Coleman-Deming line down the Roman Wall and past the Black Buttes back to Hogsback camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to a high camp on the Coleman Glacier. Day 2: Alpine start, climb the headwall, top out, descend via Coleman-Deming.
routes.bail: Retreat from the lower snow slopes is possible before committing to the ice band; once on the steep ice, downclimbing or a V-thread rappel on ice screws is the only realistic option.
routes.turnaround: Assess ice quality at the base of the headwall before committing — thin, delaminating ice is a turn-around condition, not just the clock.
routes.bestSeason: Conditions-dependent; route reports suggest a brief early-season window when the ice sheet is thicker and more consolidated.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the face or approach.
routes.detailedRack: 4 snow pickets, 5 ice screws, long runners, two ice tools per climber — a serious water-ice/alpine ice rack.
routes.proNeeds: Marginal protection on the crux ice band — screws often get only shallow purchase in thin ice; back up with picket anchors on the lower snow.
whatToBring: Extra ice screws for thin-ice backup
Warm gloves for sustained ice climbing
V-thread tool for potential rappel retreat
proTips: A serious technical outing, not a beginner glacier route — treat recent condition reports as essential beta.
The upper ice sheet can be startlingly thin; test placements before weighting them.
Descend via Coleman-Deming, not back down the face.
watchOut: Thin, unbonded ice on the upper headwall
Serac fall from walls bounding the route
Two committing bergschrund crossings
knownHazards: Unstable thin ice sheet over wet snow
Serac/icefall exposure from adjacent walls
Marginal protection on the crux pitch

pitchDetail.notes: firm snow, typically simul-climbed
two schrunds to negotiate
thin ice plate over wet snow, sparse screw placements

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Dick Widrig, Ralph Widrig — August 1948
objHaz: Crevasse crossings near the summit plateau
Large exposure over the west face on the upper section
routes.season: Best early season (commonly May) before the Coleman Glacier breaks up
routes.face: North side, the broad ridge enclosing the east side of the north face
routes.overview: A long, committing snow-and-ice ridge climb up Mt. Baker's north side, crossing the Coleman Glacier to gain a broad ridge and threading through a serac band before a steep, exposed finish to the summit plateau.
routes.beta: Cross the Coleman Glacier laterally to the ridge base, then climb snow left of the ridge crest (with crevasse crossings) to gain it. Proceed toward a serac band, choosing between climbing left of its apex, directly right of the ridge crest, or steeper terrain far right — sections here reach WI4. The final push involves steeper snow/ice with large exposure over the west face and more crevasse crossings near the summit plateau.
routes.approach: From the Heliotrope Ridge Trailhead, hike about 2.25 mi to the route fork, then rope up and cross the Coleman Glacier to the base of the ridge.
routes.descent: Coleman-Deming Glacier route
routes.descentText: Descend the standard Coleman-Deming line via the Roman Wall and Black Buttes traverse back to Hogsback camp — do not attempt to reverse the North Ridge.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach to a high camp near Hogsback or the Coleman Glacier (about 7,000-7,200 ft). Day 2: Long alpine day (12-18 hrs) climbing the North Ridge and descending via Coleman-Deming.
routes.bail: Below the serac band, retreat back across the Coleman Glacier to Hogsback camp is feasible; above the serac band, continuing to the summit and descending Coleman-Deming is the standard retreat.
routes.turnaround: Given a typical 12-18 hr round trip, set an early, conservative turnaround time — the route is long even for efficient parties.
routes.bestSeason: Early in the season (commonly May) while the Coleman Glacier is less broken up and crevassed; the serac band's difficulty varies significantly year to year.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage above the trailhead.
routes.detailedRack: 2 snow pickets, 6 ice screws, 1 mountaineering axe, 1-2 technical tools per climber — a substantial alpine ice rack for the serac band.
routes.proNeeds: Screws and pickets for the serac band and steep summit finish; route-finding through the icefall matters as much as protection.
whatToBring: Extra layers for a long alpine day
Headlamp (likely to finish in the dark)
High-calorie food for a 12-18 hr push
proTips: Climb it early season (May) when the Coleman Glacier and serac band are least broken up.
Expect the route to feel longer and more committing than its moderate rating suggests.
Descend via Coleman-Deming, not back down the ridge.
watchOut: Serac band route-finding and steep WI4 sections
Crevasse crossings both on approach and near the summit plateau
Large west-face exposure on the upper mountain
knownHazards: Icefall/serac hazard crossing the Coleman Glacier and serac band
Extended commitment (12-18 hr round trip)
Crevasse fall risk near the summit plateau

pitchDetail.notes: lateral crossing to the ridge base
climb snow left of the ridge crest
multiple line options around ice cliffs
large exposure over the west face, crevasses near the plateau

routes.fa: Norm Bodine, Jim Friar, Tim Keliher — July 4-5, 1971 (headwall variation)
objHaz: Historically poor/marginal ice and snow protection
routes.season: Narrow early-mid summer window, schrund-condition dependent
routes.face: Northeast face, the middle section of the Park Glacier Headwall
routes.overview: A rarely-climbed, remote 1971 variation up the middle of Mt. Baker's Park Glacier Headwall on the northeast side, crossing a large overhanging schrund via a steep snow/ice ramp before several leads of sustained 60° terrain to the ridge crest.
routes.beta: From Camp Kiser or the Portals, descend onto the Rainbow and Park Glaciers and approach the headwall. Cross the bergschrund — reported as 30-50 ft high and overhanging — via a steep (about 60°) snow/ice ramp, then climb roughly three and a half leads of sustained 60° terrain to the ridge crest, turning cornices near the top to reach the summit plateau.
routes.approach: From Artist Point, hike Chain Lakes Trail 682 to Ptarmigan Ridge Trail 682.1, follow Ptarmigan Ridge about 3 mi to the base of Coleman Pinnacle, then continue 0.5 mi to Camp Kiser. A primitive boot path continues toward the Park/Rainbow Glaciers and the headwall.
routes.descent: Descend via the standard Park Glacier or Coleman-Deming route
routes.descentText: From the summit plateau, most parties descend via an easier standard glacier route (Coleman-Deming or the Park Glacier line) rather than reversing the headwall.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Long approach via Ptarmigan Ridge to Camp Kiser or the Portals. Day 2: Descend to the headwall base, climb the headwall, summit, and descend via an easier glacier route.
routes.bail: Below the bergschrund, retreat back along the approach to Camp Kiser is possible; above the schrund, continuing up the ramp and leads to the ridge is generally the only practical option.
routes.turnaround: A long, remote outing — commit only with a very early start and be ready to bail before engaging the schrund if conditions look poor.
routes.bestSeason: A narrow window; historically climbed around early July. Ice/snow conditions at the schrund lip vary significantly year to year and have been reported poor.
routes.comms: No cell coverage; one of the most remote routes on the mountain.
routes.detailedRack: Ice screws and pickets for the sustained 60° terrain and schrund ramp; a full alpine ice rack given historical reports of inadequate protection.
routes.proNeeds: Protection has historically been described as poor/inadequate on this line — treat gear placements conservatively on the ramp and upper leads.
whatToBring: Extra food/water for a long, remote day
Bivy option given the long approach
proTips: This is the longest and most remote of Baker's northern approaches — budget a full day just to reach and return from the Portals/Camp Kiser area.
Cross the bergschrund ramp early before the sun softens it.
Route-finding through the headwall's middle section is the crux; recent trip reports are valuable current beta.
watchOut: Large overhanging bergschrund crossing
Cornices near the summit plateau
Remote location — self-rescue capability essential
knownHazards: Overhanging schrund lip crossing
Sustained steep ice/snow with historically marginal protection
Long, remote, glaciated approach

pitchDetail.notes: crux move over an overhanging lip
sustained steep snow/ice
turn cornices to reach the summit plateau
