objHaz: Bergschrund forms near the summit as the season warms
routes.season: Narrow early-mid season window before the summit bergschrund opens
routes.face: East side, directly up the Boulder Glacier (steepest of Baker's standard glacier routes)
routes.overview: The steepest and most remote of Mt. Baker's standard glacier routes, climbing straight up the east-side Boulder Glacier after an arduous, poorly-maintained approach — a quiet alternative to the crowded Coleman-Deming and Easton lines.
routes.beta: From high camp on Boulder Ridge (about 5,700 ft), gain the glacier and climb 500-1,000 ft avoiding small crevasses, then turn north to avoid a small buttress before heading west straight up-glacier. The final roughly 500 ft to the summit climbs a steep snow slope.
routes.approach: From Baker Lake Rd, follow FSR 1130 then FSR 1131 to the Boulder Ridge Trailhead. The trail is maintained only about 2 mi to the wilderness boundary; beyond, it becomes steep, muddy, and hard to follow with a 3rd-class scramble to reach camp on the ridge (about 5,700 ft) or a sheltered flat near the cleaver (about 7,500 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line down the Boulder Glacier
routes.descentText: Down-climb the steep summit snow slope, then reverse north/east back down the glacier to the Boulder Ridge camp, watching for softened crevasse bridges in the afternoon.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Arduous approach (4-5 hrs) to high camp on Boulder Ridge. Day 2: Alpine start, climb the Boulder Glacier (5-7 hrs) to the summit, descend the same route.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier to Boulder Ridge camp is straightforward from most points below the final summit slope.
routes.turnaround: The late-forming summit bergschrund and short climbing season mean conditions should be checked against recent trip reports before committing — turn around if the schrund looks unpassable rather than forcing it.
routes.bestSeason: A narrow early-to-mid season window before the summit bergschrund fully opens as the mountain warms; the difficult approach naturally limits traffic all season.
routes.comms: No cell coverage; one of the more remote, lightly-traveled sides of the mountain.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier rope, pickets, crevasse rescue kit; add a couple of ice screws for the late-season bergschrund crossing near the summit.
routes.proNeeds: Standard glacier snow/ice protection; the summit bergschrund crossing may need screws depending on the season.
whatToBring: Sturdy boots for the muddy, unmaintained trail
Extra time buffer for a slow, faint-trail approach
GPS/map for the unmaintained upper trail
proTips: Expect solitude — the difficult approach keeps crowds away compared to Coleman-Deming and Easton.
The summit bergschrund forms as the season warms, so climb it early if possible.
Considered the steepest of Baker's standard glacier routes.
watchOut: Faint, unmaintained upper approach trail with a 3rd-class scramble
Bergschrund forming near the summit as the season progresses
Crevasses on the Boulder Glacier
knownHazards: Late-season summit bergschrund
Glacier crevasse hazard
Difficult route-finding on the unmaintained approach

road.name: FSR 1130/1131 (Boulder Ridge Rd)
road.seasonalGate: Seasonal, summer access only
road.driveNote: From Sedro-Woolley: SR-20 east 16 mi to Baker Lake Hwy (FSR 11), 18 mi north; past Boulder Creek Campground turn left on FSR 1130 for 1 mi, then left on FSR 1131 to the trailhead

climate.typical: Wet maritime alpine climate with heavy annual snowfall and rapid weather changes
climate.spring: Best window before the summit bergschrund opens
climate.summer: Bergschrund widens; crevasses open on the lower glacier
climate.fall: Storms return
climate.winter: Extremely heavy snow, road closures, high avalanche danger

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mt. Baker Ranger District)
access.permit: No climbing permit required; Mt. Baker Wilderness group-size limit of 12, campfires prohibited except on portions of this route
access.closures: Trail maintained only about 2 mi to the wilderness boundary; beyond is steep, muddy, and hard to follow with a 3rd-class scramble

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start from high camp

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (Whatcom County Sheriff's Office dispatches Bellingham Mountain Rescue Council)
emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center, (360) 599-2714; Mt. Baker Ranger District, (360) 856-5700
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham — Level II trauma center, (360) 734-5400
emergency.notes: No cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator/PLB.

blurb: Mount Ellinor (5,944 ft) is a prominent, popular summit on the eastern front of the Olympic Mountains above Lake Cushman in Mason County, Washington, within the Mount Skokomish Wilderness. Surveyor George Davidson named it in 1853 for his fiancée Ellinor Fauntleroy; the first recorded ascent by American settlers was made in August 1879 by D.N. Utler, Mr. and Mrs. J.W. Waughop, and H.C. Esteps. It is one of the most accessible high summits in the Olympics — a steep, non-technical trail hike rather than a rock climb — known for panoramic 360-degree views and resident mountain goats.
hierarchyNote: None — verified. Given coordinates (47.521635, -123.260675) convert to essentially the same point as Wikipedia's cited 47°31'18"N 123°15'38"W, the given elevation (5,944 ft) matches the USFS Mt. Ellinor Trail #812 page exactly, and "Eastern Olympic Mountains" above Lake Cushman/Hoodsport in Mason County is geographically correct for this peak.

routes.overview: A steep, very popular 3.2-mile round-trip trail from the upper (3,500 ft) trailhead that climbs directly up Mount Ellinor's south ridge — through forest, then an open rocky/avalanche-chute section and gravelly alpine meadows — to a broad, goat-frequented summit with 360-degree views across the Olympics, Puget Sound, Hood Canal, and (on clear days) Mount Rainier.
routes.beta: From the upper trailhead the trail climbs almost immediately via stacked switchbacks for about 0.3 mile before merging with the longer lower-trailhead trail at roughly 3,900 ft. From there it continues at a steady, 'character-building' grade through forest, breaks out of the trees into rock-strewn slopes and gravelly meadows, passes through a notch, and finishes with a short scramble to the true summit. It is a non-technical trail/scramble (no rope or rack needed in summer conditions) but is consistently steep with minimal flat ground. This is the standard, most direct route; a longer alternative starts from the lower trailhead (2,600 ft, no fee) and joins the same trail at the 3,900 ft merge point.
routes.approach: From Hoodsport on US-101, take State Route 119 north 9.0 miles to its end at Forest Road 24; turn right and continue 1.6 miles to Forest Road 2419 (Big Creek Road); follow FR-2419 for 4.9 miles, passing the lower trailhead at 4.9 miles, then continue about 1.5 more miles and turn left onto FR-2419-014, following it about 1 mile to its end at the upper trailhead (3,500 ft, 47.510466, -123.247912). A Northwest Forest Pass or $5/day-use fee is required to park at the upper trailhead; the lower trailhead is free.
routes.descent: Descend via the same Summer Trail switchbacks back to the upper trailhead.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent route down the south ridge switchbacks. Take particular care descending the steep, loose upper slopes near the summit notch and through the open chute/basin, where wet rock is reported as extremely slick; lingering snow or ice here into early summer can make the descent more hazardous than the climb. Stay on the established tread through alpine meadows to protect fragile vegetation.
routes.itinerary: Single-day outing: drive to the upper trailhead (allow ~1.5-2 hrs from the Seattle/Tacoma area via Hoodsport), hike up (~2 hrs), enjoy the summit, then descend the same way (~1.5 hrs) — a half-day round trip including the drive.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at any point by turning around and descending the same trail; there is no technical commitment. The steepest, most exposed terrain is the open chute/basin and the final notch just below the summit — treat that section as the turnaround point in deteriorating weather, low visibility, or if snow/ice is present without an ice axe.
routes.turnaround: Because the upper mountain is open, south-facing, and offers little shelter or route-finding cues in cloud, set a turnaround time that gets you off the exposed chute and summit basin before afternoon thunderstorms, wind, or fog move in, and before early-season snow softens into avalanche-prone slush.
routes.bestSeason: Typically snow-free and hikeable roughly June through October, with July-September the most reliable and driest window. Snow and ice can linger in the summit chute/basin into June, sometimes requiring an ice axe for safe passage. Fall offers clear views but shorter days. Outside this window (late fall through spring), the route becomes a steep snow/ice couloir climb requiring full winter travel gear and avalanche assessment, not a standard hiking trail.
gear: Ice axe (recommended into early summer / any snow in the chute)
Microspikes or crampons (icy or snow-covered sections)
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or $5 day-use fee (upper trailhead)
Full water bottle/canteen — no reliable water source on route
Sun protection for exposed, south-facing slopes
Extra layers for wind/weather on the open ridge and summit
routes.proNeeds: None — this is a non-technical trail/scramble; no rope, harness, or protection is used in standard summer conditions. Winter/early-season ascents of the chute route call for an ice axe and traction (crampons/microspikes) rather than a climbing rack.
proTips: Start early, especially on summer weekends — both the upper and lower trailhead lots are small and fill quickly.
The upper and lower trailhead trails merge at about 3,900 ft, so with a shuttle car you can hike up one trailhead and down the other as a one-way variation.
Give the resident mountain goats a wide berth and never let them lick sweat or urine off gear or the ground — habituated goats here have charged and injured hikers in the past.
watchOut: The summit chute/basin holds snow and ice well into early summer and has been the site of serious slip-and-fall injuries requiring helicopter rescue by Mason County SAR/USCG.
Rock in the upper gully and near the summit notch is reported as extremely slick when wet.
Mountain goats near the summit have charged and gored hikers historically (including a 1999 goring incident); the trail has been temporarily closed in the past due to aggressive goat behavior.
knownHazards: Steep, loose, rock-strewn slopes above treeline with minimal margin for a slip.
A seasonal snow/avalanche chute (the winter route line) that can retain avalanche and fall hazard into early summer.
Aggressive, human-habituated mountain goats frequenting the summit area.

pitchDetail.notes: Stacked switchbacks climbing steeply through forest for ~0.3 mi, from 3,500 ft to the ~3,900 ft junction with the lower trailhead trail.
Steady, steep forested switchbacks.
Trail breaks into open rock-strewn slopes and gravelly alpine meadows; follows the historic avalanche chute line in winter.
Short final scramble through a notch to the summit; loose rock and exposure reported by climbers; the crux of the route in terms of footing.

road.name: Forest Road 2419 (Big Creek Road) and FR 2419-014
road.seasonalGate: Unmaintained and typically gated/inaccessible in winter and early spring; reopens as snow clears, usually by late spring
road.driveNote: From Hoodsport: SR-119 north 9.0 mi to FR-24; right 1.6 mi to FR-2419; continue 4.9 mi to the lower trailhead, or another ~1.5 mi and left onto FR-2419-014 to the upper trailhead at road's end.

climate.typical: Wet, changeable Olympic Peninsula mountain weather; the summit is frequently in cloud even when Hood Canal and Lake Cushman below are clear.
climate.spring: Snow lingers in the summit chute/basin into June; avalanche hazard is possible on the steeper winter line.
climate.summer: Warmest and driest window (roughly Jul-Sep); trail is typically snow-free by late June or July.
climate.fall: Cooler temperatures with often-clear views into October, but shorter days and the possibility of early-season snow.
climate.winter: The route becomes a steep, avalanche-prone snow/ice couloir requiring ice axe, crampons, and avalanche skills; the access road is frequently gated.

access.landManager: Olympic National Forest, Hood Canal Ranger District; Mount Skokomish Wilderness
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or Northwest Forest Pass at upper trailhead; free at lower trailhead
access.permit: No permit required for day hiking
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (upper trailhead only)
access.closures: Occasional past closures for aggressive mountain-goat activity; seasonal winter road gating

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, particularly on summer weekends, to secure trailhead parking and be off the exposed upper mountain before afternoon weather or peak goat activity

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Mason County Sheriff's Office non-emergency line (via MACECOM/Mason County 911): (360) 426-4441
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Forest — Hood Canal/Quilcene Office, 295142 Highway 101 S, Quilcene, WA 98376, (360) 765-2200
emergency.nearestHospital: Mason General Hospital (Mason Health), 901 Mountain View Dr, Shelton, WA, (360) 426-1611 — Level IV trauma emergency department, 24/7
emergency.notes: Search and rescue is coordinated by the Mason County Sheriff's Office with volunteer Mason County Search & Rescue; past incidents in the summit chute/basin (falls, injuries) have required helicopter extraction, including by USCG.

routes.season: Jun-Oct (summer trail); winter ascent via steep chute possible with full winter gear
objHaz: Seasonal snow/avalanche chute (winter line) with fall/avalanche risk into early summer
Slick wet rock near the summit notch
routes.corrections: Given elevation (5,944 ft) matches the primary USFS Mt. Ellinor Trail #812 source and WTA; note Wikipedia cites a slightly different NAVD88 figure of 5,952 ft (130 m prominence) for the same summit — a minor cross-source survey discrepancy, not treated as an error since the given/USFS figure is better corroborated. No other conflicts found with the given data.

blurb: Mount Noyes is a 6,173-ft summit deep in the interior of Olympic National Park (Jefferson County, WA), on the divide between the Elwha and Quinault River drainages about seven miles southeast of Mount Olympus. It is built largely of Eocene sandstone, turbidite, and basaltic oceanic crust accreted onto the Olympic core, and sits less than a mile south of much larger Mount Meany (6,695 ft), which it neighbors across a narrow col. The peak was first climbed in May 1907 by Asahel Curtis and Grant Humes while reconnoitering approaches for The Mountaineers' historic first ascent of Mount Olympus later that same year; it was named during the 1889-90 Seattle Press Expedition for newspaper publisher Crosby Stuart Noyes.

routes.overview: A remote off-trail scramble to a minor but rugged summit on the ridge linking Mount Christie, Mount Noyes, Mount Meany, and Mount Seattle, deep in the Elwha-Quinault divide country of Olympic National Park. Three established lines reach the top: via the Noyes-Meany col to the north, the Seattle-Noyes col to the southeast, or from Low Divide to the west.
routes.beta: Mount Noyes is rarely climbed as a standalone objective; most parties tag it while on a multi-day scramble traverse linking Mount Christie, Noyes, Meany, and Seattle from a base camp near Low Divide or Seattle Creek/Twelvemile Shelter. The standard line follows steep talus, heather, and (early season) snow to one of the peak's bounding cols, then scrambles class 2-3 terrain along the summit ridge to the top. Multiple trip reports describe the approach itself -- a long sidehill traverse across eroded, loose gullies beneath the peak's east and west aspects -- as the real crux of the day, more consequential than the summit scrambling.
routes.approach: From the North Fork Quinault Trailhead (~525 ft, at the end of the North Fork Quinault River Road), follow the North Fork Quinault River Trail roughly 16-17 miles, passing backcountry camps at Wolf Bar (2.5 mi), Halfway House (5.1 mi), Elip Creek (6.5 mi), Trapper Shelter (8.1 mi), and Sixteenmile (13.2 mi), to Low Divide (~3,600 ft) -- the historic pass separating the Quinault and Elwha drainages. From Low Divide, continue on the primitive, minimally-maintained Skyline Ridge Trail toward Promise Creek Bench (~5,340 ft, the trail's high point) and on to Seattle Creek/Twelvemile Shelter (a common high camp). Leave the trail here to sidehill beneath Mount Noyes across steep, eroded terrain to reach either the Noyes-Meany col (north side) or the Seattle-Noyes col (southeast side), from which the final scramble to the summit begins. Three unbridged fords of the North Fork Quinault River on the lower trail can be difficult at high water.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent col and talus/snow slopes back to the Skyline Trail, then follow the North Fork Quinault Trail out to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the same class 2-3 ridge/gully terrain used on ascent back to whichever col was used (Noyes-Meany or Seattle-Noyes). Descend steep talus and snow below the col back into the basin, then re-trace the loose sidehill traverse beneath the peak to regain the primitive Skyline Trail. Follow the Skyline Trail back over Promise Creek Bench to Low Divide, then the maintained North Fork Quinault River Trail roughly 16-17 miles back to the trailhead. Snow on the col approaches can be firm in early morning; descend with an ice axe and be ready to self-arrest. The eroded sidehill gullies beneath the peak are loose underfoot and are repeatedly cited by parties as the most hazardous part of the whole outing, both up and down.
routes.itinerary: Typical trip is 3-5 days: Day 1, hike in from the North Fork Quinault Trailhead to a camp near Low Divide, Seattle Creek, or Twelvemile Shelter (11-16 miles). Day 2, leave camp, sidehill beneath Noyes to the chosen col, scramble to the summit (often combined with Mount Meany and/or Mount Seattle on the same ridge day via the connecting cols), and return to camp. Day 3 onward, hike back out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is generally straightforward by reversing the ascent col and dropping back into the basin below; from anywhere on the route the Skyline Trail and North Fork Quinault Trail provide a continuous (if long) walking exit with no technical downclimbing required. The main bail hazard is the loose, eroded sidehill traverse beneath the peak, which is unpleasant and slow but not technical in either direction.
routes.turnaround: Given the length of the approach and the loose, exposed sidehill traverse below the peak, set a firm turnaround time that leaves ample daylight to re-cross the eroded gully sections and regain the trail before dark. Afternoon thunderstorms and fast-moving weather are common in the interior Olympics, and there is no quick escape from this terrain once committed.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is the standard window, after seasonal snow has consolidated enough on the col approaches to travel safely but before early-fall storms move in. Peakbagger and Wikipedia both cite June-August as the most favorable months for the peak generally; snow can linger on the north-facing col approaches into July.
gear: Crampons (useful for firm early-season snow on the col approaches)
Map and compass or GPS (the Skyline Trail is unmaintained with poor tread)
Bear canister (recommended in Olympic NP backcountry)
whatToBring: Wilderness backpacking permit (booked in advance via Recreation.gov)
Layers for exposed, changeable ridge weather
Trekking poles for the loose sidehill traverse
Extra food/fuel buffer for weather delays on a multi-day approach
proTips: Most parties combine Noyes with neighboring Mount Meany and/or Mount Seattle on the same trip, since all three share the same long approach up the North Fork Quinault and over Low Divide.
The sidehill traverse beneath Noyes is repeatedly reported as the most tedious and hazardous part of the day -- budget time and energy for it, not just for the summit scrambling.
A high camp at Seattle Creek/Twelvemile Shelter (rather than stopping at Low Divide) meaningfully shortens the summit day.
watchOut: The eroded sidehill gullies beneath the peak are loose and steep; multiple trip reports describe the traverse as 'nasty' and 'disheartening' to reverse.
Snow on the col approaches -- including a roughly 60-degree snowfinger reported below the Noyes-Meany col -- can be firm in early morning; carry an ice axe and know how to self-arrest.
Weather in the interior Olympics changes quickly, and the long, exposed approach leaves little option for a fast retreat once committed.
knownHazards: Loose rock and steep, eroded gully terrain on the sidehill traverse below the peak.
Firm, steep snow slopes (reported up to roughly 60 degrees in places) on the col approaches, especially early season.
Three unbridged fords of the North Fork Quinault River on the approach trail, difficult at high water.
Black bears are present along the North Fork Quinault corridor; a bear canister is recommended.

pitchDetail.notes: ~16-17 mi on the North Fork Quinault River Trail via Wolf Bar, Halfway House, Elip Creek, Trapper Shelter, and Sixteenmile camps to Low Divide (~3,600 ft).
Skyline Ridge Trail, minimal tread and routefinding required; crosses Promise Creek Bench (~5,340 ft, trail high point).
Eroded sidehill gullies beneath Noyes' east/west aspects; widely reported as the crux of the day.

pitchDetail.pitch: Col to summit (Noyes-Meany or Seattle-Noyes col)
pitchDetail.notes: Talus and/or snow slope to the chosen col, then a short class 2-3 ridge scramble to the summit.

road.name: North Fork Quinault River Road
road.status: NPS-maintained gravel road to the trailhead; check the park's current road-conditions page before travel
road.seasonalGate: May be gated or closed in winter
road.driveNote: Road ends at the North Fork Quinault Trailhead/Ranger Station, the start of the North Fork Quinault River Trail.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; wet, mild winters with heavy precipitation (especially October-April) and drier, cooler summers than the coast
climate.spring: Persistent snowpack on north-facing col approaches, often lingering into June
climate.summer: Driest, most stable window for this route, June through August
climate.fall: Increasing storm frequency and possible early snow from September onward
climate.winter: Heavy snow; road and trail access typically closed or very difficult

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (NPS) Wilderness
access.fees: $8 per person per night camping fee (age 16+) plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee per permit; a $45 annual pass covers the camping fee for 12 months
access.permit: Wilderness backpacking permit required year-round, booked in advance via Recreation.gov -- no walk-up permits at trailheads
access.passRequired: NPS wilderness permit reservation (Recreation.gov)
access.closures: North Fork Quinault River Road may be gated/closed in winter; check the NPS road-conditions page before driving in

timing.recommendedStart: Early-morning start from high camp so the sidehill traverse and any snow slopes are crossed while still cool/firm

emergency.county: Jefferson County (summit); trailhead accessed via Grays Harbor County
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Jefferson County Sheriff (911) for the summit area; Grays Harbor County Sheriff for the trailhead side
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center, Port Angeles: (360) 565-3100
emergency.notes: Extremely remote interior location roughly 16+ miles by trail from the nearest trailhead; expect an NPS search-and-rescue response and evacuation to take substantially longer than at a roadside objective.

objHaz: Steep eroded sidehill traverse gullies below the peak
Steep snow slopes on col approaches (up to ~60 deg reported)
River fords on the approach trail

blurb: Mount Olympus is the highest and most prominent peak in Washington's Olympic Mountains, rising to 7,980 ft (2,432 m) within Olympic National Park about 20 miles southeast of Forks. It is built of uplifted Eocene marine sedimentary rock (shale and sandstone) and is heavily glaciated — seven glaciers, including the Blue and Hoh Glaciers, cling to its flanks — sitting in one of the wettest climates in the continental U.S. (the west slopes receive well over 200 inches of precipitation a year). The mountain has three summits (West, Middle, East); the true summit is the West Peak, first reached in 1907 by a party including L.A. Nelson via the glacier approach that remains the standard route today.

routes.overview: A linkup of all three Mount Olympus summits — West (true summit, 7,980 ft), Middle, and East Peak — reached via the standard Blue Glacier/Snow Dome approach and descended off the East Peak side to Glacier Meadows via Glacier Pass.
routes.beta: After climbing the West Peak summit block via the standard Blue Glacier route, parties descend and traverse across to the Middle Peak, then continue to the East Peak, before dropping off the east side and descending to Glacier Meadow via Glacier Pass. It is typically done as a 3-4 day trip, carrying food for 4 days given the remote, weather-exposed setting.
routes.approach: Same as the Blue Glacier standard route: Hoh River Trail (17.5 mi) from the Hoh Rain Forest trailhead to Glacier Meadows, then across the Blue Glacier and Snow Dome to the West Peak summit block.
routes.descent: From the East Peak, descend to Glacier Pass and down to Glacier Meadows, rejoining the Hoh River Trail for the hike out.
routes.descentText: Rather than reversing the full traverse, parties descend off the East Peak side through Glacier Pass back down to Glacier Meadows, avoiding re-crossing all three summits. Expect loose, unconsolidated terrain and route-finding on the descent.
routes.itinerary: Typically 3-4 days: hike in via the Hoh River Trail to Glacier Meadows/Snow Dome high camp (1-2 days), climb West Peak then traverse to Middle and East Peaks (1 day), descend via Glacier Pass to Glacier Meadows and hike out (1 day).
routes.bail: Bail is easiest before committing to the ridge between summits; once on the West Peak summit block, retreat by the same rope/downclimb used on the standard route rather than attempting an unplanned descent line.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround based on remaining daylight and weather to ensure the crevassed glacier crossing and any technical summit-block sections are completed before conditions deteriorate or snow bridges soften in the afternoon.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through August, matching the standard Blue Glacier route window; the traverse adds a full extra summit day so weather-stability margins matter more than for the West Peak alone.
routes.rockGrade: Class 4 scrambling with a short 5.4 step on the West Peak summit block
routes.proNeeds: Standard glacier travel kit plus a light rock rack for the summit block moves; no fixed protection between summits.
proTips: Descending off the East Peak via Glacier Pass avoids re-crossing all three summits
Carry food for 4 days even if planning a 3-day push, given weather variability
watchOut: Loose rock and route-finding on the inter-summit ridges
Extended exposure to weather over a multi-summit day
knownHazards: Crevasses and bergschrunds on the Blue Glacier crossing
Loose rock traversing between West, Middle, and East summits

road.name: Upper Hoh Road
road.driveNote: Ends at the Hoh Rain Forest Visitor Center/trailhead off US-101; summer parking is limited — arrive before 8am or after 5pm to avoid long entrance-station waits.

climate.typical: Among the wettest places in the continental U.S.; the west slopes see 200+ inches of annual precipitation.
climate.summer: Relatively drier but still wet; glacier travel and river fords are primary hazards.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall, avalanche terrain above Elk Lake.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (NPS)
access.fees: NPS entrance fee applies at the Hoh entrance station; wilderness camping permit fee required.
access.permit: Wilderness permit required (reserve via recreation.gov); bear canister mandatory beyond Glacier Meadows; parties over 6 need a designated group site.
access.passRequired: NPS Olympic National Park entrance fee/pass
access.closures: No camping permitted between Glacier Meadows and the Blue Glacier; camping restricted to designated sites (Glacier Meadows, Elk Lake, Snow Dome).

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start from Snow Dome high camp on summit day

emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center, 600 E. Park Ave, Port Angeles, WA — (360) 565-3130
emergency.nearestHospital: Forks Community Hospital, 530 Bogachiel Way, Forks, WA — (360) 374-6271
emergency.notes: Remote, multi-day approach; self-rescue capability essential given distance from road access.

routes.routeName: Blue Glacier / Snow Dome — East Face Ramps Finish (Standard)
routes.overview: The standard, class 4/low-5th-class summit-block finish to the West Peak, climbing broken east-facing ramps from the top of the Blue Glacier/Snow Dome approach to the true summit.
routes.beta: From the false summit reached after crossing the upper Blue Glacier past Crystal Pass and the Five Finger towers, a short (~70 ft) rock step leads to the true summit, mostly 4th class with a few 5.4 moves. The crux is climbing through a moat with significant exposure on both sides just below the top.
routes.approach: Via the Blue Glacier standard route: Hoh River Trail to Glacier Meadows, moraine/ladder descent to the Blue Glacier, glacier crossing to Snow Dome, then up through Crystal Pass to the base of the summit block.
routes.descent: Rappel the summit pitch, then descend snow slopes (glissading where safe) back across Snow Dome and the Blue Glacier to the moraine and Glacier Meadows.
routes.descentText: From the summit, rappel the short east-face pitch back down to the false summit/glacier shoulder. From there, descend the snow slopes of Snow Dome (glissading conditions permitting), re-cross the upper Blue Glacier roped up, and regain the lateral moraine via the fixed ladder to return to Glacier Meadows.
routes.bail: Retreat is via the same short rappel used for the descent; there is no separate bail line off the summit block.
routes.turnaround: Reach the base of the summit block early enough to climb it and re-cross the glacier before afternoon softening of snow bridges or incoming weather.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through mid-August, when the Blue Glacier crossing and summit ramps are most consistently climbable.
routes.rappels: 1 rappel off the summit block (~70 ft)
routes.fa: 1907, L.A. Nelson and party (first ascent of Mount Olympus, via this general line)
routes.detailedRack: Minimal rack — a few slings/small pieces for the short pitch; can be led with minimal protection.
routes.proNeeds: Light protection only; the pitch is short and can be top-roped or simul-climbed by confident parties.
gear: Ice axe and crampons for the glacier approach
whatToBring: Approach shoes/boots suitable for both snow and rock
proTips: The crux moat crossing has exposure on both sides — test rock carefully before committing
The route can be led with minimal gear given its short length
watchOut: Loose, crumbly sedimentary rock
High exposure crossing the summit moat
knownHazards: Loose rock on the final pitch
Crevasse hazard on the glacier approach below

pitchDetail.notes: Crux through the moat with incredible exposure to both sides; loose rock; leads to the true (West Peak) summit.

road.name: Upper Hoh Road
road.driveNote: Ends at the Hoh Rain Forest Visitor Center/trailhead off US-101.

climate.typical: Among the wettest places in the continental U.S.
climate.summer: Relatively drier but still wet.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall, avalanche terrain lower on the approach.

routes.routeName: Summit Block — North Face Direct Finish (variation)
routes.overview: A harder, more direct technical variation finishing the summit block via its north side, in the low fifth class, used by experienced parties seeking more technical climbing than the standard east-ramps finish.
routes.beta: Reported by guide sources as an alternative, more sustained line up the summit pinnacle in the low fifth class; also used as a rappel line (two rappels with a 60m rope) when descending from the West Ridge route.
routes.approach: Same glacier approach as the standard route (Hoh River Trail to Glacier Meadows, Blue Glacier/Snow Dome crossing to the base of the summit block), then around to the north side of the block.
routes.descent: Descend via the standard east-ramps route or rappel the North Face itself (two rappels with a 60m rope, as used by West Ridge parties).
routes.descentText: Most parties descend via the standard summit-block finish rather than reversing the North Face. Where used as a rappel line (e.g., off the West Ridge), two rappels with a 60m rope are needed to reach the glacier below.
routes.bail: Retreat via rappel on gear/natural anchors back toward the glacier; do not attempt to downclimb unfamiliar terrain on this face.
routes.bestSeason: Summer (late June-August), matching glacier-crossing conditions for the approach.
routes.rockGrade: Low 5th class (harder than the standard east-ramps finish; exact grade not consistently published)
routes.proNeeds: A small rock rack appropriate for low-5th-class terrain on variable sedimentary rock; treat as an alpine variation rather than a well-established sport line.
gear: Glacier travel gear for the approach
watchOut: Sedimentary rock quality varies and can be loose
Less traveled than the standard finish, so less beta and fewer fixed anchors
knownHazards: Loose rock
Sustained exposure on a less-traveled line

routes.overview: The standard route up Mount Olympus: a long approach through Hoh Rain Forest, a glacier crossing of the Blue Glacier and Snow Dome, and a short class 4/5.4 summit-block finish to the true (West) summit — the line of the 1907 first ascent.
routes.beta: Follow the Hoh River Trail to Glacier Meadows, descend the lateral moraine (via a fixed ladder) to the Blue Glacier, cross the glacier roped up to Snow Dome, continue through Crystal Pass and past the Five Finger towers on the upper glacier to a false summit, then climb the short east-face ramps to the true summit. It is the most accessible and most-climbed line on the peak, though the approach alone is a substantial multi-day backpack.
routes.approach: From the Hoh Rain Forest trailhead (Hoh River Ranger Station, ~573 ft, end of Upper Hoh Road off US-101), follow the Hoh River Trail 17.5 miles through old-growth rainforest, passing 5-Mile Island, the Olympus Guard Station, Lewis Meadow, and Elk Lake, to Glacier Meadows (~4,300-4,400 ft). From Glacier Meadows, descend the steep, loose lateral moraine (~400 ft, via a fixed ladder in recent years) to reach the Blue Glacier, then travel roped up across the glacier (~2 miles) to Snow Dome (~6,700 ft), a common high camp.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: rappel/downclimb the summit block, descend Snow Dome and Crystal Pass back across the Blue Glacier, regain the moraine via the ladder, and hike out the Hoh River Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, rappel the short summit-block pitch back to the glacier shoulder. Descend the Snow Dome snowfield (glissading where conditions allow) and re-cross the upper Blue Glacier roped up through Crystal Pass, watching for softened snow bridges in the afternoon. Regain the lateral moraine via the fixed ladder and continue down to Glacier Meadows, then hike out the full 17.5-mile Hoh River Trail.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike in from the trailhead toward Elk Lake/Lewis Meadow. Day 2: Continue to Glacier Meadows (17.5 mi total) and/or push on to a Snow Dome high camp via the moraine and Blue Glacier crossing. Day 3: Alpine start from Snow Dome, cross the upper glacier via Crystal Pass to the summit block, climb the class 4/5.4 finish, and return to camp. Day 4: Hike out via the Hoh River Trail.
routes.bail: The approach is a straightforward out-and-back until the glacier; once on the Blue Glacier, retreat via the same roped route used for the ascent rather than improvising a line through crevassed terrain. From the summit block, the same short rappel/downclimb used to ascend is the only reasonable retreat.
routes.turnaround: Start early enough on summit day to complete the glacier crossing and summit-block climb before afternoon softening of snow bridges or deteriorating weather; this is a genuine multi-day wilderness commitment, so build in a weather-contingency day.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through mid-August is the NPS-recommended window, when the trail is clear of snow and the Blue Glacier's crevasses are most predictable. The glacier remains climbable into September in good years, but crevasses widen and snow bridges deteriorate later in the season; early-season attempts face higher river fords and colder but more consolidated glacier travel.
routes.rappels: 1 rappel off the summit block (~70 ft)
objHaz: avalanche terrain above Elk Lake in winter/spring
high water at river fords early season
routes.fa: 1907, L.A. Nelson and party
routes.detailedRack: Minimal technical rack: a short length of rope and a few slings for the summit pitch, plus full glacier travel kit (ice axe, crampons, rope, pickets, crevasse-rescue hardware) for the approach.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel protection (pickets, crevasse rescue system) is the primary need; the summit rock pitch requires only minimal gear.
proTips: Reserve wilderness permits early via recreation.gov given camp quotas at Glacier Meadows
Use the fixed ladder at the moraine rather than the loose, rockfall-prone slopes beside it
A Snow Dome high camp shortens summit day considerably versus pushing from Glacier Meadows
Rope up for the entire Blue Glacier crossing, including on the way back
watchOut: The lateral moraine descent is steep, loose, and has rockfall danger
Upper glacier has icefalls and bergschrunds
Snow bridges soften and crevasses widen later in the season
Summit block rock is loose sedimentary rock
knownHazards: Numerous crevasses requiring roped travel and route-finding
Rapid glacier melt producing slushy, less stable snow conditions later in the day
Exposure and loose rock on the final summit pitch
High water at river crossings in early season

pitchDetail.notes: Short (~70 ft) summit block finish; crux is the exposed moat crossing near the top.

road.name: Upper Hoh Road
road.driveNote: Paved road ending at the Hoh Rain Forest Visitor Center/trailhead off US-101; summer parking is limited — arrive before 8am or after 5pm to avoid entrance-station waits of up to two hours.

climate.spring: Higher river fords, more avalanche hazard on the approach.
climate.summer: Relatively drier but still frequently wet; primary climbing season for glacier travel.
climate.fall: Crevasses more open, snow bridges less reliable.

routes.overview: A 7-pitch, 700-foot 5.4 alpine rock ridge on the West Peak of Mount Olympus, gained from the Blue Glacier via a moat at 7,260 ft and climbed with variable rock quality and high exposure to a junction with the standard route near the summit.
routes.beta: From a Snow Dome high camp, cross the Blue Glacier southwest toward Crystal Pass and the Five Finger towers to locate the West Ridge. Gain the ridge via a moat at its lowest point (~7,260 ft) and climb roughly 600 ft of ridge, staying high on the south side to avoid looser rock above. The route is not always obvious, with sparse cairns and no established rappel stations reported as of recent trip reports.
routes.approach: From the Hoh River Ranger Station (~573 ft), follow the Hoh River Trail 17.5 miles to Glacier Meadows (~4,300 ft). Descend to the Blue Glacier via the lateral-moraine ladder, then travel roped up ~2 miles across the glacier to a Snow Dome high camp (~6,700 ft).
routes.descent: The recommended descent is via the standard Blue Glacier route through Crystal Pass (crevasse hazard), rather than reversing the ridge.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend via the standard Blue Glacier/Crystal Pass route rather than reversing the West Ridge. If bailing or descending the ridge itself, expect to downclimb much of it and carry rappel gear: a single 50m rappel off the West Face, or two rappels (60m rope) off the North Face, are the reported options.
routes.bail: Retreat down the ridge itself (downclimbing/rappelling) is possible but slow and exposed; where feasible, traverse back to the standard Blue Glacier route instead.
routes.turnaround: Given variable rock quality and sparse route-finding beta, turn around early if conditions or visibility make the ridge line unclear rather than pushing into unknown terrain late in the day.
routes.bestSeason: Summer (implied July-September) once the Blue Glacier crossing to reach the ridge base is in reasonable condition.
routes.rappels: If descending the route: 1 rappel off the West Face (50m) or 2 rappels off the North Face (60m rope); standard descent avoids rappelling the ridge at all.
objHaz: crevasses on the glacier approach to the ridge
routes.fa: 1964, Gary Maykut, Len Miller, and Joe Witte
routes.detailedRack: Single 50m dry-treated rope minimum; offset nuts 1/3"-1"; tricams to 2"; long slings for anchors and running protection over horns/chockstones; 2-3 snow pickets and glacier rescue gear for the approach.
routes.proNeeds: A light trad rack weighted toward small-to-medium gear (nuts, tricams) supplemented by long slings, since natural placements (horns, chockstones) are common on the variable rock.
whatToBring: Multi-day backpacking/glacier camp kit for the Snow Dome approach
Rappel gear in case of retreat down the ridge
proTips: Gain the ridge at the low-point moat (~7,260 ft), not higher on the crest
Stay high on the south side of the ridge — looser rock lies above
Plan to descend via the standard Blue Glacier/Crystal Pass route rather than reversing the ridge
watchOut: Rock quality varies and can be loose without warning
No cairns or fixed rap stations reported as of recent trip reports — route-finding is on you
Off-route terrain runs 5.6-5.7 with more exposure
Rockfall danger below the ridge and at the moat
knownHazards: Crevasse crossing required to reach the base of the ridge
Loose, variable-quality rock throughout
High, sustained exposure on the ridge crest

pitchDetail.notes: ~600 ft of ridge climbing above the entry moat at 7,260 ft; stay high on the south side to avoid looser rock above; gear to 2" with long slings for pro and anchors.

blurb: Sherpa Peak is an 8,630-foot granite summit in the Stuart Range within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, standing about 0.4 miles east of Mount Stuart, with Argonaut Peak roughly 1.3 miles further east. Built of granodiorite from the Mount Stuart batholith, it is marked by the distinctive "Sherpa Balanced Rock" formation along its East Ridge and by the hanging Sherpa Glacier on its north face; the peak takes its name from a local Seattle-area climbing club active in the 1950s, officially adopted as the toponym in 1988. It was first climbed in 1955 by Dave Mahre, Bill Prater, and Gene Prater.
hierarchyNote: None — coordinates (47.4717–47.4722°N, 120.8887–120.8889°W), elevation (~8,630 ft, LIDAR remeasure 8,634.5 ft), Chelan County / Alpine Lakes Wilderness / Stuart Range location, and adjacency to Mount Stuart (0.4 mi W) and Argonaut Peak (1.3 mi E) are all cross-confirmed by Wikipedia, PeakVisor, PeakBagger and WTA. The Stuart-Enchantments parent grouping is geographically correct.

routes.overview: The standard and least technical line up Sherpa Peak: a class 3-4 ridge scramble out of the south basin, over blocky granite and past the landmark Sherpa Balanced Rock, to the summit.
routes.beta: From the south basin near 7,000 ft, a broad gully trending NNE gains the ridge crest just east of the Balanced Rock. From there the route follows the ridge crest westward over blocks and sandy benches, traverses around/below the Balanced Rock (reported as sketchy when snowy or wet), and finishes on exposed but moderate terrain to the summit. Most parties rope up for the balanced-rock traverse even though the rest of the route is a scramble.
routes.rockGrade: Class 3-4 (short 5th-class moves possible near the Balanced Rock traverse)
routes.rappels: 2 rappels down the upper West Ridge to reach the South Gully
objHaz: snow/ice on the Balanced Rock traverse early season
routes.approach: Two common approaches converge on the basin below Sherpa's south side. Fastest: from the Longs Pass Trailhead (end of Forest Road 9737 off North Fork Teanaway Road), hike over Longs Pass (~6,250 ft) and drop into the upper Ingalls Creek drainage, then climb south/east into the basin below Sherpa and Argonaut Peaks (many parties camp near 6,800 ft). Longer alternative: the Ingalls Creek Trail (#1215) from US 97 near Blewett Pass reaches the same basin from the east via 'the long buttress' on Sherpa's south slopes (about 14 miles round trip from that trailhead to the summit).
routes.descent: Two rappels down the West Ridge with some downclimbing to the South Gully.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb and make two rappels along the upper West Ridge to the South Gully, a 3rd-4th class scramble that leads back down into the basin/camp area. From there retrace the ascent route back over Longs Pass, or out the Ingalls Creek Trail.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the Longs Pass Trailhead, hike over Longs Pass and down into Ingalls Creek, then up into the basin below Sherpa's south side; camp near 6,800 ft. Day 2: Climb the East Ridge to the summit, descend via two rappels down the West Ridge and the South Gully back to camp, then hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on most of the East Ridge by reversing the ridge crest back to the ascent gully; above the Balanced Rock traverse, a rappel may be needed to get back off the ridge safely in deteriorating weather.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before leaving camp based on your party's slowest pace and the day's forecast — afternoon thunderstorms are common over the Stuart Range in summer, and the ridge crest and Balanced Rock traverse offer little shelter or fast retreat once committed.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed summer through early fall once the approach gully and ridge are snow-free; the route has also been skied/climbed in winter conditions by well-equipped parties, but that is a much more serious undertaking.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Ingalls Creek/Longs Pass basins; the summit ridge may get intermittent signal. A satellite communicator is recommended for this remote wilderness approach.
gear: rope for the Balanced Rock traverse and rappels
whatToBring: overnight camp gear — most parties camp near 6,800 ft in the south basin
extra water — the basin/ridge is dry above the last reliable creek crossings
watchOut: The traverse around the Balanced Rock can be sketchy when covered in snow or wet rock.
Loose blocks and sandy benches along the ridge crest.
knownHazards: Loose rock along the ridge crest
Exposed, unroped scrambling terrain for much of the route

road.name: North Fork Teanaway Road / Forest Road 9737
road.status: Unpaved gravel for the final ~13 miles to the Longs Pass Trailhead
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 85, take WA-970 east ~7 miles to Teanaway Road; continue ~7.1 miles and bear right onto North Fork Teanaway Road; after ~5.9 miles of unpaved road, follow FR 9737 (staying left at the Beverly Campground fork, right at the Trail #1394/Ingalls Way fork) to the Longs Pass Trailhead, about 22.5 miles total from the WA-970 turnoff.

access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful/National Park Pass required for trailhead parking
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead entering Alpine Lakes Wilderness; this approach is NOT part of the quota-based core Enchantment Permit Area.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / National Park Pass

emergency.county: Chelan County (the Longs Pass/Teanaway trailhead approach begins in Kittitas County)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 non-emergency dispatch (Chelan/Douglas Counties): (509) 663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee NF: (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA: (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Because the trailhead (Kittitas County) and the peak itself (Chelan County) are in different counties, rescue coordination may involve both county sheriff's offices; always call 911 first.

routes.overview: A short, moderate alpine rock ridge linking Sherpa Col — the notch between Sherpa Peak and Mount Stuart — to the summit; a popular technical add-on for parties already up on Mount Stuart's West Ridge.
routes.beta: From Sherpa Col, mostly 3rd-class terrain leads up to the base of the ridge proper. The climbing is mostly 4th to low-5th class on blocky, generally good granite, with a short crux corner near the top (reported 5.5-5.7 depending on which side of an obvious cave/roof feature is climbed) before a short final pitch to the small, exposed summit.
routes.rockGrade: 5.6 (reported as low as 5.5 and as high as 5.7 depending on the exact line taken at the crux corner)
routes.approach: Most parties reach the base of the West Ridge via Sherpa Col, the notch between Mount Stuart and Sherpa Peak — either by climbing Mount Stuart's West Ridge first and then traversing/rappelling over to the col, or by approaching directly from the Ingalls Creek/Goat Pass basin below the Stuart and Sherpa Glaciers up to the col. From the col, easy 3rd-class terrain leads to the start of the ridge proper.
routes.descent: South Gully, a 3rd-4th class scramble.
routes.descentText: Downclimb and/or rappel back toward Sherpa Col, or descend the South Gully (3rd-4th class scramble) into the basin below the peak. Parties report that rappelling and downclimbing off this route can take nearly as long as the ascent.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a short add-on the same day as Mount Stuart's West Ridge: climb Stuart, traverse to Sherpa Col, tag Sherpa's summit via the West Ridge (roughly 3-4 hours round trip from the col), then continue the descent.
routes.bail: Retreat down-ridge to Sherpa Col is generally straightforward on the mostly 3rd-4th class terrain below the crux; above the crux corner, a short rappel may be needed.
routes.turnaround: Because this route is usually climbed as a second objective after Mount Stuart, budget a hard turnaround time that still leaves enough daylight/energy for the full descent off both peaks — afternoon thunderstorms are common over the Stuart Range in summer.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through August, once seasonal snow has cleared off the col and the ridge's ledges; climbed by multiple parties in late June with lingering snow patches nearby and again in mid-to-late July.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the basins below the peak; intermittent signal possible from the ridge/summit. A satellite communicator is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack suffices: single cams from around 0.3-3 inches (doubles in the 0.4-2 inch range are nice to have for the crux), a set of nuts, and a few slings. A single 60m rope is enough for the whole route.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needed outside the crux corner, which takes small-to-medium cams (roughly #2-#3 size).
proTips: At the crux corner, staying left of the obvious cave/roof feature keeps the climbing around 5.5; going right of it is a stouter 5.7 corner.
Commonly combined with Mount Stuart's West Ridge as a two-summit outing rather than climbed alone.
watchOut: The traverse from Mount Stuart's summit over to Sherpa Col involves loose side-hilling and often a rappel — it is more involved than it looks on the map.
Rock quality is generally good but variable; expect some loose blocks.
knownHazards: Exposed summit block and ridge crest with significant fall potential
Afternoon thunderstorm risk typical of the Stuart Range in summer

road.name: North Fork Teanaway Road / Forest Road 9737 (via Longs Pass TH) or Ingalls Creek Trailhead
road.status: Unpaved gravel for the final miles to trailhead

routes.overview: A longer, more serious alpine rock route gaining Sherpa Peak's north ridge above the Sherpa Glacier, with a short technical crux low on the ridge followed by sustained blocky climbing and scrambling to the summit.
routes.beta: The North Ridge rises in a blocky jumble from the last significant trees near ~7,000 ft to the 8,630-foot summit. Per Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide, two pitches of 5th-class climbing are needed to gain the ridge proper (around 7,200 ft) via an initial notch; from there the route alternates pitched climbing, simul-climbing, and short sections of soloing over more solid, blocky rock to a second notch and on to the summit. The route sees infrequent traffic and can be lichen-covered.
routes.rockGrade: 5.7 (two pitches of 5th class to gain the ridge at ~7,200 ft)
routes.face: North Ridge, above the Sherpa Glacier
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead (Icicle Creek Road), hike the Stuart Lake Trail and the Mountaineers Creek climbers' path (passing the Colchuck Lake turnoff) into the Sherpa-Argonaut basin below the Sherpa Glacier — roughly 4 hours, with talus and slide-alder navigation near the end that has caused parties to arrive later than planned.
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the West Ridge/South Gully back into the Sherpa-Argonaut basin; a south-side bailout down into Ingalls Creek and out the Ingalls Creek Trail to US 97 has also been used but adds many hours.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the West Ridge (downclimbing/rappelling) toward Sherpa Col and down the South Gully back to the north-side approach basin. As an alternative/bailout, descending south into Ingalls Creek is possible but leads to a roughly 9-mile hike out to US 97 and is far longer and more strenuous than retracing the approach — one party's descent this way took over 6 hours after an already long climb.
routes.itinerary: Typically a long single push or overnight: Day 1 hike in ~4 hours to the Sherpa-Argonaut basin and begin climbing; the route regularly runs into a second notch/bivy by evening. Day 1-2: continue to the summit and descend, with total car-to-car time reported around 24 hours for one party.
routes.bail: Retreat is easiest by rappelling back down the pitches already climbed to the approach notch; once past the second notch, continuing over the top and descending the West Ridge/South Gully is generally faster than reversing the full ridge.
routes.turnaround: Given the route's length and complex descent, set a conservative turnaround time and be prepared to bivy — parties have taken well over 12 hours car-to-car, and afternoon thunderstorms are common over the Stuart Range in summer.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through August; climbed in late June with snow still present at Sherpa Col (requiring a stove to melt water) and again in late July.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Sherpa-Argonaut basin or on the ridge; a satellite communicator/messenger is strongly recommended given the route's length and remoteness.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack for 5.7 terrain, plus rope(s) sized to the party.
whatToBring: Stove and fuel if snow remains at Sherpa Col for water
Bivy gear — the route regularly runs into a second day
watchOut: The route sees infrequent traffic and can be lichen-covered and loose in spots.
Route-finding on both the approach and the ridge itself is nontrivial; one party's car-to-car time exceeded 24 hours.
Strong winds are common at the notches used as bivy spots on the ridge.
knownHazards: Loose, lichen-covered rock from infrequent ascents
Long, complex approach and descent through talus and brush
Extended time on route increases exposure to afternoon thunderstorms

pitchDetail.notes: Gains the ridge proper from the initial notch near 7,200 ft; the technical crux of the route.
Blocky ridge climbing alternating pitched leads, simul-climbing, and short soloing sections to a second notch, then on to the summit.

routes.corrections: A Mountain Project page titled 'Direct North Ridge' (route/106245725) describes a IV 5.9+, 20-pitch, 2,800 ft route with a first ascent by Fred Beckey/Steve Marts (1963, upper ridge) and Mead Harris/Jay Ossiander (1970, direct variation) with the Gendarme — this is actually the famous North Ridge of neighboring Mount Stuart, NOT this Sherpa Peak North Ridge route. That FA history has not been attributed to this route to avoid conflating the two distinct climbs.

blurb: The Temple is an 8,295-foot granite summit in the Stuart Range, within the core Enchantments basin of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Chelan County), roughly 8 miles southwest of Leavenworth, WA. It sits at the west/upper end of the Enchantment Lakes cirque and is the parent peak of the more famous Prusik Peak, which rises from its west shoulder above Gnome Tarn. The peak is granite, first climbed in 1942 by Fred Beckey, and its easiest line is a Class 3-4 scramble with a short bit of low-5th-class rock near the top.

routes.overview: The standard/easiest way up The Temple: a broad ridge of talus and blocky granite leading from the core Enchantment basin to the summit, with scrambling that steepens to short low-5th-class steps near the top.
routes.beta: Reached from the core Enchantment Lakes zone (via Aasgard Pass from Colchuck Lake, or the longer Snow Lakes approach from the north). Wikipedia's summit-facts summary lists the peak's easiest route as Class 5.3, consistent with a mostly Class 3-4 ridge scramble with a brief harder step; this is the only cataloged route on the peak, so it is presumed to correspond to the South Ridge line, though no source names the ridge explicitly.
routes.approach: From US-2 on the west edge of Leavenworth, turn onto Icicle Creek Road and drive 8.4 miles, then turn left (south) onto Forest Service Road 7601 and follow it 3.7 miles of rough dirt road to the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake trailhead (elev. ~3,400 ft per USFS trailhead numbering #1599/#1599A). Hike to Colchuck Lake (4 mi, ~2,280 ft gain), then climb Aasgard Pass (~0.8 mi, ~2,200 ft gain, steep and hazardous when snow-covered) into the core Enchantment basin to reach the base of The Temple near Prusik Peak.
routes.turnaround: Because the upper ridge has real exposure and a short low-5th-class step, turn around with enough daylight margin to down-climb the ridge and clear any exposed sections before dark; Cascades afternoon thunderstorms are the other common trigger for retreat on this kind of open granite ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in the core Enchantments hiking/climbing season, roughly July through September, once the access road (gated ~November-May) and Aasgard Pass are free of snow; the Forest Service issues overnight camping permits for the Enchantment Permit Area only between May 15 and October 31.
watchOut: Aasgard Pass is a steep, loose 2,200 ft scramble that becomes a serious slide/fall hazard when snow- or ice-covered, which is common even in early summer.
The upper ridge involves real exposure on a short low-5th-class step; a rope/light rack is prudent even though most of the route is scrambling.
knownHazards: Loose talus and blocky granite on the ridge.
Steep, exposed scrambling and a short technical step near the summit.
Afternoon thunderstorms common on exposed granite terrain in the Enchantments in summer.

road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Forest Service Road 7601
road.status: Paved (Icicle Creek Rd) to rough gravel/dirt, sometimes potholed (FS Rd 7601)
road.seasonalGate: FS Rd 7601 is typically gated/closed by snow from around November through May
road.driveNote: From US-2 on the west edge of Leavenworth: Icicle Creek Road 8.4 mi, then FS Rd 7601 for 3.7 mi to the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake trailhead.

climate.typical: Alpine Cascades climate: dry, warm summers (best climbing window) and wet, snowy winters/springs; the wilderness's treeline sits around 6,400 ft, above which the terrain is open alpine rock and talus.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Enchantment Permit Area)

routes.corrections: REQUIRED PRIMARY SOURCES NOT ACCESSIBLE THIS SESSION: mountainproject.com, summitpost.org, and peakbagger.com all returned 403/404/blocked responses to every fetch attempt, and the WebSearch tool had already exhausted its session budget (200/200) before this peak could be researched, so no search-engine fallback worked either (Google/Bing/DuckDuckGo/Ecosia/Mojeek/web.archive.org all failed or returned irrelevant/geo-mismatched content). Data below is sourced only from Wikipedia (The Temple, The Enchantments, Alpine Lakes Wilderness pages), WTA's Colchuck Lake trailhead page, and general USFS Enchantment-permit facts embedded in those pages. Peak identity/coordinates/elevation were cross-verified and match cleanly (Wikipedia DMS 47°29'26"N 120°46'10"W = 47.4906,-120.7694 vs given 47.490433,-120.769569; elevation 8,295 ft matches exactly). Peak FA is sourced (1942, Fred Beckey) but I could NOT confirm the FA followed the South Ridge specifically, so route-level 'fa' is left null per the peak-vs-route FA rule. Route grade '5.3' comes from Wikipedia's 'easiest route: Class 5.3' summit fact, presumed (not confirmed) to be the South Ridge since it is the only cataloged route -- this should be re-verified against MP/SP/PB once those sources are reachable. Approach/road/permit/access details are well-sourced via WTA + Forest Service context but are for the shared Colchuck Lake/Aasgard Pass approach corridor, not Temple-summit-specific. All numeric approach stats (gainFt, lossFt, distKm, maxAngle, pitches, routeFt, timing) were left null rather than estimated. RECOMMEND RE-RUNNING this peak once MP/SP/PB access and WebSearch budget are available, to fill pitch-level detail, rack, descent method, and confirm the 5.3 grade and route-to-FA attribution.

blurb: Mount Adams (12,276 ft) is Washington's second-highest peak and, by volume, one of the largest stratovolcanoes in the Cascade Range, rising in the Mount Adams Wilderness (Gifford Pinchot National Forest) of the southern Washington Cascades near Trout Lake, with its east flank on the Yakama Indian Reservation. Built of andesite and basalt flows over roughly 500,000 years with a modern cone dating to about 10,000 years ago, it carries 12 named glaciers -- more glacial ice than any Cascade volcano except Rainier -- feeding routes from the broad, non-technical South Spur to steep icefall lines like the Adams Glacier. The peak was first climbed in 1854 by Andrew Glenn Aiken, Edward Jay Allen, and Andrew J. Burge, most likely via the North Cleaver/North Ridge line.

objHaz: Loose volcanic rock and scree, especially late season
Crevasses/bergschrund near the summit ice cap late season
routes.overview: Mount Adams' standard non-technical snow/scree route, climbing the broad south-southwest flank from the Cold Springs trailhead to the summit plateau via the Lunch Counter camp and the false summit of Pikers Peak.
routes.beta: The route follows an old road through a burn zone, crosses Morrison Creek, then climbs rocky terrain to the Lunch Counter camping area (~9,300-9,500 ft). From there climbers tackle Pikers Peak, a false summit, before the final push to the true summit cone; the descent follows the same line, often with glissading where safe.
routes.approach: From Trout Lake, drive Forest Road 80 then FR-8040 north past Morrison Creek Campground to the old Cold Springs Camp trailhead (46.1359, -121.4976).
routes.descent: Descend the ascent route; glissade chutes are commonly used where conditions allow.
routes.descentText: Retrace the South Spur back down through the Lunch Counter and burn-zone road to the Cold Springs trailhead. Popular glissade chutes exist on the upper snowfields but require care for rocks and other parties below.
routes.bail: Descend the ascent route at any point; the Lunch Counter (~9,300-9,500 ft) is a reasonable rest/bail point below Pikers Peak.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround based on weather, party energy, and daylight — climbers should be off the upper snowfields before afternoon softening or thunderstorm risk increases, particularly for the glissade descent.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed primarily June through September; as of mid-July 2026 the Forest Service reported snow sparse below 7,500 ft with rock exposed and recommended helmets for loose rock near the summit.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe and crampons or microspikes depending on snow coverage; helmet recommended for loose rock near Pikers Peak.
whatToBring: Mt. Adams Climbing Pass / wilderness permit
Extra water (limited sources above Lunch Counter)
proTips: Climb earlier in the season for firmer snow and less loose scree
The Lunch Counter (~9,300-9,500 ft) is the standard high camp
Pikers Peak is a false summit — budget extra time and energy past it
watchOut: Crowded route on summer weekends
Loose volcanic scree increases as the season progresses and snow melts out
Glissade chutes can be hazardous with rockfall or other climbers below
knownHazards: Loose volcanic rock and scree, especially late season
Glissade-related injuries on descent
Crevasses/bergschrund near the summit ice cap late season

routes.face: Lava Glacier, north side, right of the Adams Glacier
routes.overview: A moderate snow (occasionally alpine-ice) climb up the Lava Glacier on Mount Adams' north side, ascended on the glacier's right side under normal conditions.
routes.beta: Primarily moderate to steep snow, sometimes alpine ice; most of the climb can be simul-climbed and is protectable with pickets and a running belay if needed.
routes.approach: Via the Pacific Crest Trail, starting near the lava field at Devil's Garden.
routes.descent: Descend via the North Cleaver (North Ridge) or the adjacent snowfield.
routes.descentText: The North Cleaver descent crosses very loose and rotten volcanic rock; take care not to release rockfall onto other parties below.
routes.bail: Retreat down the ascent line of the glacier back toward the Pacific Crest Trail; avoid glacier travel in whiteout.
routes.turnaround: Turn around with enough margin to complete the loose-rock North Cleaver descent in daylight and before afternoon snow softening.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring, while snow still bridges crevasses and before the glacier opens up.
routes.detailedRack: Snow anchors (pickets); occasional ice screw for firmer or icy sections.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and a running belay for the moderate-to-steep snow sections.
whatToBring: Rope and crevasse-rescue kit for glacier travel
watchOut: Loose, rotten volcanic rock on the North Cleaver descent — avoid kicking rock onto other parties
Crevasse hazard varies year to year

routes.routeName: Northwest Ridge (North Face of Northwest Ridge)
routes.fa: Givler, LeBlond, McGowan, 1967
objHaz: Crevasses/moats near the Adams Glacier terminus
Loose, unstable rock on the ridge crest itself
Avalanche/sluff terrain on the approach and lower slopes
routes.face: North Face of the Northwest Ridge, climber's right of the Adams Glacier terminus
routes.overview: A moderate alpine ice/snow line (Grade II, AI2-3, PG13) climbing between the Adams Glacier and the crest of the unstable Northwest Ridge, staying climber's right of the Adams Glacier terminus to avoid crevasses and moats.
routes.beta: Gains access below the Adams Glacier to avoid crevasses/moats, then climbs sustained 40-45 degree neve staying left of the loose ridge crest before trending right at higher elevation. A rock band creates a narrow 'pinch' crux that has become more technical as the glacier has receded; lower sections are 40-45 degrees of good neve.
routes.approach: Via the Killen Creek Trail (#113) to the PCT and High Camp/Highline Trail, gaining the slope below the Adams Glacier; Trail Divide Camp is a commonly used high camp.
routes.turnaround: Turn around with margin to clear the 40-45 degree upper slope and rock-band crux before afternoon softening or increasing rockfall/icefall risk.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in June while the rock-band 'pinch' crux is more snowed-in and neve is firmer; it becomes increasingly icy and technical later in the season as the glacier recedes.
routes.detailedRack: Snow pickets, ice screws, and snargs; two ice tools; a few thin pitons can be useful at the rock-band crux.
routes.proNeeds: Running belays with pickets/snargs/screws through the 40-45 degree neve; the rock-band 'pinch' is the technical crux and may warrant a pitch of protection.
proTips: Approach below the Adams Glacier terminus to avoid crevasses and moats
Best conditions are typically mid-June before the rock-band crux ices out
watchOut: The ridge crest itself is loose and unstable — the route deliberately stays off it
The rock-band 'pinch' crux has grown more technical with glacial recession
knownHazards: Crevasses and moats near the Adams Glacier terminus
Avalanche/sluff terrain on approach
Exposure to cliffs above the Adams Glacier

pitchDetail.notes: Sustained moderate snow/ice, frontpointing

Eases toward the ridge crest and summit plateau

routes.fa: A.G. Aiken, Edward J. Allen, Andrew J. Burge, 1854
routes.overview: A non-technical alternative to the South Spur, climbing the North Ridge (North Cleaver) over loose volcanic scree to the summit plateau; likely the line of the 1854 first ascent of the peak.
routes.beta: Straightforward navigation with most obstacles bypassed on either side; after topping the ridge, climbers cross about a mile of summit plateau to reach the true summit.
routes.approach: From Randle, take Cispus Rd (FR-23), then FR-21, FR-56, FR-5603 to FR-2329 (~4 miles of forest road). Hike the Killen Creek Trail 3.5 miles, crossing the PCT, then leave trail for the ridge (compass advised).
routes.descent: Descend the same route.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent line back down the North Ridge/North Cleaver to the Killen Creek Trail; earlier-season snow cover reduces the amount of loose scree encountered underfoot.
routes.bail: Descend the ridge back to the Killen Creek Trail at any point; no technical anchors are needed.
routes.turnaround: Allow enough margin to navigate the summit plateau and loose-scree ridge descent in good visibility and daylight.
routes.bestSeason: Best earlier in the season (June) while snow still covers the loose volcanic scree; late-season ascents involve more loose talus.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe and crampons; helmet recommended for loose rock.
proTips: Climb early season for firmer snow and less loose scree
From the ridge top it's about a 1-mile traverse across the summit plateau to the true summit
watchOut: Very loose volcanic scree, especially late season
Navigation across the summit plateau in poor visibility — carry a compass
knownHazards: Loose volcanic scree/talus underfoot

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Dave Lind, Robert Mulhall, July 1945
objHaz: Loose rock on the North Ridge descent
routes.overview: Mount Adams' signature ice route, climbing the icefall of the Adams Glacier -- the largest glacier on the mountain -- on its north/northwest face, through a maze of seracs between about 9,200 and 11,800 ft.
routes.beta: Ascends generally on the right side of the glacier, staying in the right third for most of the climb; the upper icefall can be turned on either side depending on yearly conditions, leading to a false-summit plateau before the true summit. Most of the moderate snow/ice terrain can be simul-climbed by a competent team.
routes.approach: Via Forest Service roads 23, 21, and 2329 to the Killen Creek Trail (#113), then the Pacific Crest Trail and High Camp Trail.
routes.descent: Descend via the North Ridge (North Cleaver) or the west-side snowfield.
routes.descentText: The North Ridge/North Cleaver descent crosses very loose volcanic rock with rockfall hazard; take care not to release rock onto the glacier or other parties below.
routes.bail: Retreat down climber's right side of the glacier toward the approach trail before committing to the upper icefall.
routes.turnaround: Turn around with enough margin to clear the icefall and loose-rock North Ridge descent before afternoon warming increases serac and rockfall hazard.
routes.bestSeason: Primarily climbed June-July while the icefall is more coherent and crevasse bridges are intact; conditions vary significantly year to year.
routes.detailedRack: Pickets and ice screws; a rope is advisable through the icefall even though much of the moderate terrain can be simul-climbed.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets and ice screws through the icefall; rope recommended for glacier travel and crevasse rescue.
proTips: Stay in the right third of the glacier for most of the climb
The upper icefall can be turned on either side depending on yearly conditions
watchOut: Active seracs in the icefall zone between ~9,200 and 11,800 ft
Loose, rockfall-prone North Ridge descent
Crevasses lower on the glacier
knownHazards: Serac fall in the icefall zone
Crevasses

routes.face: Wilson Glacier Headwall, above/adjacent to Roosevelt Cliff on the east side
routes.overview: One of Mount Adams' most serious and rarely-climbed technical lines, on the headwall of the east-side Wilson Glacier near Roosevelt Cliff; regional sources group it with the neighboring Victory Ridge and Rusk Glacier Headwall as extremely challenging and dangerous.
routes.beta: The Wilson Glacier occupies the mountain's east slopes, within the Yakama Indian Reservation, split by the rock buttress of Roosevelt Cliff, descending from about 10,600 ft to a talus terminus near 7,400 ft; the headwall above it is very rarely climbed.
routes.turnaround: Given the route's seriousness and remoteness, set a conservative turnaround with wide weather and daylight margins.
watchOut: East-side routes lie partly within the Yakama Indian Reservation, which may require separate permission/permits
Described by regional sources as one of the mountain's most dangerous lines
knownHazards: Route sits above Roosevelt Cliff on the east-side Wilson Glacier, grouped by regional sources with the extremely hazardous Victory Ridge and Rusk Glacier Headwall lines

routes.fa: Angus and Moreau, Sept 1957
routes.overview: A moderate snow climb up the Avalanche and White Salmon Glaciers on Mount Adams' west-southwest side, occasionally involving an alpine-ice headwall.
routes.beta: Ascends the glacier on the right side under normal conditions; primarily moderate to steep snow, sometimes with a headwall of alpine ice. Most terrain allows simultaneous climbing with pickets and a running belay.
routes.approach: Via the Pacific Crest Trail or Shorthorn Trail from Morrison Creek Campground, staging at Madcat Meadow or Salt Creek before ascending.
routes.descent: Descend via the south side or an adjacent snowfield.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier's climbing line back toward the approach trail.
routes.turnaround: Turn around with margin to clear any headwall ice and glacier crevasse hazard before afternoon softening.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring, before the glaciers open up and any headwall ice becomes exposed.
routes.detailedRack: Snow and ice anchors (pickets, screws).
routes.proNeeds: Running belays with pickets; ice screws if the headwall carries alpine ice that year.
watchOut: Headwall may carry alpine ice depending on the year

routes.face: Northwest Ridge, south of the Adams Glacier, to the sub-summit "the Pinnacle"
routes.overview: A non-technical ridge scramble that gains the crest of Mount Adams' Northwest Ridge south of the Adams Glacier (commonly around 7,600 ft) and follows it up to the sub-summit known as "the Pinnacle" near 12,000 ft, distinct from the steeper, more technical North Face of the Northwest Ridge route on its flank.
routes.beta: Climbers typically gain the ridge crest south of the Adams Glacier terminus at a good spot around 7,600 ft, avoiding glacier travel above about 8,000 ft where crevasses open up; the ridgeline is then followed essentially all the way to the Pinnacle. Expect sections of loose volcanic talus and boulder fields depending on snowpack.
routes.approach: Reached via the North Ridge approach (Killen Creek Trail) or via the Adams Glacier approach, then trending onto the ridge crest south of the glacier.
routes.bail: Descend back down the ridge crest to the approach trail; avoid dropping onto the adjacent Adams Glacier above ~8,000 ft.
routes.turnaround: Turn around with margin to navigate the loose talus/boulder sections and ridge descent before dark or deteriorating weather.
proTips: Gain the ridge south of the Adams Glacier, around 7,600 ft, to avoid crevasses higher on the glacier
watchOut: Do not go above roughly 8,000 ft on the adjacent Adams Glacier — crevasses open up there
Loose volcanic talus/boulder fields depending on snowpack
knownHazards: Crevasses on the adjacent Adams Glacier above ~8,000 ft
Loose talus/boulders on the ridge

road.driveNote: From Randle, follow Cispus Rd (FR-23), FR-21, FR-56, FR-5603, to FR-2329 and the Killen Creek trailhead.

access.permit: Mt. Adams Climbing Pass, May 1-Sep 30 above 7,000 ft; free wilderness permit below
access.passRequired: Mt. Adams Climbing Pass

emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Adams Ranger District / Gifford Pinchot NF Supervisor's Office, (360) 891-5000
