routes.corrections: SummitPost's dedicated "Northwest Ridge" route page (summitpost.org/northwest-ridge/158220) could not be directly fetched (403); its route-finding guidance was cross-checked via cached search snippets and is consistent with Mountain Project's separate, more technical "North Face of NW Ridge" route description.

blurb: Mount Shuksan (9,131 ft) is a heavily glaciated peak in North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington, standing about 11.6 miles south of the Canadian border. It is composed of Shuksan greenschist, a metamorphosed oceanic basalt roughly 120 million years old, and is ringed by seven named glaciers descending from its summit pyramid. The mountain's first ascent is credited to Asahel Curtis and W. Montelius Price on September 7, 1906, via the Fisher Chimneys line, though C.E. Rusk later attributed a possible earlier 1897 ascent to Joseph Morowits.

routes.overview: A classic moderate rock finish gaining the summit pyramid from a notch at its base, following mostly bomber class-3/4 rock with a few low-5th-class moves on the final pitches.
routes.beta: Most parties reach the base via the Fisher Chimneys or Sulphide Glacier approach, cross the upper glaciers, and scramble around the bergschrund to a notch about 100 ft up the ridge. Rock quality is very good throughout and climbing is described as pleasant; all class-5 moves can be protected with slings and difficulty should not exceed 5.4.
routes.approach: Approach via Fisher Chimneys (Grade III, class 4) or the Sulphide Glacier (Grade II) route, crossing the Curtis/Sulphide glacier system to reach the base of the summit pyramid at a notch roughly 100 ft up the southeast ridge.
routes.descent: Rappel the standard 4th-class descent gully with a 60m rope.
routes.descentText: Descend by rappelling the same 4th-class gully used for the standard pyramid ascent; a full 60-meter rope is needed to reach all belay/rappel stations. Route-finding on the descent can be time-consuming, and many parties climb sections unroped when conditions allow.
routes.bail: Retreat is via down-climbing/rappelling the ridge back to the notch and re-joining the Fisher Chimneys or Sulphide Glacier descent.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that allows a full glacier crossing and descent in daylight; afternoon thunderstorms and softening snow bridges on the glacier crossings are the main reasons to bail early.
routes.bestSeason: Summer through early fall (Jul-Sep) once the approach glaciers are consolidated; early season may require setting new rappel anchors.
routes.rappels: 4th-class gully, full 60m rope needed to reach belay stations
routes.detailedRack: Double-length slings to protect the ridge suffice for experienced parties; optional protection to 1.5 inches (tricams, nuts, small cams) for less-experienced leaders.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly protected by slings over horns/flakes on the ridge; small-to-medium gear optional.
whatToBring: approach glacier gear (harness, picket, crevasse rescue kit)
proTips: Bivy at or near Winnie's Slide to avoid down-climbing the chimneys in the dark
Rock quality on the pyramid is notably good compared to much of the rest of the mountain
watchOut: Route-finding on both approach and descent eats time
Late in the season the rappel anchors may need to be reset
knownHazards: Crevassed glacier crossings on the approach
Rockfall potential on the pyramid and in the chimneys
Exposed downclimbing sections if rappel gear runs short

pitchDetail.notes: Bomber rock with good crack protection
Easier terrain, still solid rock
Low 5th class moves, all protectable with slings

road.driveNote: Access via Lake Ann trailhead off SR 542 near the Mt. Baker Ski Area, or via the Sulphide Glacier/Shannon Ridge trailhead off Baker Lake Road.

climate.typical: Maritime Cascade alpine climate; heavy winter snowpack, drier and more stable July-September.
climate.summer: Warmest, most stable conditions typically July-August.

routes.overview: A moderate glacier ascent/descent on the north side of the peak, most commonly used as a descent route after a north-side climb like the North Face.
routes.beta: Climbers ascend the White Salmon Glacier, navigate crevasses and rock bands, cross Winnie's Slide (40-45 degrees), transition onto the Curtis Glacier, traverse the exposed 'Hell's Highway' snow chute, and access the Sulphide Glacier for the final summit approach (West Ridge 4th class or a 3rd-4th class central gully).
routes.approach: Drive Mount Baker Highway to Glacier, WA; obtain a free backcountry permit at the Park Service ranger station; continue to the White Salmon Lodge pull-off. With adequate snow cover, descend to the White Salmon Creek valley toward the glacier toe; without snow, contour around the clearcut at elevation to avoid brush.
routes.descent: Standard descent route down the White Salmon Glacier itself.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent line down the White Salmon Glacier, crossing Winnie's Slide and Hell's Highway back toward the White Salmon Creek drainage and the trailhead pull-off.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier toward White Salmon Creek at any point before committing to Hell's Highway.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before crossing Hell's Highway or Winnie's Slide if visibility drops or if rockfall/avalanche activity is observed above.
routes.bestSeason: Best with good, consolidated snow cover; poor snow years make the approach brushy and the glacier crossing harder to protect.
objHaz: large avalanches off the active Hanging Glacier
routes.fa: Piley, Richards, Thompson, September 9, 1926
routes.detailedRack: Crampons, ice axe, and crevasse rescue gear; a picket or two useful on Winnie's Slide.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel protection (pickets, screws in late-season ice) rather than rock gear.
whatToBring: navigation aids for whiteout on the glacier
proTips: Frequently used as the descent for North Face or Fisher Chimneys link-ups rather than climbed alone
watchOut: The active Hanging Glacier above produces large avalanches that can sweep the approach terrain
Hell's Highway has real rockfall exposure
knownHazards: Avalanche danger from the Hanging Glacier icefall above
Rockfall in the Hell's Highway chute
Crevasse hazard on both White Salmon and Curtis Glaciers

routes.overview: A long, historic Grade IV alpine rock route on Nooksack Tower, a subsidiary summit on Shuksan's north side, established in 1946 by Fred Beckey and Clifford Schmidtke.
routes.bestSeason: Summer (Jun-Sep) alpine rock conditions typical for the range.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Clifford Schmidtke, 1946
knownHazards: Long, committing alpine rock route on a remote subsidiary peak

routes.overview: A sustained 45-50 degree snow/ice face route on Shuksan's north side, climbing to a shoulder between the Crystal and Hanging Glaciers before finishing to the summit pyramid.
routes.beta: A fun route in prime conditions (snow covering the low brush and creek on approach). The face itself is roughly 45-degree snow, steepening to about 50 degrees near the summit shoulder between the Crystal and Hanging Glaciers. Late-season bergschrund formation may force a left-leaning snow gully variation partway up the face.
routes.approach: Start at the White Salmon Lodge pull-off on Mount Baker Highway near Glacier, WA. With good snow cover, descend northeast to White Salmon Creek toward the glacier toe; without snow, contour around the clearcut at elevation to avoid dense brush and stream crossings, reaching a bivy shoulder at about 5,500 ft below the North Face.
routes.descent: Reverse the route, or descend the White Salmon Glacier (commonly preferred for varied terrain).
routes.descentText: Either down-climb/rappel the North Face itself, or descend via the White Salmon Glacier over Winnie's Slide and Hell's Highway back to the White Salmon Creek approach.
routes.bail: Retreat down the face to the 5,500 ft bivy shoulder is possible before committing to the upper mixed terrain.
routes.turnaround: Avoid the face in warm/unstable conditions given active avalanche release off the adjacent Hanging Glacier; turn around if seracs above show signs of activity.
routes.bestSeason: Best when the approach still has snow cover over the brush (typically Jun-Aug); late-season climbs need extra ice gear for the bergschrund.
objHaz: large avalanches from the active Hanging Glacier
routes.face: North Face, between Crystal and Hanging Glaciers
routes.fa: Fasset, Hanft, Thompson
routes.detailedRack: Crampons, ice axe, crevasse rescue equipment; add a second tool and ice screws for late-season firm conditions; a light rock rack may be needed depending on the chosen summit finish.
routes.proNeeds: Snow/ice protection (pickets, screws) predominant; light rock rack for the summit pyramid finish.
whatToBring: bivy gear for the 5,500 ft shoulder camp
proTips: Climb when snow still covers the low brush/creek on approach for a much easier day
watchOut: Hanging Glacier above releases large avalanches that can sweep the entire face
Late season requires climbing a left-leaning gully to bypass the open bergschrund
knownHazards: Avalanche release from the active Hanging Glacier icefall directly above the route
Bergschrund/crevasse hazard
Steepening ice up to ~50 degrees near the top

routes.overview: A finishing line up the summit pyramid's northeast ridge, typically climbed as the upper portion of a North Face / White Salmon Glacier link-up rather than as a standalone base-to-summit route.
routes.beta: Documented by a 2021 guided ascent that climbed the North Face, then finished via the Northeast Ridge of the summit pyramid, descending via Hell's Highway, Winnie's Slide, and Fisher Chimneys. Detailed grade, gear, and pitch-by-pitch beta are not published in available sources.
routes.approach: Reached via the North Face or White Salmon Glacier approach from the White Salmon Lodge pull-off on Mount Baker Highway, gaining the base of the summit pyramid before finishing up its northeast ridge.
routes.descent: Typically descended via Hell's Highway, Winnie's Slide, and the Fisher Chimneys rather than reversing the ridge.
routes.descentText: Descend south off the summit toward the Sulphide/Curtis Glacier system, cross Hell's Highway, downclimb Winnie's Slide, and descend the Fisher Chimneys to Lake Ann.
routes.bestSeason: Summer (Jun-Aug), matching North Face conditions.
objHaz: exposure high on the summit pyramid
watchOut: Sparsely documented route; expect route-finding on the ridge itself
knownHazards: High alpine exposure on the summit pyramid
Objective hazard from adjacent glaciers on approach

routes.corrections: This route was not found as a distinct entry on Mountain Project's Main Peak route list (which lists North Face, Northwest Rib, and Northwest Couloir as the documented north-side lines); the only corroborating source is a single guide trip report describing it as the finish of a North Face ascent, not a fully independent route. Treat pitch/grade fields as unconfirmed.

routes.overview: A rarely-climbed, very steep icefall route ascending directly beneath the Hanging Glacier on Shuksan's north side, historically associated with the mountain's first technical (north-side) ascent in 1939.
routes.beta: The Hanging Glacier itself is a dramatic north-side icefall dropping over 5,000 vertical feet, far steeper than the gentler Sulphide/Crystal Glaciers on the south side. A documented ascent combined the route with the NW Rib and NW Arete. Full technical description, grade, and gear beta are not available in accessible sources.
watchOut: This route climbs directly beneath a documented active icefall (source of avalanches that threaten the adjacent North Face route)
knownHazards: Serac/icefall collapse hazard from the Hanging Glacier
Steep, sustained ice terrain (over 5,000 ft of relief)

routes.corrections: Very sparse documentation for this as a standalone named route; the Hanging Glacier is well-documented as a geographic feature (Wikipedia) and as an objective hazard cited on the neighboring North Face route, but a full technical route description independent of the North Face was not found in accessible sources. Most route-specific fields left null rather than guessed.

routes.overview: The standard, most moderate route on Mount Shuksan: a long glacier walk-up from the south side to a class-3 rock scramble finish, popular as an introductory glacier/alpine climb.
routes.beta: Climbers camp at established sites around 6,245 ft on the glacier's western flank, then ascend rolling glacier steps north, flattening near 7,600 ft before trending north-northeast toward the summit pyramid. The standard finish is 3rd-class gullies to the summit; an alternative southeast ridge finish offers mostly 4th class with some low 5th class. Grade III overall (per AAI), 5.2 rock, ~30-degree snow/ice.
routes.approach: From the Shannon Ridge trailhead (about 2,500 ft) off Baker Lake Road, hike roughly 5 miles and 4,000 ft of gain via an old logging road, switchbacks through old-growth forest, and a ridge traverse to a high camp around 6,500 ft on the glacier's western flank (5-6 hours).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route down the glacier; downclimb or rappel using existing webbing anchors near the summit rock band.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the class-3 gullies back onto the Sulphide Glacier, using rappel anchors (webbing with rap rings) where present on the rock band, then retrace the glacier and Shannon Ridge trail back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike Shannon Ridge trail to high camp (~6,500 ft) on the Sulphide Glacier. Day 2: summit day - ascend the glacier and class-3 summit gullies (4-5 hrs up), descend to camp. Day 3: hike out.
routes.bail: Descend back down the glacier to the Shannon Ridge trail at any point before the summit rock band.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before starting the summit rock scramble if weather is closing in, since the final section requires exposed 3rd-class climbing and rappelling to reverse.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep; different hazards dominate at different points in the season (soft snow bridges early, bare glacier ice/crevasses late).
routes.rappels: Webbing anchors with rap rings on the summit rock band
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe, crampons (cold-weather/late-season), pickets, crevasse rescue kit; light rock gear/slings for the summit rock band.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel protection (pickets, crevasse rescue system); minimal rock protection for the class-3/4 summit scramble.
proTips: An alternative southeast ridge finish offers better rock (mostly 4th class, some low 5th) instead of the standard 3rd-class gullies
Hazards differ by season, so check current conditions before committing
watchOut: Not a 'tourist route' despite its moderate rating - real glacier and rock hazard exist
Crevasse danger increases later in the season as snow bridges thin
knownHazards: Crevasse fall potential on the glacier
Rockfall on the summit gullies
Exposed rappel/downclimb finish

pitchDetail.notes: Rolling glacier steps, flattens near 7,600 ft
Standard finish
Alternative, better rock finish

routes.overview: The historic first-ascent line and most popular technical route on Shuksan: a chimney system on the Shuksan Arm leading to a multi-glacier crossing and a class-4 summit pyramid finish.
routes.beta: Described by Fred Beckey as 'tortuous,' the route weaves through a steep, exposed chimney system before crossing several glaciers to the summit pyramid. Requires varied skills (scrambling, glacier travel, snow/ice) but route-finding is easier than it first appears - 'don't overthink it.' Most parties take 2-3 days.
routes.approach: Start at the Lake Ann trailhead above the Mt. Baker Ski Area on SR 542. Follow the maintained trail past Lake Ann and through switchbacks to the North Cascades National Park boundary; the trail then gives way to a boot-pack across talus/snow to the base of the chimneys.
routes.descent: Reverse the route, rappelling the chimneys' rap stations to bypass the 4th-class sections.
routes.descentText: Downclimb/rappel back through the Fisher Chimneys using the fixed rap stations to get past the 4th-class sections; some exposed downclimbing remains unavoidable. Watch for the moat at the snowfield crossing near the top of the chimneys.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike to Lake Ann and up to a bivy on the White Salmon Glacier outcrop or the rock rib above. Day 2: climb the chimneys, cross the glaciers, bivy near Winnie's Slide. Day 3: summit and descend.
routes.bail: Retreat down through the chimneys using existing rap stations; possible from most points below the glacier crossing.
routes.turnaround: Bivy at or below Winnie's Slide rather than push through the chimneys in the dark; turn around if the snowfield crossing/moat looks unsafe.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep; late season (when Winnie's Slide turns to ice) requires ice screws and a second tool.
routes.rappels: Multiple rap stations within the chimneys to bypass 4th-class sections
routes.fa: Clarence Fisher, Esther Buswell, Paul Hugdahl, Lars Loveseth, Winnie Spieseke, Harriet Taylor
routes.detailedRack: Basic glacier travel gear (axe, crampons, rope, pickets); add ice screws and a second tool when Winnie's Slide is icy late season.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier and snow protection predominant through the chimneys and Winnie's Slide; minimal rock gear.
proTips: If you find yourself on mossy/lichen-covered rock or heather without a boot-track, you're off route
Bivy options exist above and below Winnie's Slide with water available
watchOut: Route-finding difficulty is commonly reported, especially in the chimneys
Watch for the moat where the maintained trail's snowfield crossing narrows
knownHazards: Rockfall within the confined chimney system
Crevasse/moat hazard on the glacier crossings
Icy Winnie's Slide late season requiring extra ice gear

pitchDetail.notes: Steep, exposed chimney system on the Shuksan Arm
Snow/ice slope, can be firm/icy late season
Final scramble to summit

routes.overview: A sustained mixed snow/ice/rock line up the southwest side of the summit pyramid, first climbed August 16, 1980, combining a lower icefall, a short unprotected 5.0 rock section, and steep ice before finishing on 5th-class rock to the SE Ridge.
routes.beta: Approached from Lake Ann; below the Fisher Chimneys, traverse right (east) onto the Lower Curtis Glacier and cross to its upper SE corner, starting at the base of an icefall. The route climbs 600 ft of steep (45°) snow with vertical ice steps in the lower icefall, 150 ft more steep snow, an unprotected 60 ft 5.0 rock section, 300 ft of steep ice/snow (60-70°), 100 ft of 4th class rock to a ledge, then roughly 1,000 ft of 5th-class climbing to reach the SE Ridge.
routes.approach: From Lake Ann, approach below the Fisher Chimneys, traverse right onto the Lower Curtis Glacier, and cross to its upper southeast corner to reach the base of the route's icefall.
routes.descent: Descend via the Fisher Chimneys route: through the Hourglass, Upper Curtis Glacier, and Shuksan Arm.
routes.descentText: From the SE Ridge / summit pyramid, join and descend the standard Fisher Chimneys line - down through the Hourglass, across the Upper Curtis Glacier, and out via the Shuksan Arm.
routes.bail: Retreat down the icefall/lower snow sections is possible before the 5.0 rock step; above that, continuing to the SE Ridge and descending via Fisher Chimneys is generally more practical than reversing.
routes.turnaround: Roughly an 8-hour round trip is estimated from Lake Ann; turn around with enough margin to descend the Fisher Chimneys chimneys/rappels in daylight.
routes.bestSeason: Summer (Jun-Aug) when the icefall and couloir hold consolidated snow/ice.
objHaz: icefall/serac hazard in the lower couloir
routes.face: SW Face / SW Couloir, Summit Pyramid
routes.fa: August 16, 1980
routes.detailedRack: Minimal rack of nuts with extra-long slings, crampons, a couple of ice screws and pickets, ice axe and a second tool.
routes.proNeeds: Light rack (mostly nuts and long slings) supplemented by ice screws/pickets for the snow and ice sections; the 60 ft 5.0 section is unprotected.
whatToBring: glacier travel gear for the Lower Curtis Glacier traverse
proTips: Estimated as roughly an 8-hour round trip from Lake Ann for a fit, efficient party
watchOut: A 60-foot section of 5.0 rock climbing is unprotected
Icefall terrain in the lower couloir carries serac/rockfall hazard
knownHazards: Icefall/serac hazard in the lower 600 ft
Unprotected 5.0 rock climbing
Sustained steep ice up to 60-70 degrees

routes.overview: A committing north-side alpine ice route crossing beneath Nooksack Tower onto the Price Glacier, weaving through steep snow, alpine ice, and seracs to the base of the summit pyramid.
routes.beta: After crossing beneath the Nooksack Tower to about 6,500 ft, the route steps onto the Price Glacier and climbs steep snow and alpine ice (roughly AI2-AI3), navigating around seracs and slough chutes to a bergschrund near 7,800 ft with multiple line options (rock left, ice middle, mixed right). Above the bergschrund, continue up steep snow/ice to the base of the summit pyramid.
routes.approach: Cross beneath Nooksack Tower to approximately 6,500 ft to reach the toe of the Price Glacier (accessed from the north-side White Salmon Lodge pull-off approach).
routes.descent: Descend via the Fisher Chimneys route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend south via the Fisher Chimneys line - across the upper glaciers, down Winnie's Slide, and through the chimneys' rap stations to Lake Ann.
routes.bail: Below the bergschrund, retreat is possible back down the glacier toward the Nooksack Tower traverse; above it, continuing to the summit and descending Fisher Chimneys is the more common option.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before the bergschrund crux if seracs above show active slough/collapse activity.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-to-late summer (Jul-Aug) when the icefall and bergschrund are climbable; conditions are noted as variable season to season.
routes.face: Price Glacier, north side beneath Nooksack Tower
routes.fa: Beckey, Schwabland, Grantson, 1945
routes.detailedRack: A party climbing in mid-August carried 3 pickets, 1 fluke, 6 ice screws, and a small rock rack, and used nearly all of it.
routes.proNeeds: Snow/ice protection (pickets, screws, flukes) dominant, supplemented by a small rock rack near the bergschrund crux.
proTips: Bring a generous ice rack (screws/pickets) - parties report using nearly all of what they carried
watchOut: Active seracs and slough chutes require continuous route-finding and avoidance
The bergschrund near 7,800 ft is the technical crux with several line options
knownHazards: Serac collapse and slough avalanches off the icefall
Crevasse hazard on the glacier
Committing terrain beneath Nooksack Tower

Rock left, ice middle, or mixed right options
To base of summit pyramid

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (SR 542)
road.driveNote: Drive SR 542 to Glacier, WA, to the White Salmon Lodge pull-off trailhead for the north-side approach.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for overnight climbs
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass at trailhead

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount (360-854-7200)
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA

blurb: Whitehorse Mountain is a steep, glacier-hung peak in the North Cascades just southwest of Darrington, Washington, within the Boulder River Wilderness (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District). Its north face rises roughly 6,000 feet in under two miles, one of the steepest walls of its size in the Cascades, and holds the small Whitehorse (So-Bahli-Ahli) Glacier. The peak was first climbed in 1909 by Nels Bruseth, reportedly via the Northwest Shoulder line that remains the standard route today.

routes.overview: The standard route on Whitehorse Mountain: a long, physical line up the Northwest Shoulder via Lone Tree Pass and High Pass to the Whitehorse Glacier, finishing with an exposed class 3 (sometimes class 4 late-season) scramble to the summit block.
routes.beta: The mountain's easiest and most-traveled route, but it is long (roughly 12 miles / 7,000 ft round trip) and holds real alpine hazards: a brushy lower approach, steep snow slopes, glacier travel with crevasse risk developing from midsummer on, and an exposed rock/moat finish at the summit block. Best done April-June while the approach and glacier are still snow-covered; by July moats and crevasses open up and complicate both ascent and descent.
routes.fa: Nels Bruseth, 1909 (first recorded ascent of the peak; sources describe the Northwest Shoulder as the peak's primary/original route, so this is presumed to be the FA line, though no source explicitly states the historical party's exact line)
routes.rappels: Typically one rappel (~30m, doubled 60m rope) off the summit block using a rock horn or picket anchor; feasible most seasons per trip reports
routes.approach: From I-5 near Everett, take SR 530 east toward Darrington (~23 miles) to a Shell station at Mine Road/387th Ave NW, about 5 miles west of Darrington. Turn south toward Moose Creek and park at the bridge over Moose Creek, about 0.5 miles in (a bridge closure has added roughly 2.6 miles round trip and 400 vertical feet in past seasons - verify current status). Walk the road, staying left at forks, cross Furland Creek, and reach the signed Niederprum Camp trailhead (Trail #953, ~918 ft) about 2 miles from the highway. Follow the Niederprum Trail's switchbacks for about a mile, then continue on a steep, boot-built path gaining roughly 4,000 feet through thinning forest onto snow to Lone Tree Pass (4,973'). Follow the ridge east about half a mile, descend southeast above a small basin, then traverse south beneath cliffs to High Pass (6,032'), which accesses a broad gully leading to the Whitehorse (So-Bahli-Ahli) Glacier below the summit block.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route: down-climb or rappel off the summit block, descend the glacier and gully back to High Pass, then retrace the traverse and ridge to Lone Tree Pass and down the boot path/trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, a single rappel (often off a rock horn or picket anchor, roughly 30m with a 60m rope doubled) gets parties back onto the glacier below the summit block; some seasons allow down-climbing instead. Descend the Whitehorse Glacier and the broad gully back to High Pass, watching for softening snow bridges and the moat that develops between rock and glacier ice as the season progresses. Reverse the traverse below the cliffs and the ridge walk to Lone Tree Pass, then descend the boot path and Niederprum Trail switchbacks to the trailhead. Parties often glissade or plunge-step the snow sections above Lone Tree Pass when conditions allow. Trip reports note the High Pass gully can be hard to relocate on the descent in flat light - mark it on the way up.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done car-to-car in one very long day (~13+ hours per trip reports), starting around 2-3 AM. Some parties instead camp at Lone Tree Pass or High Pass to split the approach and reach the glacier/summit block before the snow softens.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward by reversing the route along the glacier/gully to High Pass and back over Lone Tree Pass to the trail; the crux is the summit block and glacier finish, so turn back before committing to the final gully/glacier traverse if visibility, avalanche conditions, or icefall risk deteriorate.
routes.turnaround: Given the long round-trip time and significant route-finding on the descent (especially relocating the High Pass gully), set a firm turnaround time before starting and stick to it regardless of proximity to the summit; deteriorating visibility or rapidly softening snow on the traverses are hard stop signals.
routes.bestSeason: April through June is the classic window, when the approach and glacier are consolidated snow, minimizing brush and moat exposure. By July, crevasses and the rock/glacier moat below the summit open up, adding real objective hazard; the route becomes progressively looser and more exposed into late summer.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier travel kit (harness, crevasse-rescue gear), 2-4 snow pickets, a 30-60m rope, ice axe, crampons, and 2 small cams for protecting the short rock step near the summit.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly snow/glacier protection (pickets) plus a couple of small cams for the summit rock step; a rope is carried primarily for glacier travel and the summit rappel rather than sustained pitched climbing.
proTips: Camping at Lone Tree Pass or High Pass splits the long approach and lets you reach the glacier before the snow softens.
Note landmarks on the way up for relocating the High Pass gully on the descent, especially in flat light.
Go early season (April-June) to keep the glacier/summit finish as snow rather than dealing with an opened rock-ice moat later in summer.
watchOut: A washed-out or closed bridge over Moose Creek near the trailhead has forced long detours in past seasons - verify current road/bridge status before you go.
The moat between the glacier and the summit rock widens through the season and can become a serious obstacle by mid-to-late summer.
The brushy lower approach and steep boot path to Lone Tree Pass have been described as a 'heinous bushwhack' when snow coverage is thin.
knownHazards: Avalanche danger on the traverses and gullies below High Pass and on the glacier approach.
Crevasses on the Whitehorse (So-Bahli-Ahli) Glacier, generally opening from July onward.
Bergschrund/moat separation between glacier ice and the summit rock in summer/fall.
Rockfall and icefall potential on the steep upper slopes and near the summit block.

pitchDetail.notes: Switchbacking maintained trail for about 1 mile from the trailhead (~918 ft).

pitchDetail.pitch: Boot path to Lone Tree Pass
pitchDetail.notes: Steep, sometimes brushy boot-built path gaining roughly 4,000 ft through thinning forest and onto snow to Lone Tree Pass (4,973').
Ridge walk east, then descend SE above a small basin and traverse south beneath cliffs to High Pass (6,032').
Broad gully accesses the Whitehorse (So-Bahli-Ahli) Glacier; ascend to the summit via snow/ice or exposed class 3-4 rock (~60 ft) depending on season.

road.name: SR 530 to Niederprum Rd / Moose Creek spur
road.status: Paved highway (SR 530) to a Forest Service spur; short road/user path to the trailhead
road.driveNote: The bridge over Moose Creek near the parking area has been reported closed in past seasons, adding roughly 2.6 miles round trip and 400 ft of extra gain - verify current status before the trip.

climate.typical: Wet, snowy maritime North Cascades climate with a narrow good-conditions window; best climbing typically falls April-June before the glacier opens up.
climate.spring: Prime season: consolidated snow/ice covers the glacier and much of the summit block, minimizing rock exposure and moat hazard.
climate.summer: By July, crevasses, a bergschrund, and rock/snow moats open on the Whitehorse Glacier, adding exposed class 3-4 scrambling and rockfall risk.
climate.winter: Winter ascents are possible via Wellman Basin (north face) or Snow Gulch once snow covers the brush, but those lines average 50+ degrees and require full snow/ice climbing gear.

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District
access.closures: A washed-out/closed bridge over Moose Creek near the trailhead (reported in a 2017 trip report) has forced an extra ~2.6 miles round trip and 400 ft of gain - confirm current road/bridge status before approaching.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early alpine start (climbers commonly report leaving around 2-3 AM) given the length of the day

routes.corrections: The peak's given elevation (6,851 ft) is close to but not identical to sourced figures: Wikipedia/USGS lists 6,840+ ft (NGVD29) and other trackers list 6,852 ft; climbing-beta sources consistently cite 6,840 ft (used here as highPointFt). The difference is within normal survey/datum variance, not flagged as an error.

routes.overview: A steeper, more sustained snow/ice variant using the same Northwest Shoulder approach but continuing directly up the Whitehorse Glacier and north-facing slopes to the summit rather than the class 3 rock finish - effectively the peak's glacier/ice testpiece (around AI2).
routes.beta: Follows the same Niederprum-Lone Tree Pass-High Pass approach as the standard Northwest Shoulder route, then climbs up the Whitehorse (So-Bahli-Ahli) Glacier and north-facing snow/ice slopes, steepening to roughly 50 degrees about 30 ft below the summit and hardening to around 55-degree ice in the final 10 feet. Two ice tools and crampons are typical for this finish; a rope and pickets protect the glacier and the steep summit pitch. Best climbed in spring (April-June) when the ice/snow is continuous.
routes.rappels: Rappel feasible from the summit onto the glacier below (rock or picket anchor); reported as a viable descent option in firm conditions
routes.approach: Identical approach to the standard Northwest Shoulder route: from I-5 near Everett, take SR 530 east toward Darrington (~23 miles) to a Shell station near Mine Road/387th Ave NW (5 miles west of Darrington), turn south toward Moose Creek, and park at the bridge (a closure has added ~2.6 mi RT / 400 ft in past seasons). Walk in via the Niederprum Camp trailhead (Trail #953), climb the boot path to Lone Tree Pass (4,973'), continue the ridge and traverse to High Pass (6,032'), and drop into the broad gully accessing the Whitehorse Glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the approach; rappel or down-climb off the summit block onto the glacier, then retrace the gully/High Pass/Lone Tree Pass line back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: A rappel (rock or picket anchor) from the summit regains the glacier below the steep ice step; in some conditions the final section can be down-climbed instead. From there, descend the Whitehorse Glacier and gully to High Pass, watching for the rock/ice moat that widens through the season, then reverse the traverse and ridge to Lone Tree Pass and down to the trailhead via the boot path and Niederprum Trail.
routes.itinerary: A long single day car-to-car, typically with a very early (pre-dawn) start; the technical ice finish adds time over the standard scramble route, so parties often plan for 12-14+ hours.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier and gully to High Pass is generally straightforward if turned around before committing to the steep summit ice step; once on the final steep pitch, downclimbing or a rappel is the way off.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux ice step is right at the top and softens through the day, commit only if snow/ice conditions remain firm; set a turnaround time in advance and retreat via the glacier if the steep section is wet, rockfall-prone, or visibility deteriorates.
routes.bestSeason: Spring (roughly April-June) offers the most continuous snow/ice for this line; as the season progresses the ice step degrades and the surrounding moat/bergschrund opens, increasing objective hazard.
routes.detailedRack: Two ice tools, crampons, a 30-60m rope, several snow pickets for glacier protection and the summit anchor, and a small rack of cams for any exposed rock encountered on the scramble sections.
routes.proNeeds: Snow pickets for glacier travel and the summit anchor are the primary protection; a couple of small cams cover any rock encountered lower on the route.
proTips: Bucket steps kicked by prior parties make the steep summit ice section considerably easier - it's worth checking recent trip reports for current step conditions.
Climb it early season (spring) while the ice step and glacier are still well-bonded snow rather than bare, hardened ice.
The final steepening happens fast (30 ft out from ~35 degrees to 50, then 55 degrees) - be ready to switch to front-pointing near the top.
watchOut: The moat/bergschrund below the summit ice step is a major mid-to-late-season hazard and can make the final section much harder or unsafe.
Steep sections average around 35 degrees with a short crux near 50-55 degrees - not a route to underestimate as 'just a walk-up variant.'
Snow quality on the final pitch varies significantly by time of day; softening ice increases fall risk on the descent.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Whitehorse Glacier from roughly July onward.
Bergschrund/moat separating glacier ice from the summit rock, worsening through the season.
Steep ice (up to ~55 degrees) in the final approach to the summit.
Avalanche potential on the glacier and gully approach earlier in the season.

pitchDetail.notes: Shared with the standard route: Niederprum Trail, boot path to Lone Tree Pass, ridge/traverse to High Pass.
Broad gully from High Pass drops onto the Whitehorse (So-Bahli-Ahli) Glacier.
Sustained steep snow/ice climbing on the glacier and north-facing slopes below the summit.
Final ~10 ft hardens to around 55-degree ice just below the summit; bucket steps ease passage in tracked-out conditions.

road.name: SR 530 to Niederprum Rd / Moose Creek spur
road.status: Paved highway (SR 530) to a Forest Service spur; short road/user path to the trailhead
road.driveNote: The bridge over Moose Creek near the parking area has been reported closed in past seasons, adding roughly 2.6 miles round trip and 400 ft of extra gain - verify current status before the trip.

climate.typical: Wet, snowy maritime North Cascades climate; this ice/snow line depends on a well-bonded, continuous snowpack and is most reliable in spring.
climate.spring: Best window: the glacier and summit ice step are typically continuous, well-bonded snow/ice.
climate.summer: Ice step degrades and the surrounding bergschrund/moat opens by summer, adding both difficulty and objective hazard.
climate.winter: Full winter conditions push this aspect toward sustained 50+ degree snow/ice climbing similar to the Wellman Basin/Snow Gulch winter lines on the peak.

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District
access.closures: Same Moose Creek bridge closure concern as the standard route - verify current status before approaching.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early alpine start (pre-dawn) given the long approach and technical finish

blurb: Forbidden Peak (8,815 ft) is a sharply glaciated granite horn in the North Cascades National Park, rising directly above Boston Basin near Cascade Pass in Skagit County, Washington. Its three converging ridges (West, North, East) and intervening faces give it a reputation as one of the most striking and sought-after alpine rock objectives in the range, with generally solid granite but no easy way off the summit. Fred Beckey's party (with his brother Helmy Beckey, Jim Crooks, Lloyd Anderson, and Dave Lind) made the first ascent via the West Ridge on June 1, 1940, a route later included in "Fifty Classic Climbs of North America."
hierarchyNote: None — the given name, coordinates (48.5115, -121.0579), 8,815 ft elevation, and Boston Basin parent area all check out against Wikipedia, Mountain Project, and WTA, which independently agree on the same figures.

routes.overview: The original 1940 first-ascent line and Forbidden's easiest route: a snow couloir to the ridge crest followed by sustained 3rd-4th class ridge scrambling to a short 5.6 crux step near the top.
routes.beta: Considered the classic moderate line up Forbidden Peak and one of the 'Fifty Classic Climbs of North America.' From Boston Basin, ascend scree and a snowfield to the obvious West Ridge couloir (roughly 40-45 degree snow early/mid-season). Where the couloir steepens, move left over 4th-class rock into a gully and scramble to the notch that marks the start of the ridge proper. In late season, when the couloir is unsafe from melt-out, climb the rocky gully to its left instead (4th to easy 5th class, often loose/wet). From the notch, stay on or near the ridge crest on generally solid 4th and easy 5th class rock, favoring the north (left) side when in doubt, until an obvious steep step two-thirds of the way up forces a move left around a fixed piton for the 5.6 crux, after which easier climbing leads to the summit.
routes.approach: From the Boston Basin high camp, ascend scree slopes and gullies to a snowfield leading directly to the base of the West Ridge couloir.
routes.descent: Downclimb/rappel the West Ridge (couloir or adjacent rock rib), or rappel the East Ledges/NE face.
routes.descentText: Two options are used. (1) Reverse the West Ridge: downclimb and rappel the ascent line back to the notch — a single 60m rope suffices, and parties sometimes cache boots/axe/crampons at the notch on the way up for retrieval; alternatively rappel a rock rib adjacent to the couloir with 5-6 single-rope rappels. (2) Descend via the East Ridge/East Ledges side: roughly five single-rope rappels (~400 ft) down the NE face to a ledge system, then 3rd/4th-class traversing and downclimbing back into Boston Basin. Bergschrunds and moat conditions near the notch/couloir should be checked before committing.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward only while still on or near the couloir/notch below the ridge crest — rappel the couloir or the adjacent rock rib on double-rope rappels. Above the notch, once established on the ridge crest, the terrain is mostly one-way to the summit before a safe descent option presents itself.
routes.season: Jun-Sep (couloir is snow-climbed early/mid-season; late-season parties use the rock gully left of the couloir as it melts out)
routes.fa: Lloyd Anderson, Fred Beckey, Helmy Beckey, Jim Crooks, Dave Lind — June 1, 1940 (also the first ascent of the peak)
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack (cams and nuts to ~2in) plus ice axe and crampons for the approach couloir; a single 60m rope is sufficient for both the climb and the rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Light rack — the crux step is protected by a fixed piton; otherwise mostly 4th/easy 5th class terrain needing minimal gear.
proTips: A single 60m rope is enough for the whole route, both up and on the rappel descent.
Favor the north (left) side of the ridge crest whenever the line is in doubt.
Parties sometimes stash boots, axe, and crampons at the ridge notch on the way up to retrieve on the descent.
watchOut: The crux step two-thirds up the ridge must be passed on its left side near a fixed piton — do not commit to the right.
Late-season couloir melt-out can expose loose, wet rock in the escape gully to its left.
Bergschrund/moat conditions at the base of the couloir/notch change through the season and should be checked before descending that way.
knownHazards: Rockfall and loose rock in the late-season escape gully.
Fickle mountain weather typical of the North Cascades.

pitchDetail.notes: Early/mid-season snow climb to where the couloir steepens.
Move left over rock into a gully to reach the ridge notch; late-season alternative to the couloir.
Sustained scrambling on or near the crest, favor the north side.
Steep step ~2/3 up the ridge, passed on the left near a fixed piton.
Easier climbing to the top.

road.name: Cascade River Road (FS Road 15)
road.status: Open (as of this research); typically opens to the Cascade Pass trailhead parking area by late June, occasionally slipping to early July after a heavy snow year
road.seasonalGate: Closed by snow roughly late fall through late spring; can also close temporarily for washouts/maintenance
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles from Marblemount; paved for the first ~10 miles, then rough gravel with potholes/washboard, narrowing over the final few miles with drop-offs — high-clearance vehicle recommended, no RVs or vehicles over 22 ft.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades / Boston Basin (Cascade Pass area)
climate.typical: Marine-influenced alpine climate; snow-free rock conditions generally mid/late June through September, with fast-changing weather common year-round.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack persists into June; couloirs and approach still largely snow-covered.
climate.summer: Prime climbing season; still expect afternoon thunderstorms and rapid weather changes.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing storm frequency into October.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall and avalanche hazard; road access closed.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park Complex)
access.fees: Backcountry camping permit fee: $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee (day climbing itself is not fee-based).
access.permit: Overnight backcountry permit required year-round for camping in Boston Basin (reserved via Recreation.gov, picked up at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount); day ascents from outside the park do not require a permit.
access.closures: Fixed-anchor moratorium: no new bolts/fixed anchors in park wilderness; No campfires and bear canisters required in the Boston Basin camping zone; group size capped at 12.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from Boston Basin camp to be off the summit and back down before afternoon weather builds.

waypoints.name: Boston Basin / Cascade River Road trailhead

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office dispatch: 360-336-9450 (or 911)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center (Skagit Valley Hospital), 2000 Hospital Drive, Sedro-Woolley, WA — 24-hour Level III ED, ~13 miles from Marblemount, phone 360-856-6021
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent throughout Boston Basin and on the peak; the Wilderness Information Center is the primary trip-planning and permit contact.

routes.overview: A long, scenic, and comparatively solitary glacier approach over the Quien Sabe and Boston glaciers leads to a moderate-angle ridge with the best rock climbing on the peak near its top.
routes.beta: Most parties on Forbidden go for the West or East ridges, leaving the North Ridge quieter despite excellent position. The route itself climbs at a moderate grade with sustained but easy rock and snow on the crest, steepening into some of the peak's best rock climbing around 8,400 ft before a final pitch to the summit.
routes.approach: From the Boston Basin trailhead (~3,200 ft), hike up into the basin (~5,700 ft), then continue to the Quien Sabe Glacier. Climb the glacier's west margin to about 7,500 ft to find a gully leading to an exposed notch with a rappel station, descend into the Boston Glacier, contour northwest past a large rock spur, and reach the Boston-Forbidden col, where bivy sites sit around 7,600 ft — a lengthy but scenic approach with nearly 5,000 ft of elevation gain and glacier travel.
routes.descent: West Ridge (via the Forbidden Couloir notch) or the East Ledges.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend the West Ridge: downclimb/rappel to the Forbidden Couloir notch, then either rappel the couloir itself or descend the adjacent rock buttress (schrund conditions should be checked first). Alternatively, the East Ledges descent involves roughly 400-500 ft of loose, exposed terrain negotiated with rappels and downclimbing.
routes.bail: Retreat from the ridge itself is difficult once committed above the Boston-Forbidden col bivy; the long, glaciated approach also complicates a fast retreat — turning around at or below the col, before committing to the ridge, is the main bail option.
routes.turnaround: Because the North Ridge demands a long glaciated approach and offers no quick way down, set a turnaround based on how much daylight and route-finding margin remains for both the summit ridge and the chosen descent (West Ridge/Forbidden Couloir or East Ledges) — do not push into unfamiliar rappel terrain after dark.
routes.season: Jun-Sep (glacier approach is most straightforward with a stable snow bridge before crevasses open up later in the season)
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Jack Schwabland, Don Wilde — 1952
routes.detailedRack: Crampons, axe, and crevasse-rescue gear for the Quien Sabe/Boston glacier approach; a light alpine rock rack and a 60m rope for the ridge itself; carry ice screws if attempting a late-season NW-Face-of-North-Ridge variation.
routes.proNeeds: Standard glacier travel protection (pickets/screws for crevasse rescue) on approach, light rack on the ridge proper.
whatToBring: Bivy gear (most parties camp at the Boston-Forbidden col)
proTips: Expect solitude relative to the West and East ridges — most parties choose those instead.
watchOut: Moat conditions can require creative route-finding to access the Boston Glacier.
Check bergschrund/schrund conditions on the Forbidden Couloir before committing to that descent.
knownHazards: Glacier travel and crevasse hazard on the Quien Sabe and Boston glaciers.
Exposed notch/rappel station between the two glaciers.

pitchDetail.notes: Quien Sabe Glacier west margin to ~7,500 ft, then an exposed notch/rappel into the Boston Glacier.
Contour past a rock spur to bivy sites around 7,600 ft.
From the bivy, a short steep left-facing corner leads to a level section of ridge.
Steepens near 8,400 ft with the route's best rock climbing.
Final pitch to the summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road (FS Road 15)
road.status: Open (as of this research); typically opens by late June
road.seasonalGate: Closed by snow roughly late fall through late spring
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles from Marblemount; paved to mile ~10, then gravel/washboard to the end; high-clearance recommended.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades / Boston Basin (Cascade Pass area)
climate.typical: Marine-influenced alpine climate; best glacier/rock conditions mid-summer.
climate.spring: Snow bridges more solid but approach mostly under snow.
climate.summer: Prime season; glaciers open up with more visible crevasses as summer progresses.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows.
climate.winter: Heavy snow, avalanche hazard, road closed.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park Complex)
access.fees: Backcountry permit fee: $10/person + $6 reservation fee for overnight camping.
access.permit: Overnight wilderness permit required for the Boston-Forbidden col bivy (reserve via Recreation.gov, pick up at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center).
access.closures: Fixed-anchor moratorium in park wilderness; bear canister required for camping in the area.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early alpine start given the long glaciated approach and committing descent options.

waypoints.name: Boston Basin / Cascade River Road trailhead

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office dispatch: 360-336-9450 (or 911)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center (Skagit Valley Hospital), Sedro-Woolley, WA — 360-856-6021
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage on the glacier approach or ridge; this is a remote, low-traffic line relative to the other Forbidden routes.

routes.overview: Widely regarded as Forbidden's best route: a sustained, steep face line up the tallest and hardest-to-reach part of the peak, climbing a knife-edge and chimney system to the summit.
routes.beta: Described by Fred Beckey himself as 'an exceptional climb of purity' and by climber Blake Herrington as 'Forbidden's best route.' It is the tallest, most consistently steep, and hardest-to-reach of the peak's established lines. From the toe of the face, climb steep, somewhat loose 4th-to-low-5th-class terrain for roughly 500 ft to a knife-edge, cross it at 5.6, then pull the 5.8 crux via stemming and crack climbing while staying on the ridge/face line. Where the ridge merges with the face, ascend a wide, steepening chimney, then continue on low-5th to 5.6 terrain to the summit.
routes.approach: Two options from Boston Basin: (1) via Sharkfin Col and the Boston Glacier around the North Ridge — about 6-7 hours, less hazardous, glacier-roped travel; or (2) via the West Ridge notch — about 4 hours, more technically demanding.
routes.descent: West Ridge or East Ledges.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once above the initial 500 ft of face terrain; the knife-edge and chimney sections are best reversed only in the lower portion of the route.
routes.season: Jul-Sep (a clean rock route needing snow-free/dry conditions on the face)
routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Ed Cooper — July 15, 1959
routes.detailedRack: A double rack from about .3 to 2 inches, a single set of nuts, plus a light axe and crampons for the glacier approach option.
routes.proNeeds: Solid gear placements throughout; the crux stemming/crack section wants a fuller cam selection.
whatToBring: Helmet (loose rock in the lower 500 ft)
Rope for the glacier approach variant
proTips: The West Ridge notch approach (~4h) is faster but harder than the Sharkfin Col/Boston Glacier approach (~6-7h), which is less hazardous.
watchOut: The first ~500 ft of 4th/low-5th terrain is steep and somewhat loose.
The knife-edge section demands careful footwork before the 5.8 crux.
knownHazards: Loose rock low on the face.
Long, committing approach regardless of which variant is chosen.

pitchDetail.notes: ~500 ft, steep and somewhat loose, to the knife-edge.
Exposed traverse/crossing.
Stemming and crack climbing, staying on the ridge line.
Wide chimney where the ridge merges with the face, steepening rock.
Continues to the top.

road.name: Cascade River Road (FS Road 15)
road.status: Open (as of this research); typically opens by late June
road.seasonalGate: Closed by snow roughly late fall through late spring

routes.overview: Documented on Mountain Project as the 'East Ridge Direct': an easily accessible alpine rock climb over a series of gendarmes on excellent granite, finishing with an exposed 5.8- crux.
routes.beta: Sources document this line specifically as the 'East Ridge Direct.' From a cached-gear bivy near a ridge notch, climb several low-5th-class pitches over multiple gendarmes, including a knife-edge section; the crux pitch (5.8-) has 'awesome holds' and serious exposure. The third gendarme can be bypassed on its north side if desired. Rock quality is excellent throughout, with abundant natural anchors and existing rappel webbing.
routes.approach: From Boston Basin camp, head north toward Forbidden's south face to reach a ridge notch; pass left around the first gendarme to a bivy spot where climbing gear is typically cached. Roughly 4-5 hours from the car (i.e., including the Boston Basin approach).
routes.descent: Rappel the north side of the ridge (about 5 rappels with traversing), then simul-climb/scramble back to the bivy to retrieve cached gear.
routes.descentText: From the summit, rappel the north side with some traversing over roughly 5 rappels, then simul-climb or scramble back to the bivy site below the first gendarme to pick up cached gear before continuing down to Boston Basin.
routes.bail: The lower gendarmes below the crux can generally be reversed/rappelled using the existing fixed webbing found on the route; above the crux, retreat becomes more involved.
routes.season: Jul-Sep (a clean rock ridge needing dry conditions)
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Joe Hieb, Ed Cooper, Don Claunch — 1958
routes.detailedRack: A set of cams, half a set of nuts (favoring small sizes), and lots of slings — abundant natural anchors and existing webbing reduce gear needs.
routes.proNeeds: Light-to-moderate rack; the route is well-served by natural gear and fixed webbing left by prior parties.
whatToBring: Rope for glacier/approach travel if needed
proTips: The route is described as an easily accessible alpine climb, roughly 4-5 hours from the car including the Boston Basin approach.
The third gendarme can be bypassed on its north side.
watchOut: Knife-edge section on one of the gendarmes demands careful, exposed climbing.
Multiple gendarmes must be negotiated before the crux pitch.
knownHazards: Sustained exposure along the gendarme traverse.

pitchDetail.notes: Several pitches over multiple gendarmes from the bivy.
Exposed narrow section.
Can be bypassed on the north side.
Awesome holds, serious exposure.

routes.overview: Also known as the Northeast Buttress or 'NE Ledge,' a comparatively loose and serious line on the peak's northeast side, historically climbed less often than the three main ridges.
routes.beta: First climbed as the 'Northeast Buttress' (Grade III or IV, 5.7 or 5.8) by Fred Beckey, Ron Burgner, and Tom Nephew in 1972. Sources describe the face/route as 'loose and unsavory' compared to Forbidden's other lines, with more sustained technical climbing (5.7-5.8) than the standard ridges. The route has a documented history of danger, including a fatal fall on a traverse in 1975.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Ron Burgner, Tom Nephew — 1972
watchOut: Rock quality is notably looser and less trustworthy than Forbidden's other established routes.
A documented fatal fall occurred on a traverse on this route in 1975 — treat exposed traversing sections with particular caution.
knownHazards: Loose rock throughout much of the face.
History of a fatal accident on a traverse section.

routes.corrections: This route is documented in sources primarily as the 'Northeast Buttress' (also called 'NE Ledge'); kept the given routeName 'Northeast Face' per instructions since sources treat them as the same line.

routes.overview: Forbidden Peak's standard descent line, used by most parties who summit via the West Ridge, North Ridge, East Ridge Direct, or Northwest Face; it is rarely, if ever, used as an ascent route.
routes.beta: Consistently described across multiple ascent-route descriptions (West Ridge, North Ridge, Northwest Face) as the alternate descent off Forbidden's east side: roughly 400-500 ft of loose, exposed terrain negotiated with about 5 single-rope rappels down the NE face/ledge system to a ledge, followed by 3rd/4th-class downclimbing and traversing back into Boston Basin. Sources note there is 'no easy way off Forbidden Peak,' and this descent in particular is loose and confusing to route-find.
routes.descent: Rappel/downclimb the East Ledges system on the peak's east side.
routes.descentText: From the summit area, make roughly 5 single-rope rappels (about 400-500 ft total) down the NE face to reach a broad ledge system, then negotiate 3rd/4th-class terrain — traversing and downclimbing over loose rock with significant exposure — back down into Boston Basin.
routes.bail: This is itself the standard bail/descent route off the peak from several of the ascent lines; once committed to the rappels, continuing down is generally the only option.
routes.face: East Ledges (east side descent gully/ledge system)
watchOut: Loose blocks and confusing route-finding through the ledge system.
Rope-stuck and rockfall-from-above hazards are commonly reported on this descent given how many parties funnel through it.
knownHazards: Loose rock and significant exposure throughout the ~400-500 ft of rappelling/downclimbing.
Described across multiple sources as one of the most difficult, unpleasant descents in the Cascades.
whatToBring: Extra rappel webbing/rings in case existing anchors need backing up
Helmet for rockfall from parties above

routes.corrections: This is documented as a descent route rather than an independent ascent line; SummitPost lists it as a distinct named route, consistent with the given data, but no source describes an ascent-specific grade/pitch count for it.

blurb: Mount Index is a dramatic, near-vertical rock peak in the central Cascades directly above the town of Index and the Skykomish River (Highway 2), rising over 4,000 feet above its base in barely a mile. It has three summits — North, Middle, and the true (Main/South) summit at 5,991 ft — composed largely of steep, often loose gneiss, and its northeast-facing Norwegian Buttresses hold some of the longest, most improbable alpine rock climbing in the Cascades. The main summit was first reached October 29, 1911 (H. B. Hinman, Ernest Martin, Lee Pickett, George E. Wright); the North Peak followed in 1929 via its North Face (Lionel Chute, Victor Kaartinen).

routes.overview: A 2,000-foot, multi-pitch alpine rock wall on Mount Index's northeast side above Lake Serene — one of the biggest and most improbable free climbs in the Cascades, now best known via the modern free line 'Bluebell'.
routes.beta: The North Norwegian Buttress hosts several distinct lines up the same 2,000-foot wall of steep gneiss. The wall's original ascent was an unrepeated solo aid line by Pete Doorish in the 1980s. In 2020, Sam Boyce, Lani Chapko, and Kyle Willis added 'Jötnar' (2,000ft, 16 pitches, VI 5.9 A3+) roughly 100 yards left of Doorish's line, an aid route with sustained hard nailing and a seasonal waterfall low on the route. In 2023, Nathan Hadley, Michal Rynkiewicz, and Stamati Anagnostou spent 25+ days establishing and freeing 'Bluebell' (5.13-, ~2,000ft, Mountain Project lists 17 pitches; the first ascensionists' own account describes 21), the first entirely free route up the buttress and one of the steepest long free climbs in North America. Bluebell moves through named sections — the Toe (lower-angle scrambling), the Shield, the Roofs/'3D Dike', a Slab, a sustained overhanging Headwall (the crux, pitches roughly 12-15, with a boulder-problem-hard 'Big Overhang' section going V5-V6), and easier Summit pitches with some loose rock on ledges.
routes.fa: First free ascent ('Bluebell'): Nathan Hadley, Aug 11, 2023 (solo/first free ascent); freed again by Michal Rynkiewicz and Stamati Anagnostou, Aug 21-22, 2023; route developed May-Aug 2023. The buttress's original, harder aid line was soloed by Pete Doorish in the 1980s and has never been repeated.
routes.rappels: Single 70m rope; every belay is bolted and rap-equipped, with dedicated rap-line anchors that let parties skip some belays for a faster single-rope descent, especially through the Headwall.
objHaz: Loose rock/rockfall, worse during spring snowmelt
Seasonal waterfall low on the route (wetter early season)
Multiple parties climbing the same wall - communication needed
routes.approach: Approach via the Lake Serene Trail from the Mount Index trailhead off US Highway 2 (Northwest Forest Pass required), then leave the trail to reach the base of the buttress. The route itself starts on the lowest part of the buttress, about 200 feet left of an old abandoned fixed line.
routes.descent: Rappel the route on a single 70m rope, using the dedicated bolted rap anchors at each belay.
routes.descentText: Every belay on Bluebell is bolted and equipped for rappelling, so retreat or descent is possible from essentially any point on the route. For an efficient single-70m-rope descent, parties skip certain intermediate belays in favor of dedicated rap-line anchors marked on the topo, particularly through the Headwall pitches.
routes.itinerary: The first free ascent (Nathan Hadley) was done in a single long day on Aug 11, 2023; a fit, efficient party can expect a similarly long single-day push, while less experienced or slower parties should plan for a full day dedicated to the climb with an early start, plus several hours for the rappel descent.
routes.bail: Every belay station on the route is bolted and rap-equipped, so a party can bail from virtually any point by rappelling the line of ascent back down.
routes.turnaround: This is a long (2,000ft/17-pitch), sustained wall route with real overhead rockfall exposure - set a firm turnaround time based on daylight and party speed. Retreat itself is low-risk (every anchor is rappel-ready), but afternoon weather and snowmelt-driven rockfall in the surrounding gullies are real considerations.
routes.bestSeason: The route was developed and freed between May and August 2023; its lower pitches cross a seasonal water streak that runs heaviest during spring/early-summer snowmelt and dries out as the season progresses, so late-summer conditions (as when the route was freed, in August) are considered the cleanest window.
routes.detailedRack: The route is fully bolted (sport/alpine style) - bring 16 quickdraws (a mix of standard length plus a few extended 2-4-alpine draws for rope-drag management through the roofs) and a single 70m rope for the rappel descent. No trad rack is needed.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted; protection needs are limited to quickdraws rather than a traditional rack.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the lower-angle 'Toe' scrambling
Plenty of water - long, sun-exposed wall
Light bivy gear if not climbing it car-to-car in a day
proTips: Climb later in the season (as in August) once the seasonal water streak low on the route has dried out
Bring a stick clip for reaching bolts through the steep, overhanging Headwall roofs
Use the marked dedicated rap-line anchors (not every belay) to speed up the single-rope descent
watchOut: Early-season parties may hit a seasonal waterfall low on the route
Loose blocks remain in places despite extensive cleaning during development
The 'Big Overhang' boulder-problem pitches (roughly P12-13) climb at V5-V6 - stiffer than the route grade suggests
knownHazards: Loose rock and rockfall are ongoing concerns on the wall, worse with multiple parties above/below each other.
The lower pitches cross a seasonal water streak that can be wet and cold in spring/early summer.
Natural rockfall from upper sections and surrounding gullies is possible, particularly during snowmelt.

pitchDetail.notes: Lower-quality scrambling/easier rock to gain the wall proper.
Steep, sustained climbing begins.
3D, steep roof climbing with boulder-problem-hard sections.
Technical face/slab climbing between the steeper bands.
Sustained, continuous overhanging terrain - the route's crux section; stick-clip or 'rodeo-clip' some bolts.
Easier climbing to top out, but with loose rock on ledges near the top.

climate.winter: Heavy wet snowfall; not a rock-climbing season on this wall.

routes.corrections: The routeId/name 'North Norwegian Buttress' names the wall/formation, which carries at least three distinct lines: an unrepeated 1980s solo aid line by Pete Doorish, 'Jötnar' (VI 5.9 A3+, Boyce/Chapko/Willis, 2020), and 'Bluebell' (5.13-, the first free route up the wall, Hadley/Rynkiewicz/Anagnostou, 2023). The data above follows Bluebell, which Mountain Project catalogs as the buttress's featured/classic route. Mountain Project's own route page lists 17 pitches; the first ascensionists' AAC account describes 21 pitches for the same route - Mountain Project's figure is used as the primary stat.

routes.overview: A historic, committing ridge traverse linking Mount Index's North, Middle, and Main (true) summits - Mountain Project catalogs it as 'Traverse of Mount Index'. From Lake Serene it climbs the North Peak's skyline, then follows loose, exposed 4th/low-5th-class ridge terrain south to the true summit.
routes.beta: First climbed by Pete Schoening and Fred Beckey in August 1950 (in tennis shoes, over two nights, reaching the North Peak by noon on day one), this route/traverse links all three of Mount Index's summits along the connecting skyline ridge. Modern parties typically complete the full North-Middle-Main traverse in a single long push or one bivy rather than two nights. Mountain Project rates it 5.7+ overall across 4 roped pitches within a ~5,000-foot line, with the rest being sustained scrambling and simul-climbing on loose rock.
routes.fa: Pete Schoening and Fred Beckey, August 1950 (first recorded traverse of Mount Index's upper wall/summits).
routes.rappels: A handful (or two) of rappels' worth of tat/cord for anchors, per Mountain Project - carry material for building rap anchors rather than relying on fixed gear.
objHaz: Loose rock across the whole traverse
Very limited retreat once committed past the North Peak
routes.face: North side approach and connecting ridge over the North, Middle, and Main summits
routes.approach: Hike the Lake Serene Trail from the Mount Index trailhead off US Highway 2 (Northwest Forest Pass required) to Lake Serene (roughly 4.1 miles one-way, ~2,000ft gain to the lake at ~2,521ft), then leave the trail and climb the skyline from right to left as viewed from the lake, gaining the North Peak.
routes.descent: No independent descent line is documented; parties reverse/rappel the traverse using tat/cord anchors, per Mountain Project's noted rack of 'a handful or two of rappels worth of tat.'
routes.descentText: Mountain Project's own beta for the traverse calls for 'a single alpine rack and a handful or two of rappels worth of tat if comfortable at the grade,' implying descent/retreat is via a series of short rappels off tat or natural anchors rather than a walk-off.
routes.itinerary: Originally a two-night traverse (Schoening/Beckey, Aug 1950), reaching the North Peak by noon of day one; modern parties commonly complete the full North-Middle-Main traverse in a single (long) day or with one bivy.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward back down the approach gully before committing past the North Peak. Once past the North Peak onto the connecting ridge, there is no easy independent bail route - retreat means rappelling back the way you came using tat/natural anchors.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, loose, and serious multi-summit ridge traverse with very limited bail options once past the North Peak. Set a firm turnaround time that accounts for completing the entire traverse plus descent - not just reaching the first summit - and be prepared to rappel back or bivy if benighted.
routes.bestSeason: Mountain Project notes the tradeoff directly: earlier in the season there's more snow underfoot but higher rockfall risk from freeze-thaw, while later in the season the route is drier with less snow/ice but more exposed loose rock and less available water. Described as 'quite the serious endeavor' regardless of timing.
routes.detailedRack: A single, light alpine rack (small-to-medium cams and nuts) plus a couple of alpine draws, and a handful of rappel tat/cord for anchors; two ropes are useful for the retreat/descent rappels.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly moderate 4th/low-5th-class terrain broken by short 5.7 rock steps, so protection needs are light - a single alpine rack is sufficient per Mountain Project.
whatToBring: Light bivy gear if doing it as an overnight, as in the original 1950 ascent
Extra water - little available once off snow
proTips: Start at Lake Serene and climb the skyline right to left as seen from the lake
Most modern parties compress the historic two-night first ascent into a single long day or one bivy
Carry tat/cord rather than expecting fixed rap anchors
watchOut: Loose rock throughout is the defining hazard of this traverse
Once past the North Peak, retreat options are very limited
Route-finding across three summits over loose terrain is serious and sustained
knownHazards: Loose rock is present across the entire traverse.
Snow and water conditions on route vary significantly by season.
Overhead rockfall hazard exists along the ridge.
The traverse is a serious, committing undertaking regardless of season, per Mountain Project.

climate.winter: Heavy wet snowfall; this becomes a snow/mountaineering objective (e.g. Hourglass Gully) rather than a rock traverse.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists the 'Main Peak' sub-area elevation as 5,916 ft and the 'North Peak' area as 5,914 ft; Wikipedia and the elevation already on file for this peak (5,991 ft) are better corroborated (two independent sources agree) and are retained as the summit elevation/highPointFt for this traverse. Mountain Project's own name for this climb is 'Traverse of Mount Index'; the given routeName 'Main Peak via North Approach' is kept as provided but describes the same route.

blurb: Three Fingers is a serrated, glacier-carved summit of the western North Cascades in Snohomish County, rising above the Squire Creek and Boulder River valleys within the Boulder River Wilderness. Its resistant volcanic/metamorphic rock forms three distinct rock horns (North, Middle, and South Peak); the South Peak, the highest at roughly 6,854-6,865 ft, carries a fire lookout that required blasting the summit block and is now on the National Register of Historic Places. Harry Bedal and Darrington district ranger Harold Engles made the first ascent of the peak in 1929 while scouting a lookout site.
hierarchyNote: None — the given name, coordinates (48.169895, -121.687865), and elevation (6,865 ft) all match the real Three Fingers in the North Cascades near Darrington/Granite Falls, WA (Peakbagger pid=1791: 6,865.4 ft), and "Darrington and Mountain Loop Hwy" is the correct parent area/access corridor.

routes.overview: The standard, non-technical-but-exposed route to Three Fingers' true summit: a long trail approach to Goat Flats and Tin Can Gap, a glacier-edge traverse, and a final scramble up three exposed ladders bolted/anchored into the summit block to reach the historic fire lookout.
routes.beta: This is the line Harry Bedal, Harold Engles, and Frank Benesh used to reach the South Peak summit to build the fire lookout in the early 1930s, and it remains the standard way up. From the trailhead the route follows trail to Saddle Lake, Goat Flats, and Tin Can Gap, then crosses the edge of the Queest-Alb Glacier before a rock scramble and a set of three fixed ladders (rebar and timber anchored into steep rock) gain the summit block and lookout. It is popular as a long day trip or, more commonly, an overnight from Goat Flats or Tin Can Gap given the approach length.
routes.approach: From the Verlot Public Service Center (about 11 miles east of Granite Falls), drive west on the Mountain Loop Highway about 3.9 miles, then turn onto Tupso Pass Road (FS Road 41) and follow it uphill; continue onto FS Road 4160 toward the historic Saddle Lake/Goat Flats trailhead. Road 41 is washed out about 4.8 miles in and permanently closed to vehicles at the Canyon Creek bridge (about 8 miles from the historic trailhead), so present-day parties must walk or bike roughly 9 additional miles of closed road before reaching the actual trail. From the true trailhead, hike Trail 641 to Saddle Lake, then climb through subalpine meadows to Goat Flats and on to Tin Can Gap, the last reliable camp/rest point before committing to the glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down-climb the three ladders, re-cross the Queest-Alb Glacier edge back to Tin Can Gap, and retrace the trail through Goat Flats and Saddle Lake to the trailhead (then the long closed-road walk/bike-out).
routes.descentText: There is no separate descent line — parties down-climb the same ladders and re-cross the same glacier-edge traverse used on the way up, then follow the Goat Flats/Saddle Lake trail back to the historic trailhead. Because the access road is closed well short of the old trailhead, the "descent" for current parties also includes a long walk or mountain-bike-out on the closed FS Road 41/4160 roadbed, which adds meaningfully to the total day.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike/bike the closed road and trail in to camp at Goat Flats or the more exposed but higher Tin Can Gap. Day 2: cross the Queest-Alb Glacier edge, climb the summit ladders to the lookout and back to camp, then hike out (parties in good shape sometimes combine days 1-2 into a single long day, roughly 4-6 hours up from the true trailhead per trip reports, plus the road approach).
routes.bail: The route is reversible from essentially any point — Tin Can Gap and the glacier edge are the last natural bail-out points before the ladders; if weather, visibility, or glacier conditions deteriorate, retreat the way you came rather than pressing on to the ladders.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that gets the party off the glacier-edge traverse and down the exposed summit ladders before afternoon cloud-buildup or thunderstorms reduce visibility, and that still leaves daylight for the long closed-road walk-out.
routes.bestSeason: Best from mid- to late summer into early fall, once the Queest-Alb Glacier edge has consolidated and the approach trail has mostly melted out; snow and ice can linger on the upper mountain well into summer, and an ice axe is recommended year-round even on the "standard" route.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Squire Creek/Goat Flats/Tin Can Gap basin; there have been multi-day, multi-agency SAR operations here (a 32-hour Snohomish County SAR rescue of stranded hikers in June 2023), so a satellite messenger/PLB is worth carrying.
whatToBring: Overnight camping gear (most parties camp at Goat Flats or Tin Can Gap)
Extra layers for exposed ridge/summit weather
Water treatment (limited water past Goat Flats)
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard line in normal conditions; carry a glacier-travel kit (ice axe, crampons) and consider a short rope/harness for less-experienced members on the exposed ladder pitches and glacier-edge traverse.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal on this line — the crux is exposure and glacier judgment rather than technical rock climbing; some parties rope up for the glacier-edge crossing and the ladder section.
proTips: Camp at Goat Flats or Tin Can Gap to split the long approach into two days rather than a single huge push
Confirm the current washout status of FS Road 41/4160 before you go — the drive-to trailhead no longer exists and the walk/bike-in adds ~9 miles each way
Go later in the season (Aug-Sept) for a more consolidated, safer glacier edge
watchOut: The permanent road washout adds a long road walk/bike well beyond the old trailhead mileage quoted in older guidebooks
The three summit ladders are exposed with real fall consequences — rope up less experienced parties
The Queest-Alb Glacier edge crossing requires genuine snow/crevasse judgment, not just hiking skill, and has been the site of serious SAR incidents
knownHazards: Crevasse and glacier fall hazard crossing the Queest-Alb Glacier edge
Exposed, committing ladder climbing on the summit block
Persistent snow/ice on the approach well into summer
Long, isolated approach with no cell coverage
Fragile subalpine/alpine vegetation off-trail
routes.rappels: None typically — parties down-climb the ladders and glacier edge rather than rappel
routes.face: Standard/Lookout route: Goat Flats - Tin Can Gap - Queest-Alb Glacier edge - summit ladders
routes.fa: Harry Bedal, Harold Engles & Frank Benesh, c. 1929-1933 (route used to construct the fire lookout)

pitchDetail.notes: Long forest/subalpine trail; the modern approach adds a closed-road walk/bike before the historic trailhead.

pitchDetail.pitch: Goat Flats to Tin Can Gap
pitchDetail.notes: Meadow and talus trail to the last camp before the glacier.

pitchDetail.pitch: Tin Can Gap across the Queest-Alb Glacier edge

Three fixed ladders (rebar/timber anchored) gain the blasted summit block and L-4 lookout cabin at ~6,850 ft.

climate.typical: Wet, cloud-prone western Cascades climate; snow and ice can linger on the Queest-Alb Glacier and summit approach well into summer.
climate.spring: Deep, unstable snowpack with avalanche/cornice hazard on approach slopes.
climate.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep) once the glacier edge has consolidated; afternoon thunderstorm buildup is common.
climate.fall: Often clear and stable into September, but shorter days and possible early-season snow.
climate.winter: Full winter mountaineering conditions; the standard route is not a winter objective.
