routes.overview: The standard technical line up Three Fingers' North Peak (~6,870 ft), reached via the same Goat Flats-Tin Can Gap-Camp Saddle approach as the South Peak lookout, then a West Couloir into a South Face finish involving one short 5th-class chimney move.
routes.beta: Parties typically combine this with the South Peak lookout climb in a single trip since both share the same long approach. From Camp Saddle/Tin Can Gap, descend onto the glacier and traverse to the base of the North Peak, then climb the West Couloir to the South Face. One trip report describes the climbing as 'Class 4 with one Class 5 move in the chimney, then easing to Class 2 and 3 near the summit' — easier than some guidebook descriptions suggest, with rock character compared to Chimney Rock.
routes.approach: Same trailhead and trail as the South Peak route (see South Peak via Lookout for the current road-washout situation): Saddle Lake, Goat Flats, Tin Can Gap/Camp Saddle. From Camp Saddle, continue past the standard South Peak turnoff, descending onto the glacier to traverse toward the base of the North Peak's west side.
routes.descent: Reverse the West Couloir/South Face line back to the glacier and Camp Saddle/Tin Can Gap, then retrace the Goat Flats/Saddle Lake trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Down-climb the South Face and West Couloir back onto the glacier, re-traverse to Camp Saddle/Tin Can Gap, and follow the standard trail out through Goat Flats and Saddle Lake to the trailhead (plus the long closed-road walk/bike-out on FS 41/4160).
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed as a long single day from a Goat Flats or Tin Can Gap/Camp Saddle camp, often paired with the South Peak lookout climb; one trip report logged roughly 6.3 hours to the summit from the trailhead, with the round trip (both North and South Peaks) totaling about 14 miles and 5,600 ft of elevation gain/loss.
routes.bail: Retreat down the West Couloir/South Face to the glacier is straightforward from most points below the crux chimney; once past the roped 5th-class move, downclimbing or a short rappel off gear may be needed to retreat.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before committing to the crux chimney move if weather, rockfall, or party pace put a safe return to the glacier and long road-walk-out in doubt.
routes.bestSeason: Mid- to late summer into fall, same window as the South Peak lookout climb; snow/ice in the couloir can persist later into the season than the standard route.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the approach basin or on the peak itself.
whatToBring: Overnight camping gear if combining with South Peak
routes.detailedRack: A light rack — slings and a small selection of cams/nuts — is enough for protecting the short 5th-class chimney move; the rest of the route is unroped scrambling.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal gear placements needed, concentrated at the one 5th-class chimney move; considerable exposure on this section 'warrants a rope' per trip reports even though the technical difficulty is modest.
proTips: Combine with the South Peak lookout climb to make the most of the long approach
The route climbs easier than some older guidebook descriptions suggest
watchOut: Considerable exposure on the crux chimney despite modest technical grade
Route-finding through the West Couloir to South Face transition
Lingering snow/ice in the couloir
knownHazards: Exposed 5th-class chimney move
Glacier travel to reach the peak's base
Rockfall potential in the couloir
Weather/fog on the upper mountain

pitchDetail.notes: Descend from Camp Saddle/Tin Can Gap onto the glacier and traverse to the North Peak's west side.
Snow/rock couloir leading to the South Face.
Short chimney move, then easing to Class 2-3 to the summit.

climate.summer: Prime season once the couloir has melted out enough for safe travel.
climate.fall: Generally stable but shorter days.
climate.winter: The North Peak's Northeast Face has seen a technical winter ice ascent (IV WI4+ M3, Feb 2007), but this standard line is not a winter route.

routes.overview: A technical rock line up the South Face of Three Fingers' Middle Peak (~6,800 ft), the least-documented and least-visited of the mountain's three summits.
routes.beta: The Middle Peak is climbed far less often than the South Peak (lookout) or North Peak and has very little published route detail; it shares the same long Goat Flats/Tin Can Gap approach as the other two summits before diverging toward the Middle Peak's south side. Treat any attempt as a serious, lightly-documented alpine rock objective and be prepared to route-find.
routes.approach: Same trailhead and trail as the South Peak route (Saddle Lake - Goat Flats - Tin Can Gap/Camp Saddle), diverging from the standard lookout line toward the Middle Peak's south aspect; expect the same closed-road approach on FS 41/4160 as the other two routes.
routes.bail: Retreat down the approach slopes/glacier the way you came; there is no documented alternate descent for this route.
routes.turnaround: Given the sparse documentation of this line, set a conservative turnaround time and be prepared to retreat rather than push into unknown terrain in poor weather.
routes.bestSeason: Likely the same mid- to late-summer window as the other Three Fingers summits, once approach snow has melted out, though this is inferred from the shared approach rather than route-specific reporting.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the approach basin (shared with the other Three Fingers routes).
watchOut: Very little published beta exists for this line — expect real route-finding uncertainty
Shares the same long, isolated approach and glacier hazards as the North and South Peak routes
knownHazards: Glacier travel on approach
Undocumented rock quality/route-finding on the face
Isolated approach with no cell coverage
routes.corrections: Given data lists this route only as "Middle Peak (South Face)" with no stats; despite extensive research across primary sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, AAC Publications, trip-report sites), no route-specific grade, pitch count, or first-ascent information could be found — this appears to be a genuinely under-documented line compared to the South and North Peak routes.

hierarchyNote: Geographic mismatch: Bulls Tooth's coordinates (47.6828, -121.0037) and elevation (6,849 ft) are confirmed by Peakbagger, PeakVisor, and Wikipedia — but they place the peak in the Chiwaukum Mountains, Chelan County, ~5.5 mi southeast of Stevens Pass, within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest). This is nowhere near Snoqualmie Pass (which sits ~45 mi south-southwest, in a different sub-range and a different national forest, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF). The given parentArea "Snoqualmie Pass" appears to be a hierarchy placement error; this peak should almost certainly be re-parented under a Stevens Pass / Chiwaukum Mountains / Central Cascades bucket. I proceeded with the enrichment under the given id as instructed, but this should be corrected in the DB.
blurb: Bulls Tooth is a 6,849 ft multi-summited peak in the Chiwaukum Mountains, a sub-range of the Cascades in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness about 5.5 miles southeast of Stevens Pass in Chelan County, Washington. Named by USFS surveyor Albert H. Sylvester for its fang-like profile, it is built of glacially-scoured Late Cretaceous tonalite and stands as a highly prominent (1,293 ft), isolated summit with 360-degree views, feeling almost island-like above the surrounding basins. It sees no recorded technical climbing history; ascents are off-trail Class 3 scrambles, most commonly combined with a visit to the nearby Chain Lakes basin.

routes.overview: An off-trail Class 3 scramble to Bulls Tooth's summit, typically approached via the Chain Lakes basin either from Stevens Pass to the north (via the PCT and a cross-country drop into the Icicle Creek headwaters) or from valley trails on the Icicle Creek side to the south.
routes.beta: Bulls Tooth has no maintained summit trail. Parties reach the base via the Chain Lakes basin — a scenic, blueberry-covered alpine bench in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness — then climb through steep heather, mossy slabs, brush, and short cliff bands to boulder fields below the summit block, finishing on a short, exposed Class 3 pitch of looser, sandier rock near the top. Route-finding is the main challenge; there is no cairned or established line.
routes.approach: Two approaches are used. (1) From Stevens Pass on US Hwy 2, follow the Pacific Crest Trail southbound; near Lake Josephine, leave the PCT and descend cross-country into the Icicle Creek headwaters, then climb roughly 1,800 ft to the Chain Lakes basin, from which the summit scramble begins. (2) From the south, valley trails in the Icicle Creek/Chiwaukum drainage (WTA references Wildhorse Creek Trail #1592 and Whitepine Creek Trail #1582 for a 'Bulls Tooth Loop') provide an alternate, less-documented approach into the same basin. Both approaches are largely off-trail in their upper sections and require solid map/compass or GPS navigation.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route through the boulder fields and heather/slab bands back to Chain Lakes; use extra caution on the loose, sandy rock just below the summit.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the same Class 3 terrain used on ascent — the exposed sandy step below the true summit is often the crux in both directions and is easier to protect or spot on the way down. From Chain Lakes, retrace either the Icicle Creek/PCT approach back toward Stevens Pass or the Wildhorse/Whitepine Creek trail approach back to the Icicle Creek valley, depending on which way you came in.
routes.bail: The scramble section above Chain Lakes has no fixed retreat points; downclimb the same terrain you ascended. Below Chain Lakes, the approach trails/cross-country routes back to either Stevens Pass or the Icicle Creek valley trailheads serve as the bail-out path.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit scramble — the off-trail approach through heather, slab, and boulder terrain is slow and disorienting in poor visibility, and the exposed sandy rock near the top is significantly more hazardous if reached late or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in late summer (roughly July through September) after snow has cleared from the heather slopes and boulder fields; the Chain Lakes basin trip reports found are from late August.
knownHazards: Extended off-trail route-finding through heather, mossy slabs, brush, and cliff bands below Chain Lakes
Loose, sandy rock on the final scramble pitch just below the summit
Remote, unmaintained terrain within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness with no cell coverage expected
watchOut: The final approach is described as a 'deceptively complex mix of steep heather, mossy slabs, bushes, and cliffs' — budget more time than the map distance suggests
The last stretch below the true summit is 'looser and sandier,' enough that some parties in trip reports have stopped short of the true high point
proTips: Combine the climb with a Chain Lakes camp or dayhike from there — the basin itself is a worthwhile destination with blueberries in season
Expect no cairns or established tread above Chain Lakes; be comfortable with unmarked Class 3 route-finding
gear: Map and compass or GPS (route-finding is largely off-trail)
whatToBring: Plenty of water (limited reliable sources above Chain Lakes)
Layers for exposed, high alpine conditions
Trekking poles for the heather/slab sections
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection or established rock climbing route exists; this is an unroped scramble for parties comfortable with exposed Class 3 terrain and loose rock.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep heather, mossy slabs, brush, and short cliff bands; primary route-finding challenge
~100 vertical feet on looser, sandier rock; the exposed crux of the route

road.driveNote: Access is via either US Highway 2 at Stevens Pass (PCT southbound approach) or Icicle Creek Road (Forest Road 7600, pavement transitioning to gravel) off Highway 2 at Leavenworth for Icicle Creek valley trailheads; the exact trailhead used for the Wildhorse Creek/Whitepine Creek approach referenced by WTA was not confirmed in available sources.

access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit at trailhead (Alpine Lakes Wilderness); Wilderness group-size limits apply
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (typical for Okanogan-Wenatchee NF trailheads in this corridor)

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt (6849) and lat/lng (47.682624, -121.003708) are confirmed accurate against Peakbagger (6849.4 ft) and PeakVisor (47.682798, -121.003675). However, the given parentArea 'Snoqualmie Pass' is geographically incorrect — multiple sources (Wikipedia, PeakVisor, Peakbagger, WTA, Lists of John) place Bulls Tooth in the Chiwaukum Mountains near Stevens Pass, Chelan County, not Snoqualmie Pass. See hierarchyNote.

blurb: Gothic Peak (6,213 ft) rises in the Monte Cristo group of the North Cascades in Snohomish County, Washington, just south of Barlow Pass along the Mountain Loop Highway, sharing Gothic Basin and Foggy Lake with its slightly higher neighbor Del Campo Peak (separated by Foggy Pass). Built of sandstone, quartz diorite, and conglomerate-argillite, the peak's standard route is an unroped Class 3 scramble up its north slope from Foggy Lake. It was first climbed on June 10, 1934 by Don Blair, Willard Carr, Norval Grigg, and Art Winder, and takes its name from prospector William Gothic, an early claim-holder in Gothic Basin (officially adopted in 1967).

routes.overview: The standard and easiest way up Gothic Peak: a Class 3 scramble up the mountain's north slope from Foggy Lake in Gothic Basin, commonly combined with neighboring Del Campo Peak across Foggy Pass in the same trip.
routes.beta: From the Weden Creek/Gothic Basin trail's end at Foggy Lake, skirt the lake (stay climber's left/south side) and pick up a boot path and cairns climbing boulder fields toward the saddle on Gothic's southeast ridge. From the saddle, most parties traverse onto the north-side slate/talus slope to bypass an exposed Class 4 line directly on the ridge crest, regaining the crest near the top for a final, exposed ~150 ft of Class 3 scrambling (with moves bordering Class 4) to the summit block. Route-finding above the lake is not obvious and is easy to lose on the talus.
routes.approach: From the Barlow Pass trailhead/parking area on the Mountain Loop Highway, walk about 1 mile on the closed Monte Cristo Road paralleling the Sauk River to the signed Weden Creek (Gothic Basin) Trail #724 junction. The trail climbs steadily and steeply through forest switchbacks from about 2,300-2,360 ft, crossing several creeks, then breaks into open subalpine terrain and becomes progressively more of a rock scramble as it enters Gothic Basin near 5,000 ft, passing two lower tarns before reaching Foggy Lake, which sits below Gothic and Del Campo Peaks and is often still frozen well into summer.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back down the north-side slate/talus slope to the saddle and down to Foggy Lake, then retrace the Weden Creek Trail to Barlow Pass.
routes.descentText: Downclimb from the summit block carefully, especially where a moat can open between rock and lingering snow near the top later in the season. Regain the southeast ridge saddle and drop back onto the north-slope talus/slate to avoid the exposed Class 4 ridge-crest terrain. Where snow still covers the basin (common into July), many parties glissade or plunge-step sections of the descent to Foggy Lake, cutting descent time roughly in half compared to dry-talus travel. From Foggy Lake, follow the boot path and Trail #724 back down through the basin and forest switchbacks to the Monte Cristo Road and Barlow Pass.
routes.itinerary: Day trip: pre-dawn/early start from Barlow Pass; ~3-4 hrs to Foggy Lake via Gothic Basin; under 1 hr to scramble the summit; combine with Del Campo Peak across Foggy Pass if time and conditions allow; descend to Foggy Lake and out to Barlow Pass, total car-to-car around 8 hours. Alternatively, backpack in and camp at Foggy Lake to climb both peaks unhurried the next morning.
routes.bail: This is a straightforward out-and-back scramble with no fixed rappel stations — retreat at any point simply by downclimbing/reversing the ascent line back to the southeast ridge saddle and down to Foggy Lake. There is no shortcut off the upper mountain other than reversing the route.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting that accounts for the full round trip to Foggy Lake and back plus the summit scramble, and turn around if lingering snow, fog, or deteriorating weather make the unmarked route-finding above the lake unsafe to continue.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September, once seasonal snow has receded enough to expose the scramble route, though the upper basin and Foggy Lake can hold snow into early September in heavy years; climbing while snow still covers brush and scree lower down can make travel faster for parties comfortable on snow.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock gear or rope is used on this route — it is an unroped scramble. Carry an ice axe and microspikes/light traction into early-to-mid summer for the approach snow gullies and any residual snow or moat near the summit.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection needed; helmet recommended given loose rock on the upper ridge and traffic from parties above.
whatToBring: Map/compass or GPS track (route above Foggy Lake is unmarked)
Extra water (limited reliable sources above the basin)
Bear canister or food-hang if camping at Foggy Lake per National Forest regulations
proTips: Stay to climber's left around Foggy Lake and look for the boot path/cairns to the saddle on Gothic's southeast ridge — the route is easy to lose on featureless talus.
Many parties combine Gothic Peak with neighboring Del Campo Peak across Foggy Pass in the same day trip.
Climbing while snow still covers the brush and scree below the basin can make for faster, easier travel than a fully melted-out dry-talus ascent.
watchOut: The final ~150 ft below the summit is exposed Class 3 with moves that border on Class 4 — a fall here would be serious.
A moat can open between snow and rock near the summit block later in the season, adding an awkward, exposed step.
The route above Foggy Lake is unmarked and easy to lose on the talus/slate slopes.
knownHazards: Loose rock and suspect blocks on the upper ridge, though heavy scramble traffic keeps the common line reasonably solid.
Snow gullies and avalanche runout can obstruct the Weden Creek trail into early summer.
Frequent fog in Gothic Basin can obscure route-finding on both approach and descent.

pitchDetail.notes: 1-mile road walk from Barlow Pass, then the 3.3-mi Weden Creek Trail #724 climbs steadily through forest from ~2,300 ft to open basin terrain near 5,000 ft.
Boulder-hop past two lower tarns to Foggy Lake, often snow-covered into summer.

pitchDetail.pitch: Foggy Lake to SE ridge saddle
pitchDetail.notes: Boot path climbs boulder fields to a saddle on Gothic Peak's southeast ridge.
Traverse onto the north-side slate/talus slope to avoid the exposed Class 4 ridge-crest line; the final ~150 ft is the crux, with a possible summit moat late season.

routes.fa: Don Blair, Willard Carr, Norval Grigg, Art Winder — June 10, 1934 (peak first ascent, presumed via this same standard north-slope line, the peak's only established route)
routes.rockGrade: Class 3 (brief Class 4 moves near the summit block)
routes.face: North slope (bypass route around the SE ridge crest)
routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 6,214 ft is a slight mismatch: Wikipedia, Stephabegg, Lemkeclimbs, and washingtonstatehiking.com all consistently cite 6,213 ft, which was used for this route's highPointFt. The peak's documented first ascent (Blair/Carr/Grigg/Winder, June 10, 1934) is attributed to this route as the peak's only established/standard line, matching Wikipedia's description of 'the easiest route' via the north slope; it is not separately documented as a distinct route-specific FA.

road.seasonalGate: Highway access to Barlow Pass and the trail itself are effectively closed by snow in winter
road.driveNote: From Granite Falls, follow the Mountain Loop Highway east past the Verlot Public Service Center (~19-20 miles) to the Barlow Pass trailhead/parking area.

climate.typical: Wet, mild Cascade winters with heavy snowpack; a short dry summer window
climate.spring: Deep snow lingers in Gothic Basin and on approach gullies well into June
climate.summer: Best conditions July-September, though the upper basin and Foggy Lake can hold snow into early September in heavy years
climate.fall: Snowfall typically returns by late September/October, closing out the season
climate.winter: Highway and trail are snowbound and not maintained for winter travel

blurb: Hibox Mountain (6,547-6,550 ft) is the high point of Box Ridge in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the Cascade Range, about five miles east of Snoqualmie Pass in Kittitas County, Washington. It is a granite/metamorphic peak of the Chikamin Peak group, rising roughly 3,250 feet above Box Canyon in about a mile, and is best known as a popular class 3 scramble reached via the Rachel Lake Trail rather than for any technical rock climbing history.

routes.overview: A popular class 3 scramble up Hibox's south/east side: the Rachel Lake Trail leads to a boot path that climbs through timber and two rocky cliff bands before finishing up a short, exposed gully to the summit block.
routes.beta: From the Rachel Lake trailhead, follow the maintained Rachel Lake Trail for roughly 2 miles to the first avalanche-chute clearing. A boot path for Hibox leaves the main trail here (or at the second chute crossing) and climbs steeply but stays class 2 through timber to near treeline. Above the trees, go right around the first band of cliffs, then left around a rock outcrop, continue through more timber to a second cliff band, and take the gully on the right to gain the upper mountain. From there head right (east) along the south side of the summit rocks to a gully on the east side, which is climbed with a bit of class 3 near the top to reach the summit. Route-finding is the main challenge; there is no continuous trail above the boot path.
routes.approach: Take I-90 east to Exit 62 (Kachess Lake). Turn onto Kachess Lake Road (Forest Road 49) and head northeast toward Kachess Lake Campground, then turn onto Forest Road 4930 and follow it roughly 4 miles to its end at the large Rachel Lake trailhead parking lot. The trailhead lot fills early on summer weekends; one trip report advises arriving by 7 a.m. From the trailhead, hike the Rachel Lake Trail about 2 miles to the first avalanche-chute clearing, where the unmarked Hibox boot path branches off toward the peak.
routes.descent: Descend the same route: down-climb the summit gully and retrace the boot path back down through the cliff bands to the Rachel Lake Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the ascent gully on the east side of the summit rocks (short class 3 moves), traverse back along the south side of the rocks, and down-climb through the second and first cliff bands via the same gullies/outcrop line used on the way up. Pick up the boot path through timber back down to the avalanche-chute clearing on the Rachel Lake Trail, then follow the maintained trail back to the trailhead. No rappels are used or reported; the whole route is down-climbed.
routes.itinerary: Single car-to-car day: drive to the Rachel Lake trailhead, hike ~2 miles of maintained trail to the avalanche clearing, follow the boot path and class 2-3 scramble terrain to the summit, then reverse the entire route to the car the same day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on this route since it has no technical protection or rappels: if the summit gully looks wet, icy, or beyond your comfort level, turn around anywhere below it and down-climb the boot path back to the Rachel Lake Trail.
routes.turnaround: Because the entire route must be down-climbed on the same line (no rappels), turn back before committing to the upper cliff bands or summit gully if weather is deteriorating, the rock is wet/icy, or the group's pace won't leave enough daylight margin to reverse the class 2-3 terrain safely.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is roughly July through September/October, once the boot path and upper gullies are free of snow; AllTrails lists July-October as the busiest window. Trip reports show high year-to-year variability in early-season snow: some July ascents find no snow at all, while others report patches lingering near 5,500 ft and above. In shoulder-season or heavier-snow conditions, an ice axe and crampons may be needed on the upper mountain.
routes.rappels: None; the route is down-climbed via the same line as the ascent.
objHaz: loose rock on the class 2-3 sections (reported near 3,800 ft and 4,500 ft)
route-finding difficulty above the boot path
exposure on the short class 3 summit gully
early/shoulder-season snow and ice on the upper gully
seasonal wildfire smoke/closures in the area
routes.face: East-side summit gully, reached via the south side of the summit rocks
routes.proNeeds: This is an unroped class 3 scramble for most parties; no fixed protection or standard rack is used. A short rope may be carried by some parties as a precaution for the exposed summit gully, especially in early-season or wet/icy conditions, but it is not standard gear for this route.
gear: ice axe and crampons (early season/shoulder season only)
whatToBring: extra water (no reliable water sources on the route)
sun protection (open avalanche chutes and meadows)
map/compass or GPS track (route-finding required off the boot path)
insect repellent (hornets reported in some seasons)
proTips: The unmarked Hibox boot path leaves the Rachel Lake Trail about 150 feet into the woods past the first avalanche-chute crossing (or at the second chute crossing) -- watch closely or you'll walk right past it.
In low-snow years the route can be climbed nearly snow-free by mid-July; in bigger years check recent trip reports for whether an axe and crampons are warranted on the upper gully.
Arrive early -- the Rachel Lake trailhead lot is popular and fills up on summer weekends.
The climb can be combined with nearby scramble peaks (Chikamin, Alta, Kaleetan, Guye, Chair) for a bigger day in the same area.
watchOut: The boot path and upper gullies are unmarked and require active route-finding, not just trail-following.
Loose rock has been reported on the class 2-3 sections around 3,800 ft and 4,500 ft.
The final summit gully has a short but genuinely exposed class 3 move.
Check current-year fire activity/closures for the Kachess Lake area before heading out -- the Three Queens Fire has prompted area advisories near Little Lake Kachess in recent conditions.
knownHazards: Loose rock is present on the class 2-3 scrambling sections, reported near 3,800 ft and 4,500 ft elevation.
The summit block requires a short class 3 move with real exposure.
There is no water on the route, so all water must be carried in.
Early or shoulder-season parties may encounter snow and ice on the upper mountain requiring an ice axe and crampons.

pitchDetail.notes: ~2 miles of maintained Rachel Lake Trail from the trailhead to the first avalanche-chute clearing.
Unmarked boot path leaves the main trail near the avalanche-chute crossing and climbs steeply through timber; stays class 2 to near the forest edge.
Go right around the first cliff band, left around a rock outcrop, through more timber to a second cliff band, then up a gully on the right to gain the upper mountain.

Traverse east along the south side of the summit rocks to a gully on the east side; climb it with a short class 3 section near the top to reach the true summit (map-measured up to ~47 degrees in the steepest section).

road.name: Forest Road 4930 (via Kachess Lake Road / FR 49) from I-90 Exit 62
road.status: Good gravel road to a large trailhead parking lot at road's end
road.driveNote: From I-90 eastbound take Exit 62 (Kachess Lake), turn onto Kachess Lake Road (FR 49) toward Kachess Lake Campground, then turn onto FR 4930 and follow it about 4 miles to the Rachel Lake trailhead parking area at the road's end.

climate.typical: Snow-free ascents are typical from roughly July through September; in low-snow years only isolated patches remain near/above 5,500 ft by midsummer.
climate.summer: Peak climbing season; most July-August trip reports describe little to no snow on route.
climate.fall: Accessible into October in typical years per trail-use data, with increasing chance of early-season snow/ice on the upper gully as the season progresses.
climate.winter: Not a standard winter objective, but the Mazamas list the climb as done in all four seasons, noting an ice axe and crampons may be needed in early-season/winter conditions.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful (National Park/Interagency) Pass required at the trailhead
access.closures: No permanent route closures known; check current Forest Service fire information before going, as the Three Queens Fire near Little Lake Kachess has prompted area-avoidance advisories in recent conditions.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning -- the Rachel Lake trailhead lot is popular and fills up on summer weekends; one trip report recommends arriving by 7 a.m.

emergency.county: Kittitas County (summit); trailhead access is near the King/Kittitas county line off I-90
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
emergency.notes: As of mid-July 2026, the Three Queens Fire burning roughly 1.5 miles northwest of Little Lake Kachess has prompted responders to request avoidance of the area; check current Forest Service/InciWeb status before approaching via Kachess Lake Road.

routes.corrections: Sources cluster tightly around the given elevation: Wikipedia lists 6,550 ft, willhiteweb.com lists 6,547 ft, and the provided 6,548 ft falls within that same narrow range (likely just rounding/survey differences between sources) -- no correction needed. WTA's hike page lists a slightly higher 6,560 ft high point and 3,900 ft gain vs. 3,800 ft corroborated by willhiteweb.com and Mazamas; the 3,800 ft figure (2 independent sources) was used for gainFt/lossFt.

blurb: Mount Fury sits in the Picket Range within North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington, and is the range's second-highest summit after nearby Luna Peak. Composed of biotite gneiss/granite born of Cretaceous-era metamorphism and later carved by Pleistocene glaciation, the mountain has two distinct summits — East Fury (the main/higher summit, first climbed in 1938) and West Fury — joined by a chossy, gendarme-studded connecting ridge. West Peak was long regarded as one of the North Cascades' "last great problems" for its extreme remoteness and complex approach, not being summited until August 19, 1958, and its dramatic mile-long South Buttress (Mongo Ridge) wasn't climbed until Wayne Wallace's solo first ascent in 2006.

routes.fa: Wayne Wallace, solo — August 24-27, 2006
routes.rockGrade: 5.10 (sustained 5.4-5.9 with a 5.10 crux and one 5.11 headwall variation reported)
routes.rappels: ~10-12 rappels down the ridge's gendarmes/towers (varies with route-finding/bypass choices)
objHaz: extreme remoteness with very limited retreat options
routes.face: South Buttress (Mongo Ridge) of West Peak
routes.overview: The South Buttress of West Peak — known as Mongo Ridge — is a mile-long, roughly 4,000-foot ridge crest bristling with towers and gendarmes, first soloed by Wayne Wallace over four days in August 2006 as one of the last unclimbed major lines in the North Cascades.
routes.beta: Mongo Ridge is a long, sustained alpine ridge climb (5.4-5.9 predominant, with a 5.10 crux) up the South Buttress of Mount Fury's West Peak. Rather than bushwhacking directly up from Goodell Creek, most parties first climb East Fury via the standard approach, then descend roughly 4,000 ft into the Access Creek/Goodell Creek basin to reach the base of the ridge, and finally climb back up and over a sequence of gendarmes/towers to the West Peak summit. Rock quality is generally decent granite/granodiorite but with significant loose sections, especially near the ridge's gain point and the false summit.
routes.approach: Start at the Ross Dam Trailhead on WA-20, hike down and cross Ross Dam, then take the Ross Lake Resort water taxi (~15 min) to the Big Beaver trailhead. Follow the Big Beaver Trail toward Luna Camp, then climb to Luna Col and traverse to East Fury. Summit East Fury, then descend roughly 4,000 ft into the Access Creek/Goodell Creek basin to gain the base of the South Buttress (Mongo Ridge).
routes.descent: Reverse the ridge back over the false summit and gendarmes to East Fury, then descend via the standard Luna Col/Access Creek route.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, downclimb/rappel back across the false summit and gendarme sequence to the point where the ridge was gained near East Fury, re-ascend East Fury, and descend its standard route back to Luna Col and out via the Big Beaver Trail to Ross Lake.
routes.itinerary: A typical 6-day plan: Day 1 - Ross Lake to Luna Camp; Day 2 - Luna Camp to Luna Col; Day 3 - summit East Fury and climb the first ~7 pitches to gain the ridge; Day 4 - climb Mongo Ridge to the false summit (long day, 12+ hours of climbing); Day 5 - false summit to true summit and back over East Fury; Day 6 - exit via Access Creek/Big Beaver Trail.
routes.bail: Retreat is extremely limited once past the first tower; the ridge is essentially committing. The only realistic options are reversing rappels back the way you came or continuing over the top — carry extra rappel gear/anchors and plan for a multi-day retreat given the remoteness.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day commitment and near-total lack of bail options, set a firm turnaround/bivy decision point each climbing day based on remaining daylight, weather, and party fatigue rather than a fixed clock time; abort to the nearest safe bivy ledge (several are documented on the route) if a thunderstorm threatens on this highly exposed ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through August; an early-July start in a heavier snow year is favored so notches and the Rooster Comb bypass still hold snow for water, since the route otherwise has little water on it. Afternoon thunderstorms are a real risk in late summer.
routes.detailedRack: A single alpine rack covers the terrain: cams roughly 0.1-2 (singles), doubles in the 0.3-0.75 range, and a good selection of nuts; one small offset/beak-style piece is useful for thin cracks. Carry about 50 ft of 6mm cordelette for backing up marginal anchors. A single 60m rope is reportedly sufficient if taking the Rooster Comb bypass.
routes.proNeeds: Predominantly small-to-medium cams and nuts protecting sustained, often R-rated 5.4-5.9 ridge climbing on rock of variable quality; anchors are frequently built from questionable blocks, so redundant/backed-up anchors are important.
gear: glacier travel gear for the approach
whatToBring: bivy gear for 5-6 days on route
extra water capacity for dry sections
proTips: Go in an early-season, heavy-snow year so notches and the Rooster Comb bypass still hold snow for water
Climb East Fury first and descend into the basin to reach the ridge, rather than bushwhacking up from Goodell Creek
Take the Rooster Comb bypass (about 6 pitches, 2 rappels) instead of the direct crux traverse if conditions or time are marginal
A single 60m rope is enough if you take the bypass
watchOut: Most pitches are R-rated with sparse protection on loose rock
A rappel anchor built on a loose block has failed on this route before, severing a rope — always back up anchors
There is no quick bail once committed past the first tower
Afternoon thunderstorms build quickly on this exposed, remote ridge
knownHazards: Sustained loose rock/choss along the ridge crest and near the false summit
Significant rockfall potential from gendarmes and towers
Extreme remoteness — one gendarme is nicknamed the 'Pole of Remoteness' and reputed to be one of the hardest-to-reach points in the lower 48
Moats where snow meets rock can complicate access to the ridge in some seasons

pitchDetail.pitch: Gaining the ridge from East Fury
pitchDetail.notes: ~7 pitches of scrambling/roped climbing to reach the ridge crest after descending from East Fury
3 pitches to the tower's summit
2 pitches; ~40m rappel off the far side
6 pitches; one party called a section here the wildest pitch they'd climbed; ~40m rappel; the summit ledge reportedly sleeps ~5
3 pitches; ~50m rappel
Direct crux traverse, or a 6-pitch/2-rappel bypass around it favored in lower-snow years
1 pitch to a gendarme reputed to be one of the hardest-to-reach points in the lower 48
~2 pitches; notably loose rock near the top

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20) to Ross Dam Trailhead
road.status: Paved and open seasonally west of the Washington Pass/Rainy Pass winter closure gates (this trailhead itself is on the open, lower-elevation portion of WA-20)
road.seasonalGate: Not directly gated (Ross Dam Trailhead is west of the seasonal SR-20 mountain closure), but access also depends on Ross Lake Resort's seasonal water-taxi operating dates
road.driveNote: Park at the Ross Dam Trailhead pullout on WA-20, hike down to Ross Lake, cross Ross Dam, and take the Ross Lake Resort water taxi (fee, ~15 min) to the Big Beaver trailhead.

climate.typical: Marine west-coast mountain climate with heavy orographic precipitation off the Pacific; wet, changeable weather is common even in summer
climate.summer: Short climbing window (roughly July-August) with warm days but frequent afternoon thunderstorms
climate.fall: Wetter, with early snow possible by September-October
climate.winter: Heavy, wet snow and high avalanche hazard; the approach is essentially inaccessible

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: No park entrance fee (North Cascades NP is fee-free); the Ross Lake Resort water taxi charges its own fee
access.permit: A wilderness/backcountry permit is required for all overnight stays — reserve via recreation.gov or obtain a walk-up permit at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, WA
access.passRequired: None (no Northwest Forest Pass needed within the park; the wilderness permit substitutes)
access.closures: Access depends on Ross Lake Resort's seasonal water-taxi schedule; check current NPS trail/washout conditions on the Big Beaver Trail before approaching

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine starts on climbing days given the sustained, many-pitch nature of the route

waypoints.name: West Peak of Mount Fury (West Fury) summit

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (coordinates with Whatcom County Sheriff's Office and Whatcom County Search & Rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA
emergency.notes: Extremely remote terrain with essentially no cell coverage; a satellite communicator/PLB is strongly advised, and rescue response can take a very long time given the terrain.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 8,303 ft vs. 8,301 ft per Wikipedia/USGS-derived listings — negligible measurement variance, not corrected further. Pitch/rappel counts vary slightly by source and by which optional bypass (e.g., the Rooster Comb bypass) a party takes: the original 2006 FA and most secondary sources cite ~25 pitches and roughly a dozen rappels, while one detailed 2022 ascent account recorded closer to 30 pitches and 8 rappels via a bypass variation — both are reported here as a range rather than picking one as definitive.

routes.fa: Duke Watson, Phil Sharpe, Warren Spickard, Vic Josendal, and Maury Muzzy — August 19, 1958 (first ascent of West Peak; published in the American Alpine Journal, 1959)
routes.rockGrade: 5.4 (up to class 4/low-5th on the connecting towers)
objHaz: chossy/loose rock along the connecting ridge
exposure on 3 towers up to class 5.4
remote setting with limited direct escape
routes.face: Connecting ridge between East Fury and West Peak summits
routes.overview: A moderate (up to class 4/5.4) roughly half-mile ridge traverse connecting East Fury's summit to West Peak, typically climbed as a continuation of an East Fury ascent or as part of a full Picket Range traverse rather than as a standalone objective.
routes.beta: This is the historic line of approach to West Peak: 1958's first-ascent party reached the summit via the West Ridge after approaching from the Challenger Glacier side, and the peak is also reached today by traversing the connecting ridge from East Fury's summit across three distinct, chossy towers (up to 5.4) before the final push to West Peak.
routes.approach: Start at the Ross Dam Trailhead on WA-20, cross Ross Dam, and take the Ross Lake Resort water taxi to the Big Beaver trailhead. Hike the Big Beaver Trail to Luna Camp, climb to Luna Col, and continue to East Fury's summit (or approach via the Challenger Glacier from the north/west for the original West Ridge line).
routes.descent: Reverse the ridge traverse back to East Fury, then descend East Fury's standard route via Luna Col.
routes.descentText: From West Peak's summit, downclimb/reverse the connecting ridge across the three towers back to East Fury, then descend East Fury's standard glacier/scramble route to Luna Col and out via the Big Beaver Trail.
routes.bail: Once committed to the connecting ridge, retreat generally means reversing back toward East Fury; there is no quick, direct descent off the West Peak side of this line.
routes.turnaround: Because retreat from the connecting ridge is slow and this route is usually done as an add-on after already climbing East Fury, build in a conservative turnaround based on remaining daylight and party energy for the return traverse and descent, not a fixed clock time.
routes.bestSeason: Summer (roughly July-September), matching the broader Picket Range climbing season when the approach glaciers and trail are most passable.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack suffices for the class 4-5.4 towers — a handful of small-to-mid cams and nuts — since this line is far shorter and less sustained than Mongo Ridge; full glacier-travel kit (rope, crevasse-rescue gear, axe/crampons) is needed for the approach.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needs — mostly natural gear or slung horns/blocks with occasional small cam or nut placements on the class 5 sections.
gear: light rack of small-to-mid cams and nuts
glacier travel rope and crevasse-rescue kit
ice axe and crampons for the approach glaciers
whatToBring: bivy gear for the multi-day approach
proTips: Combine with an East Fury ascent or a full Picket Range traverse rather than treating this as a standalone objective
Carry only a light rack for the class 5 sections — the towers are short
Full glacier-travel gear is needed for the Luna Col/Challenger Glacier approach regardless of which side you come from
watchOut: Rock quality is notably poor/chossy on this connecting ridge
This is not a standalone route — it requires first reaching East Fury's summit (or the Challenger Glacier side for the original line)
Retreat once on the ridge is slow and mostly limited to reversing your steps
knownHazards: Loose, chossy rock across the three connecting towers
Exposure on class 4-5.4 terrain well above the surrounding glaciers
General Picket Range remoteness limiting rescue/retreat options

pitchDetail.notes: Considered the crux tower before reaching West Peak's summit

climate.fall: Wetter, with early snow possible by September-October

waypoints.name: West Peak of Mount Fury (West Fury) summit

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (coordinates with Whatcom County Sheriff's Office and Whatcom County Search & Rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA
