routes.corrections: Sources conflict on the exact identity/history of this line. An August 19, 1958 ascent by Duke Watson, Phil Sharpe, Warren Spickard, Vic Josendal, and Maury Muzzy is recorded as the first ascent of West Peak via a 'West Ridge,' approached from the Challenger Glacier (west/northwest) side. Mountain Project separately documents a 'Ridge Traverse from East Fury' (up to 5.4, 3 towers), which may be a distinct, easier connecting line — a traverse by Don Keller, Joan Firey, and Joe Firey is also referenced in period accounts but undated. This entry treats the 1958 ascent as the best-documented first ascent of the West Peak summit, but it is not certain to be the identical named line described by Mountain Project's traverse route; given elevation of 8,303 ft vs. 8,301 ft in independent sources is a negligible discrepancy.

blurb: Mount Daniel is the highest peak in both King and Kittitas Counties, sitting on the Cascade Crest within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of central Washington and carrying five small named summits (West/true, Middle, East, West Pyramid, Northwest) above several small, rapidly retreating glaciers (Lynch, Daniel, Hyas Creek). The mountain is built of Oligocene-age andesite, dacite, and rhyolite volcanic rock (breccia, tuff, and interbedded volcanic sandstone), and Mountain Project describes it as "likely the gentlest peak of its size in the Cascades," known for easy glacier climbing rather than technical rock. It was first climbed in 1925 by a Mountaineers club party via the Lynch Glacier on the peak's west side, though earlier surveyor ascents are suspected.

routes.rockGrade: Class 3 (3rd-class scrambling on the summit block/ridge; Mountain Project also lists a PG13 runout rating)
routes.aspect: N/NE (Daniel Glacier side) and SE (Southeast Ridge/Slopes side)
routes.face: Daniel Glacier lies on the north/northeast side of the peak, between the Middle Summit and East Peak, terminating in an icefall; the Southeast Ridge (aka Southeast Slopes) is the broad ridge just west of Peggy's Pond, climbing past Point 7662 to the col between East Peak and Middle Summit.
routes.overview: A moderate, low-fifth-to-third-class alpine outing that pairs an easy but crevassed glacier crossing on Mount Daniel's north side with a talus/neve ridge scramble on the southeast side; many parties climb one aspect and descend the other as a loop out of Peggy's Pond.
routes.beta: Mountain Project rates this line 3rd class with a PG13 runout warning; conditions matter more than technical difficulty. With good snow cover the ridge and glacier travel are quick and pleasant (some parties glissade descents); by late season the Southeast Ridge degrades to loose, chossy scrambling and the Daniel Glacier opens up with more crevasses, sometimes forcing a reroute via the Hyas Creek Glacier to the East Summit instead.
routes.approach: From Cathedral Rock Trailhead (~3,350 ft, reached via Salmon la Sac Rd/FS-4330), follow the Cathedral Pass Trail #1345 roughly 4.5 miles to Cathedral Pass (~5,500 ft) where it meets the PCT. Take the established climbers' path along the south side of Cathedral Rock to Peggy's Pond, a common camping area. For the Southeast Ridge, climb the obvious broad ridge just west of the pond. For Daniel Glacier, continue/traverse toward the glacier's toe on the north side below the Middle/East Summit saddle (an alternate, longer approach used by some parties reaches this side via Marmot Lake/Jade Lake and Dip Top Gap from the Necklace Valley/PCT corridor to the north).
routes.descent: Descend the same aspect you climbed, or complete a loop by descending the opposite side, returning through Peggy's Pond and out via Cathedral Pass Trail.
routes.descentText: From the true (west) summit, downclimb or traverse the plateau just south of the Middle Summit back to the East Peak/Middle Summit col. To descend the Southeast Ridge, drop east down talus and neve, staying right of a bedrock hump, past Point 7662, following cairns back toward Circle Lake and Peggy's Pond. To descend via Daniel Glacier, downclimb north onto the glacier, threading crevasses back to its toe, then contour back around to Peggy's Pond. From Peggy's Pond, retrace the climbers' path past Cathedral Rock and down the Cathedral Pass Trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Cathedral Rock Trailhead and hike ~4.5 miles up Cathedral Pass Trail #1345 to Cathedral Pass, then continue past Cathedral Rock to Peggy's Pond; camp near the pond. Day 2: From Peggy's Pond, climb the Southeast Ridge (or traverse to and rope up for the Daniel Glacier) to the East Peak/Middle Summit col, then to the true (west) summit; descend the opposite aspect to make a loop back to Peggy's Pond. Day 2 or 3: Hike out via Cathedral Pass to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat down the side you ascended rather than committing to the unfamiliar opposite aspect — the Southeast Ridge talus/neve is the more straightforward bail option compared to down-climbing the crevassed Daniel Glacier. Regroup at Peggy's Pond and exit via the Cathedral Pass Trail.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit push — afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Central Cascades in summer, and softening snow bridges on the Daniel Glacier increase crevasse-fall risk as the day warms.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is the typical window, once the Cathedral Pass approach and Peggy's Pond basin are snow-free enough for travel. The Southeast Ridge is reportedly more enjoyable with some lingering snow cover (easier travel, glissade potential) and becomes loose, chossy scrambling once fully melted out by late summer. The Daniel Glacier is best crossed earlier in the season before crevasses open further; if it becomes too broken or icy, parties sometimes reroute via the Hyas Creek Glacier to reach the East Summit instead.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit is needed for the Daniel Glacier side: harness, glacier rope, crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks/pulley/rescue pulley), ice axe, and crampons. The Southeast Ridge/Slopes side is typically climbed unroped as a scramble in dry conditions (Mountain Project lists protection as N/A), though an axe is worth carrying for lingering snow/neve near Point 7662 and the summit col. A helmet is recommended on both sides for rockfall and icefall.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed or trad protection is placed on this route — the technical requirement is glacier-travel/crevasse-rescue gear for the Daniel Glacier side, not rock protection.
whatToBring: overnight pack if camping at Peggy's Pond
extra layers for exposed ridge and glacier travel
proTips: If the Daniel Glacier is too crevassed or icy late-season, an alternative is to reach the East Summit via the Hyas Creek Glacier instead (per The Mountaineers).
Many parties treat this as a loop or point-to-point traverse: up one aspect (glacier or ridge), down the other, both funneling through Peggy's Pond.
Follow the cairns closely on the Southeast Ridge's loose talus sections to avoid route-finding trouble.
Earlier-season snow cover makes the Southeast Ridge easier and more enjoyable than late-summer conditions, when it becomes chossy, loose scrambling.
watchOut: Daniel Glacier is an active, crevassed glacier terminating in an icefall — rope up and carry a crevasse-rescue kit.
Loose talus and route-finding difficulty on the Southeast Ridge, particularly past Point 7662.
Severe mosquitoes in the Peggy's Pond/sub-alpine basins in late July and August.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in Central Cascades summers — get off the summit and glacier before they build.
knownHazards: Crevasse-fall hazard on the actively retreating, icefall-terminating Daniel Glacier.
Rockfall and loose rock (choss) on the talus slopes below the summit block.
Exposed class 3 scrambling on the final summit block/ridge (Mountain Project's PG13 rating).
Lingering snowfields and neve on the upper mountain into mid-summer create slip-and-fall potential without an ice axe.

pitchDetail.notes: Cathedral Pass Trail #1345 from Cathedral Rock Trailhead (~3,350 ft) to Cathedral Pass (~5,500 ft) at the PCT, ~4.5 miles; climbers' path past Cathedral Rock to Peggy's Pond.
Cross the glacier on the north/northeast side toward the Middle/East Summit saddle; crevasse navigation required, glacier terminates in an icefall.
From Peggy's Pond, climb the broad ridge west of the pond, up talus and neve, past Point 7662, keeping right of a bedrock hump, to the saddle between East Peak and Middle Summit.
Climb the Middle Summit or traverse the plateau just south of it to reach the true (western) summit.

road.name: Salmon la Sac Rd (SR-903) to FS Road 4330; alternately Foss River Rd NE/FS-68 for the Necklace Valley/north-side approach
road.driveNote: From Roslyn, take Highway 903 north through Ronald (becomes Salmon La Sac Road); before Salmon La Sac Campground turn right onto FS-4330 at 15.5 miles and continue ~27.8 miles to Cathedral Rock Trailhead. The north-side approach toward Marmot/Jade Lakes instead uses Foss River Road NE off Highway 2 (paved 2.5 miles, then gravel FS-68 ~4.2 miles) to the Necklace Valley Trailhead.

climate.typical: Snow-free approach conditions typically run June through September; the upper mountain and glaciers hold snow well into July.
climate.spring: Trailhead road and lower trail often remain snow-covered into May/June; heavy snowpack persists on the approach and route.
climate.summer: Prime climbing season (Jun-Sep); afternoon thunderstorms are common and mosquitoes can be severe in the sub-alpine basins in late July/August.
climate.fall: Cooler, drier conditions can extend into October before snow returns, with shortening daylight.
climate.winter: Road and trail are typically snowed in; the route becomes a winter/ski-mountaineering objective only.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: No route/climbing fee; a parking pass is required at the trailhead
access.permit: Self-issue wilderness permit at the trailhead (no fee)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass

timing.recommendedStart: Early/alpine start recommended for firmer glacier and snow conditions before afternoon softening and thunderstorm risk

emergency.county: Kittitas County, WA (trailhead/approach); the summit area sits on the Kittitas/King County line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (509) 925-8534
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest — (509) 852-1100, 803 W. 2nd St, Cle Elum, WA 98922
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, 603 S Chestnut St, Ellensburg, WA 98926
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness approach — expect no reliable cell coverage beyond the trailhead/road corridor; a self-issue wilderness permit is required.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project's area page for Mount Daniel lists an elevation of 3,080 ft, which is clearly an error (contradicted by every other source and by this same route's own GPS pin at ~7,977-7,986 ft) — not adopted. Broader elevation figures vary by source (Wikipedia/SummitPost ~7,960+ ft, WTA 7,986 ft, backtrackmaps 7,965 ft); the catalog's 7,977 ft falls within this range and was not overwritten. The peak's first ascent (1925, Mountaineers club, via Lynch Glacier) is a PEAK first ascent, not a first ascent of this Daniel Glacier/Southeast Slopes line — Mountain Project lists the FA for both the Southeast Ridge and Lynch Glacier routes as 'Unknown,' so route 'fa' was left null rather than reusing the peak's 1925 date.

blurb: "North Peak" sits in a remote, little-documented cluster of summits along the Blue Mountain ridge in the Glacier Peak Wilderness (Chelan County, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest), immediately adjacent to the well-documented Gunsight Peak (8,198 ft) — the tallest of this ridge series, flanked by Blue Glacier to the east and the larger Chickamin Glacier to the west. Local climbers refer to this cluster informally as "the Gunsight Range"; the area is granitic North Cascades rock reached only via long approaches (historically noted as boat-assisted access), and it sees very little documented traffic — only a single trip report (July 2007) could be located online describing a West Face ascent of the range's north peak.
hierarchyNote: Flagging for review, not blocking: the given coordinates (48.3068, -120.994) are essentially identical (within ~30 m) to the official GNIS/Wikipedia coordinates for "Gunsight Peak" (48°18'24"N 120°59'38"W = 48.3067, -120.9939), a distinct, named, 8,198 ft peak in the same immediate area described by Wikipedia as "the tallest of a series of peaks along Blue Mountain." It's unclear whether this "North Peak" is (a) a genuinely separate, slightly lower summit in that same ridge series close enough to Gunsight Peak that coordinates round to nearly the same value, (b) an informal/alternate name locally used for part of the same massif, or (c) a data-entry artifact where North Peak's coordinates were copied from Gunsight Peak's. A 2007 cascadeclimbers.com trip report titled "Gunsight range - West face-north peak" confirms the peak/route combination (North Peak, West Face, within "the Gunsight Range") is a real, if extremely obscure, climbing objective distinct from the AAC/Wikipedia-documented "Gunsight Peak" ascent route — so I did not assign Gunsight Peak's own elevation (8,198 ft) to this entry, since I can't confirm the two are the same summit. I could not access the full trip report (cascadeclimbers.com blocks automated fetches; archive.org/wayback fetches are blocked in this environment) beyond a search-engine snippet confirming its existence, date (July 25, 2007), and boat-based access. Peakbagger.com, SummitPost, and Mountain Project all either lack a page for this peak or blocked automated access (403) during this research session; the web search tool's budget was also exhausted mid-session. Recommend a follow-up pass with direct browser access (not automated fetch) to cascadeclimbers.com and peakbagger.com if more certainty is wanted.

routes.beta: The only located documentation of this route is a single cascadeclimbers.com trip report (dated July 25, 2007) titled "Gunsight range - West face-north peak," which confirms climbers reached "the fabled Gunsight Range" by boat and climbed the West Face of its north peak. The full report text could not be retrieved (site blocks automated access), so grade, pitch count, and detailed line description are not available from accessible sources.

access.landManager: Glacier Peak Wilderness, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (formerly Wenatchee National Forest)

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt for the peak was null; a genuinely distinct, well-documented peak ("Gunsight Peak," 8,198 ft, USGS quad Agnes Mountain WA) shares near-identical coordinates with this entry, but since I cannot confirm North Peak and Gunsight Peak are the same summit, I did not fill in that elevation as highPointFt — see hierarchyNote for full explanation.

blurb: Boston Peak (8,894 ft) rises in the Boston Basin area of North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, Washington, connected to Buckner Mountain by the sharp arête of Ripsaw Ridge. It sits on the Skagit/Columbia watershed divide and is flanked by the Boston, Quien Sabe, and Davenport glaciers; the peak's rock is notably steep and loose/friable throughout. It was named for the Boston Mine, worked on its southeast side in the late 1800s, and first summited in 1938 by Calder Bressler, Ray Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers.
hierarchyNote: None — verified. Boston Peak's coordinates (48.4954, -121.0362) and elevation (8,894 ft) match Wikipedia/USGS-derived data (48°29'44"N 121°02'10"W, 8,894 ft) almost exactly, and Boston Basin is confirmed by multiple independent sources (NPS, The Mountaineers, WTA, trip reports) as the correct sub-basin of North Cascades National Park containing this peak, alongside Sahale, Forbidden Peak, and Buckner Mountain. Peak and parent area are consistent and correctly placed.

routes.overview: A long, sharp, extremely loose ridge line running northwest from Boston Peak's summit toward Sharkfin Tower; documented as part of a 2018 circumnavigation of Boston Basin rather than as a standalone guidebook route.
routes.beta: This ridge was climbed by Sam Boyce and Kyle Willis in 2018 as one leg of their 'Boston Marathon' — a ~26.2-mile, mostly-soloed technical traverse circling the entire rim of Boston Basin (26 pitches up to 5.8 R, 26 rappels, 20,000+ ft of technical terrain). Their account calls this 'the likely unclimbed northwest ridge of Boston Peak,' describing 'deep... virgin choss' rated 5.5 X, including a two-pitch section climbed à cheval (straddling the ridge crest) across rock 'the consistency of feta cheese,' followed by roughly 1,000 additional feet of similarly poor climbing. It is accessed along the basin rim from the direction of Sharkfin Tower. No dedicated guidebook (Beckey, Mountain Project, SummitPost) description of this specific ridge as an independent route was found — treat it as an obscure, rarely-repeated line rather than a maintained trade route.
routes.approach: Approached via Boston Basin (from the Cascade Pass Trailhead at the end of Cascade River Road) and along the basin's rim toward Sharkfin Tower, then onto the northwest ridge crest of Boston Peak itself.
routes.fa: Sam Boyce & Kyle Willis, 2018 (as part of the 'Boston Marathon' Boston Basin traverse; described by the party as likely the first ascent of this specific ridge line)
routes.face: Northwest Ridge (basin-rim ridge toward Sharkfin Tower)
knownHazards: Rock quality on this ridge is described firsthand as extremely poor ("virgin choss"), with an à cheval section across friable, crumbly stone.
The route is rated X (serious/runout) for long stretches — protection is sparse and falls would be dangerous.
This is an unmaintained, essentially unrepeated line with no established rappel/retreat stations.
watchOut: Do not assume solid rock anywhere on this ridge — test every hold.
There is no documented descent or bail route specific to this ridge; retreat means reversing loose, exposed terrain.
proTips: Treat this as an exploratory alpine ridge rather than an established route — go with a party experienced in loose 5th-class terrain and X-rated runouts.
Moving individually (not simul-climbing) through the loosest sections, as the first ascent party did, reduces the chance of rockfall onto a partner.
routes.bail: No documented bail points; retreat requires downclimbing/rappelling the loose ridge crest back the way it was climbed, or dropping into Boston Basin on rotten rock/scree if a lower point permits.
routes.turnaround: On loose, largely unprotected alpine ridges like this, turn around well before afternoon thunderstorm buildup or if rockfall risk increases (e.g., after rain, or with parties above); poor rock quality means conditions can deteriorate quickly.
routes.bestSeason: Best attempted in mid-to-late summer (July-September) once snow has cleared from the ridge and approach gullies, reducing (but not eliminating) loose-rock/rockfall hazard.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boston Basin or on the ridge; satellite communicator/messenger recommended for this remote, rarely-traveled line.
routes.proNeeds: Sparse, unreliable protection on rotten rock — treat gear placements as marginal at best; a standard alpine rock rack was carried by the first-ascent party but the rating (X) reflects that protection frequently could not be trusted.
whatToBring: extra rope/webbing for improvised rappel anchors on loose rock
sturdy gloves for handling sharp/loose rock

pitchDetail.notes: Loose ridge crest, described as 'virgin choss'
Straddling the ridge across rock 'the consistency of feta cheese'
Continued loose climbing to the summit area, described as 'best forgotten'

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Gravel beyond mile 10; typically opens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead by late June (sometimes delayed to early July by snow)
road.seasonalGate: Subject to winter/off-season closures and periodic maintenance/emergency closures; foot and bicycle travel is allowed past closed gates
road.driveNote: First 10 miles paved, then rough gravel; final ~3 miles to Cascade Pass Trailhead are narrow with steep drop-offs and no turnarounds — high-clearance vehicle recommended, trailers/vehicles over 22 ft or 8 ft wide prohibited beyond milepost 18

climate.typical: Alpine climate with a short weather-safe window; snow can linger into July on approach terrain
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack, avalanche hazard on approach slopes, road likely still gated
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); afternoon thunderstorms possible
climate.fall: Shortening days, early snow possible by late September/October
climate.winter: Road closed, deep snowpack, not a winter objective

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS)
access.permit: Backcountry/wilderness permit required year-round for any overnight stay in Boston Basin, reserved via recreation.gov with walk-up availability at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.closures: Boston Basin is a designated high-use cross-country zone: bear canisters required, no campfires, composting toilet, 12-person site/group-size limits

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (360) 428-3211 (911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Drive, Sedro-Woolley, WA 98284
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in Boston Basin; carry a satellite communicator. Climbing rangers based at the Marblemount WIC can advise on current route/rockfall conditions.

routes.corrections: The peak's overall first ascent (1938, Bressler/Clough/Cox/Myers per Wikipedia) is a separate event from this ridge's first documented ascent (Boyce & Willis, 2018) — the two must not be conflated.

routes.overview: The standard/easiest line on Boston Peak: a basic glacier approach across the Quien Sabe Glacier followed by a class 3/4 scramble up loose rock ledges on the east/southeast side of the peak to the summit.
routes.beta: Guides (Summitborn, The Mountaineers) and trip reports describe this as Boston Peak's standard route: approach across the Quien Sabe Glacier (or via the Sahale Arm/Sahale-Boston col) to reach the base of the peak, then a steep, exposed class 3/4 ledge system straight up the east-facing rock to the summit. Rock is notoriously loose throughout ('rotten terrain'); parties typically move one at a time on the worst sections to avoid knocking rock onto each other. The Mountaineers run this as a 'Basic Glacier Climb' program listing, formally naming it 'Boston Peak/Southeast Face via Quien Sabe Glacier.' Descent is generally by the same line, using rappels.
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass Trailhead (end of Cascade River Road, ~3,600 ft), hike into Boston Basin via the climbers' boot path, establish camp in the basin (a designated NPS high-use zone), then head up toward the Sahale-Boston col/Quien Sabe Glacier. Rope up for glacier travel around ~6,700 ft, cross the Quien Sabe Glacier avoiding crevasses, and gain the base of the peak's east-facing rock below the col.
routes.rappels: Multiple rappels (parties commonly use three rappels on 60m ropes on the descent)
objHaz: glacier crevasses on the Quien Sabe Glacier
knownHazards: The rock throughout the upper mountain is loose and friable — described by Wikipedia's peak summary as 'very loose and friable' overall.
Rockfall risk is high enough that many parties space out or move one at a time through the worst ledge sections.
The Quien Sabe Glacier requires standard crevasse-aware glacier travel (rope, axe, crampons).
watchOut: Treat every hold on the ledge system as suspect; helmet use is essential given rockfall history.
Glacier conditions and crevasse coverage change through the season — assess before roping up.
proTips: Many parties combine this climb with Sahale Peak, approaching both from the same Sahale Arm/Boston Basin camp.
Go early to avoid rockfall exposure from parties above on the ledges, and to cross the glacier while snow is still firm.
routes.bail: Retreat down the same class 3/4 ledge system used on the ascent, rappelling as needed, back to the Sahale-Boston col and across the Quien Sabe Glacier to Boston Basin.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on glacier travel and afternoon thunderstorm risk — the loose rock and glacier crossing are both markedly more hazardous in poor visibility or after the rock has been warmed and destabilized by full sun.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-summer through early fall (roughly July-September), after the Cascade River Road opens and glacier/snow conditions on the Quien Sabe Glacier and approach have consolidated; conditions are climber-dependent and should be checked with the Marblemount climbing rangers before a trip.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in Boston Basin or on the peak; a satellite communicator or PLB is recommended given the remote, glaciated setting.
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier travel kit (rope, harness, crevasse rescue gear) plus a handful of slings/small rack for protecting the class 4 ledges and building rappel anchors; no dedicated big rock rack is typically needed for this scrambling-grade route.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection; natural gear (slings around horns/blocks) and occasional small cams/nuts suffice for the class 4 sections, supplemented by rappel anchors built from available rock features or webbing.
whatToBring: approach shoes or light mountaineering boots

pitchDetail.notes: Rope up ~6,700 ft; cross Quien Sabe Glacier avoiding crevasses to the Sahale-Boston col
Steep, exposed, loose ledges directly to the summit

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Gravel beyond mile 10; typically opens to the Cascade Pass Trailhead by late June (sometimes early July)
road.seasonalGate: Subject to winter/off-season and maintenance closures; foot/bicycle travel allowed past closed gates
road.driveNote: First 10 miles paved; final ~3 miles to Cascade Pass Trailhead are narrow with steep drop-offs — high-clearance vehicle recommended

climate.typical: Alpine glaciated terrain; weather windows are short and can change quickly
climate.spring: Heavy snow cover, road likely closed, high avalanche hazard
climate.summer: Primary season (Jul-Sep); best glacier and rock conditions
climate.fall: Shortening season, early snow possible by late Sept/Oct
climate.winter: Road closed, not a winter objective

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS)
access.permit: Backcountry/wilderness permit required year-round for overnight stays in Boston Basin, via recreation.gov reservation or walk-up at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center
access.closures: Boston Basin high-use zone: bear canisters required, no campfires, composting toilet, 12-person group/site limits

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/early morning from Boston Basin camp to cross the glacier before the rock and snow warm up

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (360) 428-3211 (911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Drive, Sedro-Woolley, WA 98284
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in Boston Basin; carry a satellite communicator. Climbing rangers at the Marblemount WIC can advise on current glacier/rockfall conditions before your trip.

routes.corrections: The routeId ('wa_boston_peak_southwest_face') and the provided routeName ('Southeast Face') conflict on aspect. Every reputable source found (The Mountaineers' official program listing, a 2013 cascadeclimbers.com trip report title, and general peak-aspect descriptions) names this route 'Southeast Face via Quien Sabe Glacier' — i.e., the face itself faces east/southeast even though the Quien Sabe Glacier crossed on approach lies on the peak's west/southwest flank per Wikipedia's glacier description. Recommend the database routeId eventually be corrected to reflect 'southeast_face' to match the sourced route name; kept routeName as given ('Southeast Face') since that matches the sourced literature.

blurb: Mount Deception (7,788 ft) is the second-highest peak in Washington's Olympic Mountains, behind only Mount Olympus, and the highest point of the eastern Olympics — sitting at the head of Royal Basin in Olympic National Park, Jefferson County. It is built of Eocene basalt (including pillow lava) and clastic wedge/oceanic-crust material typical of the obducted Olympic core, carries two small glaciers on its north-facing slopes, and was renamed from the original "Mount Holmes" (honoring a Boston Herald journalist on the 1889-90 Seattle Press Expedition) because climbers had such difficulty locating routes on the often cloud-shrouded summit.

routes.overview: The standard line follows the Royal Basin trail to Royal Lake, continues into upper Royal Basin, then climbs a glacial moraine to steep, loose Class 2-3 terrain on the mountain's northeast side to reach the summit ridge.
routes.beta: A long approach-heavy alpine scramble rather than a technical climb: strenuous trail miles to Royal Basin followed by route-finding-intensive scrambling on loose rock and (early season) steep snow. Difficulty is driven by navigation and terrain, not a single technical crux; the peak's reputation for being hard to 'find a way up' is the origin of its name.
routes.approach: From the Upper Dungeness Trailhead, follow the Royal Basin Trail approximately 7.2 miles (gaining roughly 2,550 ft) to Royal Lake at about 5,100 ft. Continue about 1 more mile into upper Royal Basin, then leave the trail to cross glacial moraine/talus toward the base of the peak's northeast-side slopes before beginning the Class 2-3 scramble to the summit ridge.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent route back through upper Royal Basin to Royal Lake and out the Royal Basin Trail to the Upper Dungeness Trailhead.
routes.descentText: No distinct descent line is documented; parties descend the same loose Class 2-3 slopes and moraine used on ascent, moving carefully to avoid kicking rock onto climbers below, then follow the maintained trail back out from Royal Lake.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat this as a multi-day trip: hike in and camp in Royal Basin (Royal Creek, Lower Royal Meadow, or Royal Lake designated sites) on day one, climb and return to camp on day two, hike out on day three. Very strong parties have completed it as a single long day.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and route-finding-dependent scrambling, set a firm turnaround time before you leave camp and stick to it — deteriorating weather or losing the route on cloud-covered upper slopes is the primary reported hazard, and reversing loose Class 2-3 terrain in the dark or in a whiteout is far more dangerous than climbing it.
routes.bestSeason: July through September is the most reliable window, when the upper mountain has mostly shed its snow and becomes a loose rock scramble; in June and early July the route is more of a steep snow climb requiring an ice axe and crampons. The Royal Basin quota/permit season runs roughly May 1 through September 30.
routes.face: Northeast side / upper Royal Basin headwall
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection; this is an unroped scramble. Carry an ice axe (and crampons in early season) for the steep snow that persists into early summer, and treat the upper mountain's loose rock with the same caution as a technical route regarding rockfall onto parties below.
gear: Bear canister (required for camping in Royal Basin)
whatToBring: Map/compass or GPS — route-finding is the crux
Multi-day backpacking kit if camping in Royal Basin
Extra layers for exposed alpine terrain
proTips: Route-finding, not technical difficulty, is what turns parties back — the peak earned its name from how hard early climbers found it to spot a line up from below.
Camp in Royal Basin and climb light and fast from upper basin rather than carrying full packs onto the mountain.
Expect the character of the route to shift from a steep snow climb in June to a loose rock scramble by mid-to-late summer.
watchOut: Loose, poor-quality rock (pillow lava) on the upper slopes — significant rockfall hazard to parties below
Persistent steep snow/névé into early summer requiring self-arrest skills
The summit is frequently cloud-covered, making route-finding difficult and disorienting
knownHazards: The National Park Service notes fatalities have occurred on Mount Deception and recommends climbers be experienced in self-arrest, rock climbing, and route-finding.
A multi-person helicopter rescue has been reported on the mountain, underscoring the seriousness of the terrain despite its 'scramble' classification.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime Pacific Northwest alpine climate typical of the Olympics' east side, drier than the range's west-side rainforest valleys but still snow-heavy in winter.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack lingering into early summer; trail access to Royal Basin may still involve snow travel.
climate.summer: Driest and most stable period (Jul-Sep); upper mountain sheds snow and becomes a loose talus/scree scramble.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing storm frequency by October.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche-prone conditions make the approach and climb a serious mountaineering undertaking.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (NPS) / Olympic National Forest for the trailhead
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass required to park at the Upper Dungeness Trailhead
access.permit: Wilderness camping permit required year-round for overnight stays in Royal Basin, booked via recreation.gov; Royal Basin is quota-limited roughly May 1-Sept 30
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass (trailhead parking)
access.closures: Campfires prohibited above 3,500 ft; camping restricted to designated sites (Royal Creek, Lower Royal Meadow, Royal Lake); bear canisters required

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Jefferson County Sheriff's Office, 360-385-3831 (non-emergency); call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center, 360-565-3130, 600 E. Park Ave, Port Angeles, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Jefferson Healthcare, 834 Sheridan Street, Port Townsend, WA
emergency.notes: Search and rescue in this area may be coordinated by the Jefferson County Sheriff's Office and/or Olympic National Park rangers depending on exact location relative to park boundary.

routes.overview: Also known as the Northeast Couloir or Northeast Chute, this is a steeper, more direct alternative to the standard scramble, climbing a prominent NE-facing snow couloir from upper Royal Basin straight to the upper mountain.
routes.beta: A steep snow couloir climb on the mountain's northeast aspect, reported by climbers as a direct, sustained line taking roughly 3 hours from upper Royal Lake Basin to the top of the couloir in good snow conditions. It sees a narrow seasonal window — climbed successfully in both an early-summer trip report and at least one winter ascent — and functions as the technical/snow-climbing counterpart to the standard rock scramble.
routes.approach: Same approach as the standard route: Upper Dungeness Trailhead to Royal Lake (~7.2 mi) and on into upper Royal Basin, then trend toward the base of the northeast couloir rather than the standard scramble slopes.
routes.descent: Descend via the same couloir or the standard scramble route back to upper Royal Basin, depending on snow conditions, then retrace the Royal Basin Trail out.
routes.descentText: Climbers typically downclimb or glissade the couloir if snow conditions allow, or traverse to descend via the standard route's looser terrain; either way the exit funnels back through upper Royal Basin to Royal Lake and the maintained trail.
routes.itinerary: As with the standard route, most parties camp in Royal Basin and climb the couloir as a day mission from camp; at least one recorded ascent was completed as part of a long winter day trip.
routes.turnaround: Snow condition and avalanche hazard, not a fixed clock time, should dictate turnaround — climbers report only a small reliable weather/snow window for this line each spring, so be prepared to retreat if the couloir shows signs of instability or the snow has gone rotten.
routes.bestSeason: Best in a narrow spring window when the couloir holds consolidated snow; one trip report specifically notes the route 'usually has a small window in the spring.' A winter ascent has also been documented, and an early-summer (late June) ascent was reported, but conditions are more variable outside spring.
routes.proNeeds: Standard steep-snow-climbing protection: ice axe and often a second tool, crampons, and a helmet; this is an unroped or occasionally short-roped snow climb rather than a pitched, protected route in the trip reports found.
whatToBring: Wilderness camping permit if overnighting in Royal Basin
Avalanche awareness / current snow conditions check before committing
Navigation tools for the cross-country approach to the couloir base
proTips: One climber described the route as 'very steep and fun,' taking about 3 hours from upper Royal Lake Basin to the top.
The couloir's reliable window is short and spring-specific — check recent trip reports for current conditions before planning around it.
watchOut: Narrow, condition-dependent climbing window (spring)
Steep, sustained snow with real fall consequences
Rockfall/avalanche potential as the couloir warms through the day
knownHazards: Steep, sustained snow climbing with self-arrest consequences if conditions are firm or icy.
Avalanche and wet-slide potential given the couloir's narrow reliable window and orientation.

climate.typical: Maritime Pacific Northwest alpine climate on the Olympics' northeast side.
climate.spring: Best climbing window for this couloir when snow is consolidated; still avalanche-prone in unsettled weather.
climate.summer: Couloir snow deteriorates and can melt out or go rotten by mid-summer.
climate.fall: Not typically climbed as a snow route once the couloir has melted out.
climate.winter: Full winter conditions with serious avalanche hazard; documented but a committing undertaking given the long approach.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (NPS) / Olympic National Forest for the trailhead
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass required to park at the Upper Dungeness Trailhead
access.permit: Wilderness camping permit required for overnight stays in Royal Basin via recreation.gov
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass (trailhead parking)
access.closures: Campfires prohibited above 3,500 ft; camping restricted to designated Royal Basin sites

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Jefferson County Sheriff's Office, 360-385-3831 (non-emergency); call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center, 360-565-3130, 600 E. Park Ave, Port Angeles, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Jefferson Healthcare, 834 Sheridan Street, Port Townsend, WA
emergency.notes: Same SAR coverage as the standard route: Jefferson County Sheriff and/or Olympic National Park rangers depending on location.

routes.corrections: The 7,861 ft gain and 21.94 mi round-trip figure for this route come from a single winter ascent trip report (Facebook), and the ~3-hour couloir time from a separate solo-climb video; both are sourced but weaker than the multi-source standard-route data, included here as the best available figures rather than an estimate.

blurb: The Castle is a minor rock summit in the Tatoosh Range of Mount Rainier National Park, sitting about 0.2 mile immediately east of Pinnacle Peak along the same volcanic ridgeline between Pinnacle and Unicorn Peak. Built of the andesitic volcanic rock typical of the Tatoosh Range, it is a small, aesthetic pinnacle offering short, easy fifth-class scrambling/climbing rather than a sustained technical objective, with views of Mount Rainier, Mount Adams, and Mount St. Helens from its summit block. No documented first-ascent party or date has been located for the peak.

routes.overview: A short (~40 ft), easy fifth-class scramble/climb up The Castle's southeast aspect to its small summit pinnacle — one of several similar short lines on this face, reached from a bench/meadow below.
routes.beta: The summit of The Castle is most easily reached via a handful of short, easy 5th-class lines on the peak's SE aspect. Climbers gain a meadow/bench at roughly 6,200 ft below the face, then scramble to steeper terrain and pick a line; options further east on this aspect tend to be easier than climbing more directly. The small summit block holds a couple of climbers and has fixed slings for a short rappel back to the base.
routes.approach: From the Reflection Lake trailhead on Stevens Canyon Road, follow the Pinnacle Saddle Trail about 1.3 miles (roughly 1,050 ft of gain) to the Pinnacle-Plummer saddle. From the saddle, the easiest approach follows a user trail below (south of) Pinnacle Peak's south face, then either scrambles directly up and over the small connecting ridge or drops around it to the south to reach The Castle's SE meadow/bench (~6,200 ft) below the climbing. In winter, access instead starts from the Narada Falls parking area, a longer approach on snow.
routes.descent: Downclimb the easy 5th-class line, or make a single short (~40 ft) rappel from fixed slings at the summit.
routes.descentText: The summit pinnacle is small but can hold a couple of climbers at once; fixed slings are in place for a single-rope rappel of roughly 40 feet back to the base of the climb. Parties comfortable on low-5th-class terrain can also downclimb the ascent line. From the base, reverse the approach across the SE bench and scree/talus below Pinnacle's south face back to the Pinnacle-Plummer saddle, then descend the Pinnacle Saddle Trail to the Reflection Lake trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a single day from Reflection Lake (or combined with Pinnacle Peak and/or Plummer Peak as a linkup): hike the Pinnacle Saddle Trail to the Pinnacle-Plummer saddle, traverse below Pinnacle's south face to The Castle's SE bench, scramble/climb the short SE-aspect line to the summit, rappel or downclimb, then reverse the approach back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at any point — downclimb back to the SE bench and reverse the short approach traverse to the Pinnacle-Plummer saddle and trailhead; there is no long commitment above the initial scramble.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach crosses open, exposed scree/talus and can hold lingering snow into early summer, turn back if visibility drops, weather deteriorates, or the traverse feels unsafe without proper footing/an ice axe — the short technical section is easily reversed, so retreating costs little time or effort.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in summer, roughly July through September, once snow has cleared from the approach traverse and the SE bench below the face. Early-season parties may still encounter snow patches on the contour from the Pinnacle-Plummer saddle and should carry an ice axe.
routes.fa: Not documented (Mountain Project lists first ascent as unknown)
routes.rockGrade: Easy 5th (French 1+ / Ewbanks 3 / UIAA I / ZA 5 / British M 1c)
routes.rappels: Single ~40 ft rappel from fixed slings at the summit
routes.detailedRack: A small rack of finger-to-hand-sized trad gear (small-to-mid-size cams and a few stoppers) is enough to protect the short crux moves; the rappel anchor is pre-rigged fixed slings on the summit block, so no gear is needed to build a descent anchor.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needed — a handful of finger-to-hand-sized cams/stoppers cover the low-5th crux moves on this short route.
gear: Sturdy approach shoes or light boots
whatToBring: Ice axe/traction if snow lingers on the saddle traverse
Sun protection (open, exposed approach and summit)
Extra layer for wind on the exposed ridge
Map/GPS for the Pinnacle-Plummer traverse route-finding
proTips: Options further east along the SE aspect tend to be easier than climbing the line most directly
The summit block is small but can hold a couple of climbers at once
Reaching the SE meadow/bench at ~6,200 ft directly avoids some of the scree traverse below Pinnacle's south face
Often combined with Pinnacle Peak and/or Plummer Peak as a single-day linkup
watchOut: Loose, unstable volcanic rock typical of the Tatoosh Range
Old snow can linger on the saddle traverse into mid-summer
Exposed scree/talus below Pinnacle's south face is engaging, especially with a pack
knownHazards: Loose rock on both the approach and the short climb
Residual snow/ice on shaded traverse sections into early summer
Steep scree and talus present a slip hazard even though no glacier travel is involved

pitchDetail.notes: Short ~40 ft scramble/climb up the SE face to the summit block; finger-to-hand gear protects the crux; lines further east on this aspect are easier.

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road
road.status: Open for the 2026 summer season (confirmed open as of early July 2026)
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed to vehicles in winter due to snow; tire chains required park-wide Nov 1 - May 1
road.driveNote: Reached via Longmire or Paradise; park at the Reflection Lake trailhead. In winter, park instead at Narada Falls and approach on snow.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy orographic precipitation; summers are typically the clearest, driest window and winters are mostly cloudy with heavy, wet snowfall.
climate.spring: Lingering snow, especially on shaded terrain and the Pinnacle-Plummer saddle traverse.
climate.summer: Generally the driest and clearest season, roughly July-September.
climate.fall: Increasing storm frequency; early-season snow possible by October.
climate.winter: Heavy, wet snowfall closes vehicle access to Reflection Lake; approach shifts to Narada Falls with a much longer, snow-travel approach.

access.landManager: National Park Service - Mount Rainier National Park
access.fees: Park entrance fee: $30/vehicle (7-day) or $15/person walk-in/bicycle; no cash accepted at entrance stations.
access.permit: No climbing permit required for this non-glaciated scramble (NPS requires a permit only on glaciers or above ~10,000 ft high camps); no overnight wilderness permit needed for a day ascent.
access.passRequired: America the Beautiful interagency pass accepted in lieu of the entrance fee.
access.closures: Stevens Canyon Road / Reflection Lake access is closed seasonally in winter; check the NPS road status page before visiting.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, to allow a full day for the approach traverse, the short technical scramble, and the return before afternoon weather builds.

emergency.county: Lewis County, WA (the summit sits near the Lewis/Pierce county line within Mount Rainier National Park)
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier NP main dispatch/visitor information: 360-569-2211 (Longmire Wilderness Information Center / Paradise Ranger Station)
emergency.notes: Mount Rainier NP rangers handle search-and-rescue within park boundaries; cell coverage is unreliable across the Tatoosh Range backcountry.

routes.corrections: Given elevationFt was null. Sources disagree on The Castle's elevation: Mountain Project lists 6,393 ft; Wikipedia's dedicated page lists 6,440 ft (with a paired prominence of 200 ft); the Wikipedia 'Tatoosh Range' list article gives 6,460 ft; willhiteweb.com states 6,640 ft. Used Wikipedia's 6,440 ft/200 ft prominence figure (the only source pairing elevation with a specific prominence value) as the best-supported estimate for highPointFt, but flagging the roughly 250 ft spread across sources for review. Peakbagger.com and SummitPost.org (both required primary sources) could not be retrieved despite repeated attempts (persistent HTTP 403 blocks via multiple direct and proxy fetch attempts); cross-checking relied instead on Mountain Project, Wikipedia, NPS, willhiteweb.com, Mazamas, and cascadeclimber.com.

blurb: Silver Star Mountain (8,876 ft) is the highest and most prominent peak in the Washington Pass area of the North Cascades, rising about four miles northeast of the pass in the granite of the Golden Horn batholith. It carries east and west summits and stands alongside the adjacent Wine Spires (Burgundy, Chablis, Chianti, Pernod, Whine Spire, etc.), offering everything from a moderate glacier walk-up to hard technical rock and mixed lines. Lage Wernstedt made the first recorded ascent in 1926; Fred Beckey's party opened the now-standard Silver Star Glacier route in May 1952.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Joe Heib, Herb Staley, and Don Wilde, May 1952
routes.rockGrade: 4th class (low-5th class scrambling near the summit)
objHaz: icy glacier surface in late season
routes.overview: The standard route up Silver Star Mountain: a moderate glacier crossing on the peak's north side from Burgundy Col, finishing with an airy, exposed scramble to the summit.
routes.beta: From Burgundy Col, downclimb steep snow onto the glacier and head east toward the rock leading to the summit. The final approach transitions onto rock with scrambling from 3rd class up to low-5th class; the true summit is airy and exposed 4th-to-low-5th class.
routes.approach: Two main approaches are used. Burgundy Col (primary): start at Highway 20 near mile marker 166 (about 4 miles east of Washington Pass), cross the creek on a log, and follow a climbers' trail gaining roughly 3,500 feet through forest to Burgundy Col, then downclimb steep snow to reach the glacier. Silver Star Creek (alternative, best in early season): stays on the east side of the mountain the entire way, avoiding the col climb.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back over Burgundy Col to the Highway 20 trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the scrambling terrain back onto the Silver Star Glacier, cross back to Burgundy Col, then descend the col's steep snow and the climbers' trail to the trailhead near mile marker 166 on Highway 20.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed June through August, when the glacier holds continuous consolidated snow. A late-June trip report described the glacier still snow-covered with a short handline used on the descent, while other parties note the glacier becomes 'very icy' later in the season, so earlier-summer timing is generally preferred.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier travel kit (rope, crampons, ice axe/tools); most parties carry at most a light rock rack for the low-5th-class scrambling to the summit.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel protection as conditions dictate (pickets); a light rack is optional for the short summit scramble.
whatToBring: sturdy boots/approach shoes for scree and scrambling
proTips: Go earlier in summer for continuous snow coverage on the glacier and approach — parties report the glacier turns very icy by late season.
The Silver Star Creek approach avoids the Burgundy Col climb entirely and stays on the mountain's east side the whole way, a useful early-season alternative.
watchOut: The final summit block is airy, exposed 4th-to-low-5th-class terrain.
Loose rock is present on the descent trail back to treeline.
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Silver Star Glacier.
Steep snow on the descent off Burgundy Col.
Glacier surface can become icy and firm in late season.

pitchDetail.notes: Highway 20 (MM 166) to Burgundy Col, roughly 3,500 ft gain
Downclimb from the col onto the glacier, travel east
Exposed, airy summit block

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20)
road.seasonalGate: Gated for winter at Silver Star gate, milepost 171, typically closing early December and reopening mid-to-late April
road.driveNote: Park along SR 20 near mile marker 166, about 4 miles east of Washington Pass.

climate.summer: Generally warm, stable bluebird conditions in June-August per trip reports
climate.winter: Highway 20 closed; trailhead access not possible

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.closures: SR 20 (North Cascades Highway) is gated for winter roughly December through April, cutting off vehicle access to the trailhead

routes.corrections: Could not verify a documented 'Northeast Ridge' route on Silver Star Mountain across any checked source (Mountain Project's full area/sub-area route listing, the American Alpine Journal database, Cascade Climbers forum search, climberkyle.com, or SummitPost snippets). The documented named lines on this peak are: Silver Star Glacier (standard route, north side), East Ridge (5.9+ Grade V, via Cedar Creek), West Face Couloir (AI2 M4), Northwest Face (IV WI4+ M6-, on the west summit), Central Couloir, and Gato Negro (IV 5.10+, on adjacent Whine Spire). This route entry could not be substantiated as a distinct, separately documented line online; recommend confirming its existence/identity against a physical Beckey Cascade Alpine Guide or Selected Climbs in the Cascades before further enrichment. Left unfilled rather than guessing to avoid fabricating grades, FA, or approach beta for an unverified route.

routes.fa: Geoff Childs and Larry Goldie, September 14, 2000 (soloed on the first ascent, bypassing some ridge features, and originally graded 5.8 IV)
objHaz: sustained route-finding on the ridge crest
routes.overview: A long, continuously exposed ridge line from the Cedar Creek Trailhead over several sub-summits to Silver Star's main summit; often overlooked, rated 5.9+ Grade V if roped continuously, easier if the party solos or short-ropes the more moderate sections.
routes.beta: From the Cedar Creek Trailhead, gain the ridge early and follow its crest essentially the whole way to the summit, passing several sub-summits along the way. Route-finding on loose rock is the main challenge; difficulty rises to 5.9+ V or harder if a party ropes up earlier or maintains continuous contact with the ridge crest.
routes.approach: Start at the Cedar Creek Trailhead. A few hundred feet up the main trail, turn right onto the old horse-packers' trail shown on Forest Service maps, then leave the trail to gain and follow the ridge for the remainder of the route to the summit.
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine rock rack for 5.9+ terrain; carrying spare cord to back up rappel anchors is recommended by the route's documentation.
routes.proNeeds: Standard rack sized for sustained 5.9+ ridge climbing; supplemental cord/webbing for anchor backups is worth carrying.
whatToBring: approach shoes suited to ridge scrambling
proTips: Stay as close to the ridge crest as possible — per the first-ascent party, that is the best route-finding beta.
The route can be climbed quickly by soloing/scrambling easier sections at roughly 5.8 IV, or fully pitched out at a more sustained 5.9+ V.
watchOut: Loose rock is present throughout the ridge.
The ridge is long, remote, and demands solid route-finding through multiple sub-summits.
knownHazards: Loose, sometimes rotten rock typical of North Cascades alpine ridges.
Sustained, committing position along the ridge with limited retreat options.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20)
road.seasonalGate: Gated for winter at Silver Star gate, milepost 171, typically closing early December and reopening mid-to-late April
road.driveNote: Access via the Cedar Creek Trailhead off SR 20 near Washington Pass.

climate.winter: Highway 20 closed; trailhead access not possible

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.closures: SR 20 (North Cascades Highway) is gated for winter roughly December through April

hierarchyNote: Confirmed: coordinates and name match — Wikipedia lists Mount Constance at 47°46'22"N 123°07'38"W (=47.7728,-123.1274) and 7,756 ft, identical to the given data, and Eastern Olympic Mountains is geographically correct (Constance is the highest peak on the range's eastern crest above Hood Canal/Brinnon). One minor discrepancy worth flagging: several independent sources (SummitPost, WillhiteWeb, Olympic Peninsula tourism site, multiple trip reports) cite 7,743 ft rather than 7,756 ft for the same summit — likely an older USGS figure vs. a newer LiDAR-derived one, not a different peak. Not changing the given elevation since Wikipedia and the given data agree, but noting the split in the literature.
blurb: Mount Constance (7,756 ft) is the third-highest peak in Washington's Olympic Mountains and the highest point on the range's eastern escarpment above Hood Canal, rising more than 6,900 ft above the Dosewallips River in under 3 horizontal miles. It is built of loose, often rotten Eocene basalt — obducted oceanic crust characteristic of the Olympics' geologic core — which gives its class 3 to mid-class 5 routes a reputation for sustained exposure and rockfall rather than clean rock. The peak was first climbed in 1922 by R. Schellin and A. E. Smith, approaching from the southeast.

routes.overview: The standard line up Mount Constance: from Lake Constance the route climbs Avalanche Canyon and the South Chute to a notch below the summit ridge, crosses the exposed Finger and Terrible Traverses along class 3-4 terrain, and finishes through a chimney on the north side of the summit block to the true summit.
routes.beta: Grade II, mostly class 3 with sustained exposure and occasional class 4/low 5th-class moves near the top; a rope is commonly carried for the traverses and the final chimney even though most parties climb it unroped. Loose basalt makes rockfall the dominant hazard the entire way.
routes.approach: From US 101 at Brinnon, drive Dosewallips Road (FR 2610) roughly 13 miles; the last stretch has been closed to vehicles since a January 2002 washout, so most parties park at the washout (~5 miles short of the old ranger station/trailhead) and walk or bike the closed road. From the road's end, the Lake Constance Trail climbs a brutally steep ~1.8-2.2 miles and about 3,200-3,300 ft (rated one of the steepest trails in the Olympics) to Lake Constance, the usual base camp. From the lake, traverse into Avalanche Canyon — a large rock formation called 'the Thumb' marks the entrance to the South Chute — and climb roughly 1,900 ft of loose scree/talus in the chute to about 6,600 ft.
routes.descent: Down-climb the same route: reverse the North Chimney, Terrible Traverse and Finger Traverse, descend the South Chute and Avalanche Canyon back to Lake Constance, then hike/bike out the closed road.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse through the North Chimney to the upper ridge, then re-cross the Terrible Traverse and Finger Traverse (or take the class-4 bypass around the Finger Traverse). Downclimb the scree gully to the saddle, regain the ~150 ft lost there on the way up, then descend the South Chute's loose scree/talus back into Avalanche Canyon and down to Lake Constance camp. Snow patches often linger in the upper canyon into midsummer and can make the descent faster (glissade) or more hazardous depending on conditions.
routes.itinerary: Typical plan is 2 days: Day 1 — hike the closed Dosewallips Road and Lake Constance Trail to a camp at Lake Constance (permit required). Day 2 — pre-dawn start for the summit push via Avalanche Canyon, South Chute, the two traverses and North Chimney, then return to camp and hike out the same day or on Day 3.
routes.bail: The route has no real alternative descent — retreat means reversing the line back down through the traverses and South Chute to Lake Constance. Bail before committing above the notch/traverses if weather, daylight, or party ability are in question, since down-climbing the exposed traverses in poor conditions is the main source of accidents.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux terrain (traverses and chimney) is exposed, loose, and slower to reverse than to climb, set a hard turnaround based on how much daylight is left to safely re-cross the traverses and descend the scree of the South Chute — not a fixed clock time — and turn around early if weather or route-finding is in doubt.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the reliable window once the South Chute and traverses are snow-free; earlier in the season the route becomes a snow climb requiring an ice axe and crampons on the chute and gullies. Conditions vary significantly year to year with residual snowpack.
routes.detailedRack: Many parties carry a rope, harnesses, and a small rack (slings/a few pieces) for the traverses and chimney as a safety net even though the terrain is typically climbed unroped by experienced scramblers; helmets are considered mandatory given constant rockfall.
whatToBring: Bear canister or approved food storage
Water treatment (limited water above Lake Constance)
Bike (optional, for the closed road approach)
proTips: Use 'the Thumb' rock formation in Avalanche Canyon as the landmark for entering the South Chute.
A 4th-class bypass exists on the west side of the Finger Traverse for parties wanting to avoid its exposure.
Biking the closed lower Dosewallips Road saves significant time and energy versus walking it.
watchOut: Constant rockfall on loose Olympic basalt, especially in the South Chute and gullies.
The Finger and Terrible Traverses are exposed above sheer drops and can be wet, icy, or snow-covered well into summer.
Route-finding is difficult in fog or low visibility on the upper ridge.
knownHazards: Loose, rotten basalt rockfall throughout the route.
Exposed, down-sloping ledge traverses (Finger Traverse, Terrible Traverse) above significant drops.
Lingering snow/ice in the South Chute and summit gullies into early summer.
No maintained ranger presence in the Dosewallips drainage since the 2002 road washout.

pitchDetail.notes: Closed Dosewallips Road to the Lake Constance Trailhead, then the steep trail (~3,200-3,300 ft in ~2 miles) to Lake Constance.
Traverse from the lake into Avalanche Canyon; 'the Thumb' rock marks the entrance to the South Chute.
~1,900 ft of loose scree/talus up the chute to roughly 6,600 ft.
Cross a saddle, descend ~150 ft, then climb a scree gully to a notch on the summit ridge.
Down-sloping ledge above a sheer drop, worked with finger holds; class-4 bypass available to the west.
A second exposed ledge/tower traverse on the summit ridge; passability varies with snow/ice cover by season.
Chimney on the north side of the summit block leads to the final short scramble to the true summit.

road.name: Dosewallips Road (FR 2610)
road.status: Closed to vehicles beyond the January 2002 washout
road.driveNote: From US 101 at Brinnon, follow Dosewallips Rd/FR 2610 about 13 miles toward the trailhead; the final several miles are closed past the washout and must be walked or biked. Call the ranger station for current road/trail conditions before heading out.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime mountain climate typical of the Olympics' eastern crest, with a distinct drier window in mid-late summer.
climate.spring: Heavy residual snowpack on the upper route into June; approach trail can have snow/blowdown.
climate.summer: The reliable climbing window (roughly July-September) once the traverses and chute are largely snow-free.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows and earlier snow return by late September/October.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche conditions make the route a full winter mountaineering/avalanche-terrain undertaking, not attempted as a scramble.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (upper route, Lake Constance) / Olympic National Forest (lower Dosewallips Rd corridor)
access.fees: Olympic National Park entrance fee or America the Beautiful pass
access.permit: Quota wilderness camping permit required for overnight stays at Lake Constance, reserved via recreation.gov in the summer season; self-issue permits used outside the quota period.
access.passRequired: NPS entrance pass plus wilderness camping permit
access.closures: Dosewallips Road closed to vehicle traffic beyond the 2002 washout, roughly 5 miles below the historic trailhead/ranger station.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn start from a Lake Constance camp, given the length and exposure of the technical terrain high on the route.

routes.corrections: Given elevation (7,756 ft) matches Wikipedia and is retained, but note several other sources (SummitPost, WillhiteWeb, trip reports) cite 7,743 ft for the same summit.
routes.face: Summit block, finished via a chimney on its north side

routes.overview: A named crux ledge on Mount Constance's standard/summit-ridge route: a down-sloping ledge crossed above a sheer drop, using finger jams worked into a crack, encountered en route between the South Chute notch and the North Chimney.
routes.beta: Not an independent line to the summit but the signature exposed move of the standard route — described in guide sources with an SDI-style difficulty of roughly T-3/E-3/S-3/R-3 (technical class 3, high exposure). A class-4 bypass exists on the west side for parties wanting to avoid the ledge itself.
routes.approach: Same as the standard route: Dosewallips Road washout to the Lake Constance Trail, Avalanche Canyon and the South Chute to the notch below the summit ridge — the Finger Traverse is encountered on the upper ridge beyond the notch, before the Terrible Traverse and North Chimney.
routes.descent: Reverse the traverse (or use the class-4 bypass) back to the notch, then descend the South Chute and Avalanche Canyon to Lake Constance.
routes.descentText: Down-climb back across the ledge in the same finger-jam style, or take the west-side class-4 bypass if conditions (wet rock, snow) make the traverse itself feel harder than the bypass; continue down the scree gully, saddle, South Chute and Avalanche Canyon to Lake Constance.
routes.bail: Reverse the ledge crossing (or use the class-4 bypass) back toward the notch and South Chute; there is no separate rappel/escape option specific to this section.
routes.turnaround: Treat the traverse as a go/no-go point: if it is wet, snow-covered, or the party is unsure of down-climbing it later, turn back before committing further along the ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July-September when the ledge is dry; can be wet, icy, or partly snow-covered outside that window.
gear: Sticky-soled approach or rock shoes helpful for the ledge's fingerholds
proTips: A class-4 bypass exists to the west of the Finger Traverse for parties wanting to avoid its exposure.
Dry, dusty basalt gives noticeably better friction on the ledge than wet or lichen-covered rock.
watchOut: Down-sloping ledge above a sheer drop with limited margin for error.
Can be wet or snow/ice covered well into early summer.
knownHazards: Severe exposure above a sheer drop
Loose or wet holds on the ledge
Possible snow/ice cover reducing friction
routes.face: Upper summit ridge, between the South Chute notch and the North Chimney

routes.overview: A distinct alpine rock route up the prominent ridge rising directly above Crystal Pass, at the head of Avalanche Canyon — mostly broken class 2-3 ridge scrambling with a roped 5th-class crux groove/crack, rejoining the standard summit ridge near the top.
routes.beta: Rated around 5.5 (crux reported as low as 5.4 by some parties), with roughly 1,100 ft of roped/simul climbing above Crystal Pass. Lower sections are simul-climbable class 2-3 with occasional 4th-class steps; the crux is a vertical crack/groove protected by a fixed piton, followed by ledge traverses, an open-book wall section, and sometimes a snow-covered slab hand-traverse before easier talus leads to the summit horn.
routes.approach: From the Dosewallips Road washout, hike/bike to the Lake Constance Trailhead, then climb the steep ~2.2-mile, ~3,250-ft trail to Lake Constance. From the lake, travel roughly 1.5 miles through Avalanche Canyon to Crystal Pass at its head; the West Arête route begins above the pass after traversing around a small tower.
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the standard route: continue over the summit ridge across the Finger and Terrible Traverses, then down the South Chute through the (often snow-filled) upper gullies back to Lake Constance.
routes.descentText: From the top of the arête, join the standard summit-ridge route and reverse it: class 3-4 scrambling across the Finger Traverse and Terrible Traverse (or the bypass), then descend the South Chute — frequently retaining snowfields well into the season — back down into Avalanche Canyon and to Lake Constance camp. Parties starting pre-dawn have reported the final ~3,600 ft of descent to camp finishing after dark.
routes.itinerary: A long single push is common: pre-dawn (roughly 5:30 AM) start from Lake Constance, climb to and up the arête, traverse the summit ridge, and descend the South Chute back to camp, often finishing well after dark (car-to-car round trips of around 19 hours have been reported, including the road-biking approach).
routes.bail: Retreat below the crux groove is difficult once committed; the safest bail is before starting up the technical rock above Crystal Pass. Above the crux, continuing to the summit and descending the standard route is generally more practical than reversing the arête.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach, technical crux, and difficult-to-reverse terrain, start pre-dawn and set a turnaround based on remaining daylight for the summit-ridge traverses and South Chute descent — this is a long enough day that finishing in darkness is common even for parties that start early.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable roughly June through September; early-season (June) ascents encounter snow still covering the scree approach and possibly the upper ledges, adding difficulty and requiring care on the hand-traverse sections.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine rock rack of small-to-medium cams and stoppers on a 50m rope; a fixed piton protects the crux crack. Bivy gear is a sensible addition given the length of the day.
routes.proNeeds: Small to medium cams and chocks; a fixed piton is present at the crux but should not be trusted as the sole protection.
gear: Ice axe (early season snow on approach/ledges)
whatToBring: Bivy gear (in case of a late finish)
Headlamp (long days often finish after dark)
proTips: Start pre-dawn (around 5:30 AM) from Lake Constance given the length of the round trip.
Expect the crux groove/crack to be the technical bar for the route (5.4-5.5); everything else is class 2-4.
Bring a headlamp — round trips commonly finish after dark even with an early start.
watchOut: Route-finding difficulty on the arête, especially in fog.
Wet slab and snow-covered ledges near the crux can be greasy or require a hand traverse on snow.
Long, committing day — descent via the standard route's traverses and South Chute is itself serious terrain.
knownHazards: Exposed scrambling above significant drops on the ridge
Soft snow causing wet slides on ledges near the crux
Icing/wet rock reducing friction on the crux groove
Loose rock typical of Olympic basalt

pitchDetail.notes: Via Avalanche Canyon to Crystal Pass at the head of the canyon, then traverse around a small tower to the base of the arête.
Rising traverse on the right side of the ridge crest; broken terrain, mostly simul-climbable.
Vertical crack/groove crux with a fixed piton for protection.
Right-trending then left-trending ledges, an open-book wall, sometimes a snow-covered slab hand-traverse.
Talus scrambling to the summit horn, joining the standard route's summit ridge.

road.name: Dosewallips Road (FR 2610)
road.status: Closed to vehicles beyond the January 2002 washout
road.driveNote: Same approach as the standard route: park at the washout on US 101/FR 2610 near Brinnon and hike or bike the closed road to the Lake Constance Trailhead.

climate.typical: Wet maritime climate typical of the Olympics; a drier window opens in summer.
climate.spring: Snow typically still covers the scree approach and can affect upper ledges into June.
climate.summer: The main climbing season, June through September.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows by late September.
climate.winter: Not a viable season for this route; full winter mountaineering conditions apply.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (upper route) / Olympic National Forest (lower road corridor)
access.fees: Olympic National Park entrance fee or America the Beautiful pass
access.permit: Wilderness camping permit required for an overnight at Lake Constance; quota system via recreation.gov in summer.
access.passRequired: NPS entrance pass plus wilderness camping permit
access.closures: Dosewallips Road closed to vehicles beyond the 2002 washout.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn, around 5:30 AM, from a Lake Constance camp.

routes.face: West Arête, rising above Crystal Pass at the head of Avalanche Canyon

routes.overview: A second named exposed ledge/tower crossing on Mount Constance's standard summit-ridge route, encountered along with the Finger Traverse between the South Chute notch and the North Chimney; its condition (dry ledges vs. lingering snow/ice) varies significantly by season.
routes.beta: Part of the standard route rather than an independent line — class 3-4 exposed ridge terrain around towers, with 'options around towers' reported by parties depending on conditions; late-season ascents have found the ledges 'entirely melted out,' implying the traverse can hold snow/ice earlier in the season.
routes.approach: Same as the standard route: Dosewallips Road washout to Lake Constance, Avalanche Canyon and South Chute to the ridge notch, then along the summit ridge past the Finger Traverse to the Terrible Traverse before the final North Chimney.
routes.descent: Reverse the traverse back toward the notch and South Chute, or use the noted alternate options around the towers, then descend to Lake Constance via the standard route.
routes.descentText: Down-climb back across the exposed ledges/towers in the same style as the ascent, taking whichever of the 'options around towers' best suits current snow/ice conditions, then continue down the scree gully, South Chute and Avalanche Canyon to Lake Constance.
routes.bail: Reverse toward the notch and South Chute; no independent escape route exists for this section.
routes.turnaround: Treat this traverse, like the Finger Traverse, as a go/no-go checkpoint — if it is icy or snow-covered and the party is not comfortable down-climbing it later, turn back before committing further along the ridge.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July-September; earlier-season parties have found the ledges holding snow/ice, while a September ascent found them 'entirely melted out.'
gear: Ice axe (if snow/ice present early season)
proTips: Several 'options around the towers' exist here depending on current snow/ice conditions — scout before committing to one line.
watchOut: Ledges can be snow- or ice-covered well into summer, changing the difficulty significantly from a dry-rock ascent.
Severe exposure around the towers.
knownHazards: Exposure above significant drops around towers
Variable snow/ice cover depending on season and year
Loose rock typical of the peak
routes.face: Upper summit ridge, between the Finger Traverse and the North Chimney

blurb: Dragontail Peak (8,840+ ft) is the second-highest summit in the Stuart Range, part of Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness, rising directly above Colchuck Lake in the Enchantments about 3.2 miles west of Mount Stuart. The peak is built of granite dating to the Cretaceous period, and takes its name from a serrated line of rock needles on its southwest crest that early climbers thought resembled a "dragon tail" (name officially adopted in 1955). Its north face holds some of the range's most sought-after alpine objectives, from moderate snow/ice couloirs to sustained granite ridge and face routes up to 5.12.
hierarchyNote: None — name, coordinates (47.4787,-120.8334 vs. Wikipedia's 47°28'43"N/120°50'00"W), elevation (8,840 ft), and the "Stuart-Enchantments" parent area all cross-check cleanly against Wikipedia, Mountain Project, and WTA.

routes.fa: John Bonneville & Mark Weigelt, 1970 (some sources list "Mark Wright"); "Fin Direct" finish added by Pat Cruver & Kit Lewis, 1975
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in summer on dry rock; the lower scrambling sections and ridge crest can hold snow/ice into early season, and the descent traverse gets icy in shoulder season.
routes.overview: A twelve-pitch, 2,000-foot granite ridge climb up Dragontail's north face crest to a distinctive rock "fin" near the top — one of the most highly rated moderate alpine rock routes in the Enchantments.
routes.beta: Clean granite with some lower-angle scrambling that can be loose in places, climber's left of Serpentine Arete. Key pitches include an offwidth (P2) and finger cracks with layback moves (P4) below the summit fin.
routes.approach: Approach via the Colchuck Lake trail (Stuart Lake TH) to the lake, then around/along the north shore toward the base of Dragontail's north face; the route starts left (west) of Serpentine Arete.
routes.descent: Traverse the ridge crest eastward to easier ground; two rappels are an alternative in icy conditions.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route, descend by traversing/downclimbing the ridge east toward the standard Aasgard Pass descent line. In icy or early-season conditions, parties have used two rappels instead of the ridge traverse.
gear: Crampons/axe if snow lingers on approach or descent
routes.detailedRack: A medium rack to #3.5 Camalot covers most of the route, but a #6 Camalot is specifically required for the offwidth crux pitch (P2). Standard rack for finger/hand cracks on P4.
routes.proNeeds: Solid traditional protection throughout on clean granite; the offwidth pitch is the one non-standard gear requirement (#6 Camalot).
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the trail, swapped for rock shoes/boots at the base
Water/layers for a full alpine day
proTips: Start early — this is one of the most popular moderate routes in the Enchantments and can back up
Bring a #6 Camalot specifically for the P2 offwidth, it's not optional
The summit "fin" near the top is the route's signature feature
watchOut: Lower scrambling sections can be loose
P2 offwidth needs the big cam or it's a rough lead
Descent traverse can be icy in early/late season
knownHazards: Loose rock in the lower scrambling terrain
Afternoon thunderstorms are common over the Stuart Range in summer
Exposure along the ridge crest
routes.turnaround: Retreat from the ridge is straightforward by downclimbing/traversing back the way you came for most of the route; commit to the upper fin section only with stable weather, since the terrain above is exposed and retreat gets harder.
