pitchDetail.notes: Offwidth crux, requires a #6 Camalot.
Finger cracks with layback moves.
Climbs past "the fin," a distinctive rock formation near the summit.

routes.fa: Layton [surname unclear in source] & Eric Wolf, June 27, 2004 (per AAC Publications / American Alpine Journal) — Mountain Project lists "Layton/Wolfe, 2006," which this AAJ trip account contradicts on year
routes.bestSeason: Summer, once the north/northeast face is dry.
routes.overview: A seven-pitch dihedral system on Dragontail's northeast face that joins the older Northeast Arete route roughly two-thirds of the way up to finish.
routes.beta: Start a couple of ridges below Backbone Ridge; look for the clean dihedral a couple of pitches up. The route climbs a sustained corner/dihedral system before merging into the 1972 Northeast Arete route for its final pitches.
routes.approach: Via Colchuck Lake to the base of Dragontail's north/northeast face, similar to the approach for Backbone Ridge and Gerber-Sink.
routes.descentText: Not documented in available sources for this specific route; since it finishes via the Northeast Arete, parties likely use the same Aasgard Pass descent used by neighboring north-face routes, but this is not directly confirmed for Der Dihedral.
routes.detailedRack: Standard rack to 3 inches (per Mountain Project); no unusual gear called out.
routes.proNeeds: Standard granite dihedral/corner protection.
watchOut: Photos/comments reference "beware the belayer slayer" — suggesting rockfall exposure at belays
knownHazards: Rockfall potential noted at at least one belay ("belayer slayer")
Northeast aspect can hold snow/ice later into the season than sun-facing routes
routes.turnaround: Since the route joins the Northeast Arete partway up with an unconfirmed descent, treat any retreat as a full down-lead/rappel of the dihedral system unless you can confirm the upper exit; don't commit above the halfway point without confidence in the finish and descent.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists this as a 6-pitch route first climbed by "Layton/Wolfe" in 2006; the AAC Publications (American Alpine Journal) first-hand account instead dates the FA to June 27, 2004 and describes 7 new pitches. The AAJ account is treated as more authoritative for FA date/pitch count since it's a first-hand report from the climbing party.

routes.fa: Andy Wyatt & Chris Potts, August 2020
routes.face: East Face of the NE Towers
routes.bestSeason: Summer alpine season, once the Aasgard Pass approach snow has consolidated or melted out.
routes.overview: 900+ feet of sustained crack and face climbing on the east-facing side of Dragontail's NE Towers, topping out on the easternmost summit of the towers via the left-hand skyline.
routes.beta: Long pitches (up to 58 m) on excellent, sustained granite with belay ledges roughly every rope-length. The route was newly established and cleaned in August 2020.
routes.approach: Hike to the south side of Colchuck Lake, then follow the Aasgard Pass trail up to roughly 85% of its height (about 7,300-7,400 ft), leaving the trail there and crossing scree/talus to the base of the route.
routes.descent: Scramble 3rd/4th-class terrain southwest to a gully below the final belay, then join the standard Aasgard Pass descent.
routes.descentText: From the top, scramble southwest across 3rd/4th class terrain to a clean gully roughly 500 ft below the final belay. Descend that gully to a large ledge, then join the standard Aasgard Pass descent back to Colchuck Lake. The route is not set up for rappelling.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from .2 to #2 Camalot, singles of .1 and #3, a healthy stopper set, and a good assortment of runners; consider triples from .3-#2 plus a second #3 and a #4 for a fuller rack.
routes.proNeeds: Sustained crack protection on every pitch; doubles in the mid-range sizes are the priority.
whatToBring: Layers — east face can be cold/shaded early
proTips: Leave the Aasgard Pass trail around 7,300-7,400 ft to reach the base
P2's long splitter corner is described as one of the best pitches in the range — the "money pitch"
watchOut: P1 is insecure/flaring 5.10a — the crux comes early
Route not equipped for rappel descent — commit to the scramble descent
knownHazards: Loose talus/scree on the approach traverse off the Aasgard Pass trail
3rd/4th class descent terrain has exposure
routes.turnaround: Because the route isn't set up to rappel, only commit above P1 if you're confident finishing all 5 pitches and completing the 3rd/4th-class descent scramble.

pitchDetail.notes: 58m, main crack system, insecure/flaring - route's technical crux.
55m, sustained corner crack, the standout "money pitch."
45m, broken crack system leading to the upper headwall.
58m, splitter headwall hand crack.
55m, wandering mixed crack/face to the ridge high point.

routes.iceGrade: WI3+ M4 (winter conditions; can reach M5+ in variations)
routes.season: primarily a winter/spring ice-and-mixed route once formed; a summer 4th/low-5th class rock line also exists
routes.bestSeason: Best known as a winter/spring mixed climb (WI3-4, ~M4) when ice forms in the runnels; a lower-key summer rock ascent (4th/low 5th, a couple of 5.7 cruxes) is also possible.
routes.overview: An alpine line on Dragontail's north face between Triple Couloirs and Backbone Ridge that has become a modern winter mixed classic, far more sustained and exposed than nearby Triple Couloirs when ice conditions are good.
routes.beta: Ten pitches ascending roughly 1,000+ feet of ice/neve through runnels into the north-face bowl, then mixed rock-and-ice terrain including an ice chimney, a large snow bowl, and a final thin-ice-over-rock M4 crux.
routes.approach: Via Colchuck Lake to the base of Dragontail's north face, between the Triple Couloirs and Backbone Ridge lines.
routes.descent: Unroped descent via the boot-packed couloir back toward the lake basin, then out via Aasgard Pass (parties have glissaded soft snow on Aasgard Pass on the way down).
routes.descentText: After topping out, descend unroped via the boot-packed couloir. Continue down toward Colchuck Lake and out via Aasgard Pass; in soft spring conditions parties have glissaded much of the Aasgard Pass descent without crampons.
routes.detailedRack: Summer: medium rack to 3 inches. Winter/mixed: 4 or more ice screws, a single rock rack, and pitons recommended depending on conditions; a 70m rope allows simul-climbing between belays.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed rock and ice protection; screw placements are conditions-dependent since the route sees thin, delaminating ice on sun-exposed entry pitches.
proTips: Route quality is highly praised — regarded by some as one of the best in Washington State
70m rope + simul-climbing speeds up the linked pitches
watchOut: Entry pitches are sun-baked and can be delaminated/thin
Rockfall risk from parties climbing ahead
knownHazards: Thin, sun-affected ice on lower pitches
Rockfall from parties above
Long, sustained, exposed climbing relative to neighboring Triple Couloirs
routes.turnaround: If the entry ice is visibly thin/delaminated from sun exposure, or if parties are stacked above you on the route, reconsider — the runnels and mixed sections above are sustained and retreat gets harder the higher you go.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (one trip report reached the base by ~5:45am from a lake camp)

routes.season: spring, per the Triple Couloirs route it belongs to
routes.bestSeason: Spring, when climbed as the entry section of Triple Couloirs.
routes.overview: The lowest of the three couloirs on Dragontail's north face, named the "Hidden Couloir" because it isn't visible from Colchuck Lake; it is the entry pitch of the Triple Couloirs route rather than an independently graded line.
routes.beta: Roughly 800 feet of 40-50° snow/ice climbing, often soloed by parties in stable conditions, before the route steepens into the technical "runnels" crux that leads to the Second Couloir.
routes.approach: From Colchuck Lake, head toward the lowest rock protrusion near the middle of the north face; two possible starts lead into the Hidden/First Couloir.
routes.descentText: Not a standalone descent — climbers either continue up through the Triple Couloirs line to the summit and descend via Aasgard Pass, or downclimb the couloir to retreat.
gear: A few pickets for a snow/ice couloir of this angle
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection typically placed on this initial moderate-angle section; most parties solo or simul-climb it.
watchOut: Often confused with a standalone route — it is actually the first section of Triple Couloirs
Snow condition/consistency dictates safety since it's frequently soloed
knownHazards: Avalanche potential on the approach slopes and within the couloir
Rockfall/icefall from above
Not visible from Colchuck Lake, making conditions hard to assess from below
routes.bail: Downclimb the couloir back toward Colchuck Lake if conditions deteriorate before committing to the runnels above.
routes.turnaround: If the couloir's snow/ice is inconsistent (e.g., breakable crust, wet-slide risk) or if the runnels above look unformed, retreat down the Hidden Couloir rather than committing higher.

pitchDetail.notes: ~800 ft, often soloed in stable conditions; leads to the technical "runnels" crux of Triple Couloirs.

routes.corrections: This is not an independently named/graded route on Mountain Project or SummitPost. Per SummitPost's Triple Couloirs page and multiple trip reports (climberkyle.com, American Alpine Institute), "Hidden Couloir" is the name for the FIRST of the three couloirs that make up the Triple Couloirs route — it is not visible from Colchuck Lake, hence the name, but it is a section of Triple Couloirs (wa_dragontail_peak_r4) rather than a separate climb. Data here describes that first-couloir section specifically; flag for review as it may belong merged into the Triple Couloirs entry rather than standing alone.

routes.season: snow-climb season, generally spring through mid-summer
routes.bestSeason: Best as a spring/early-summer snow climb while the Colchuck Glacier and gully hold consolidated snow; becomes looser and more rockfall-prone late season.
routes.overview: A moderate snow couloir climbing from Colchuck Col (the notch between Colchuck and Dragontail Peaks) up a south-facing gully to Dragontail's summit ridge, popularly known as "Pandora's Box" and catalogued on Mountain Project as Dragontail's "West Route."
routes.beta: From the top of the Colchuck Glacier at Colchuck Col, climb the snow gully on the south side to the summit ridge, cross the notch, and traverse northeast toward the true summit.
routes.approach: From the Colchuck Lake trailhead, gain the Colchuck Glacier and ascend to the col/notch between Dragontail and Colchuck Peaks (Colchuck Col).
routes.descent: Most parties descend via Aasgard Pass rather than retracing the Colchuck Glacier, to make a loop/traverse.
routes.descentText: Rather than reversing the Colchuck Glacier descent, many parties continue over Dragontail's summit and descend via Aasgard Pass back to Colchuck Lake. Aasgard Pass itself is loose, chossy terrain with real rockfall danger — care is needed.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack typically needed; this is a snow/glacier travel route requiring axe and crampons and standard glacier travel gear if crossing Colchuck Glacier crevasses.
routes.proNeeds: None beyond snow-travel gear under normal conditions; a rope/pickets may be prudent if the glacier is crevassed or the gully is icy.
whatToBring: Helmet (rockfall in Aasgard Pass area on descent)
proTips: Plan the route as a traverse: up via Colchuck Col, down via Aasgard Pass, rather than retracing your steps
watchOut: Aasgard Pass descent is loose/"chossy" with significant rockfall danger
Overnight camping in this zone requires a permit that can be hard to obtain in season
knownHazards: Rockfall on Aasgard Pass during descent
Crevasse hazard while crossing Colchuck Glacier on approach
Steep snow sections in the gully itself
routes.turnaround: If the Colchuck Glacier is icy/crevassed beyond your party's comfort, or afternoon conditions are softening the Aasgard Pass descent into rockfall-prone slop, turn back rather than push the traverse.

routes.fa: Bill Joiner, Leslie Nelson & Dave Seman, May 1974
routes.season: spring (Mar-May) preferred; possible in winter with stable avalanche conditions
routes.bestSeason: Spring is preferred for stable snow/ice; the route is also climbed in winter when avalanche conditions allow, and becomes drier/rockier by mid-summer.
routes.overview: Dragontail's classic north-face ice/mixed testpiece: three linked couloirs separated by rock/ice steps, climbing roughly 2,500 feet from Colchuck Lake's basin to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: From Colchuck Lake, approach the lowest rock protrusion near the middle of the north face and enter the Hidden (First) Couloir via one of two starts. Climb ~800 ft of 40-50° snow/ice, then tackle the steep "runnels" crux (roughly 3 pitches / 140m of 70-80° ice and mixed terrain, up to M3+), continue up the moderate-angle Second Couloir (600+ ft), and finish up the easier-angled Third Couloir (a few hundred feet, often good WI2 ice) to the summit ridge.
routes.approach: From Colchuck Lake, head toward the lowest rock protrusion roughly in the middle of the peak's north face and bear slightly left to find the two possible starts into the First (Hidden) Couloir.
routes.descent: From the summit ridge, go right to the true summit, then descend down and right to a notch and take a direct line toward Aasgard Pass; some parties instead descend above Snow Creek Glacier toward Aasgard Pass before dropping to Colchuck Lake.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, downclimb to a notch on the ridge, then head directly toward Aasgard Pass. An alternate line described in some trip reports traverses above Snow Creek Glacier en route to Aasgard Pass. From the pass, descend the standard slopes back to Colchuck Lake and out via the Colchuck Lake Trail.
gear: Medium rack: small nuts, 4-5 pitons, cams .5-2"
4-5 ice screws (short screws, e.g. 10cm, noted as worthwhile)
routes.detailedRack: A medium rack including small nuts, 4-5 pins, and cams from .5 to #2, plus 4-5 ice screws (short 10cm screws specifically called out as useful in thin conditions) and two pickets.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is often sparse by necessity — the route "requires a great deal of soloing or run-out climbing," especially through the runnels crux when ice is thin.
whatToBring: Mountaineering boots (approach can be done partly in trail shoes, swapping before the snowline)
proTips: Not recommended for beginners — comfort on steep snow and solid WI3 movement are real assets
10cm ice screws were noted as especially useful when ice is thin
Camp on the south shore of Colchuck Lake to stage the climb
watchOut: The runnels crux can be paper-thin ice over slabby rock in low-ice years, forcing a bypass via WI2+ gullies
Route is long, committing, and often run-out
Avalanche-prone slopes above Snow Creek Glacier on the descent option
knownHazards: Thin/unpredictable ice in the runnels crux
Loose, sugar-type snow reported in the Second Couloir
Avalanche terrain above Snow Creek Glacier
Long, committing, run-out climbing throughout
routes.bail: Downclimb the couloir just climbed if conditions deteriorate; retreat gets progressively harder above the runnels crux, so assess conditions carefully before committing past the First Couloir.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before entering the runnels crux if ice looks thin/rotten or if avalanche conditions are elevated on the upper mountain; this route is not a place to push marginal conditions given its length and limited retreat options.

road.name: Icicle Creek Road (FR 7600) to FR 7601
road.seasonalGate: FR 7601 gated seasonally, roughly Nov-May
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, take Icicle Creek Road 8.4 mi (in winter, foot/snowshoe travel adds ~3.5 mi from Bridge Creek Campground), then FR 7601 to the Stuart Lake trailhead.

climate.typical: Spring couloir/ice conditions dominate the popular season; a poor snow/ice year can leave the runnels thin or rotten.
climate.spring: Prime season, typically March-May.
climate.summer: Route degrades to loose rock/scree; rarely climbed.
climate.winter: Climbable with stable avalanche conditions; approach road access is more limited.

access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required for trailhead parking
access.permit: Enchantments Permit (Colchuck Zone) required for overnight camping May 15-Oct 31; outside that window self-registration typically applies
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

pitchDetail.notes: ~800 ft; often soloed in good conditions; two possible starts.
~3 pitches (140m); can be paper-thin ice over slab in low-ice years, forcing a bypass via cliffs/gullies to the right.
600+ ft; reported knee-deep loose sugar snow in some conditions.
Easy-angled but very runout, slabby mixed terrain with thin seams.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists FA as unknown; American Alpine Institute credits the FA to Bill Joiner, Leslie Nelson, and Dave Seman in May 1974, which is used here.

blurb: McClellan Peak (8,368 ft) is a summit on McClellan Ridge in the Stuart Range, Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Chelan County, forming the southern rim of the Enchantment Lakes basin in Washington's Central-East Cascades. Composed of Late Cretaceous tonalite plutons shaped by Pleistocene glaciation, it is a granite summit that is largely non-technical (class 2-3 scrambling on its standard lines) but offers a few short technical routes on subsidiary formations such as Crystal Lake Tower's 5.8 Southwest Rib. It was first climbed on July 5, 1946 by Ken Prestrud, Keith Rankin, and William Herston, and is named for Army explorer George B. McClellan, who passed through the area in 1853.

objHaz: Lingering or icy snow patches into midsummer
routes.face: Southwest Face (Crystal Creek side of the peak)
routes.overview: The standard non-technical line up McClellan Peak, climbing the mountain's southwest side above Crystal Creek from the Ingalls Creek valley to gain the summit ridge, finishing with a short class 2-3 scramble to the summit blocks.
routes.beta: Most Enchantments visitors reach McClellan via the long traverse from Snow Lakes, but the Southwest Scramble approached from Ingalls Creek and Crystal Creek reaches the same class 2-3 summit scramble from the quieter west side, outside the quota-permitted Core Enchantment Zone. From a high camp in the Crystal Creek basin, climb a rising traverse toward the saddle below Crystal Lake and up McClellan's southwest face/rib to the summit ridge, then scramble along the ridge to the summit. The final scramble is rated class 2-3 with an occasional low class 3 move; most parties climb it unroped in good conditions, though some carry a rope for margin.
routes.approach: From the Ingalls Creek Trailhead (off US-97 near Blewett Pass, milepost 178, via Ingalls Creek Road) follow the Ingalls Creek Trail (#1215) west/southwest for roughly 7.5 miles to the Crystal Creek crossing. Leave the maintained trail and follow a climbers' path up the Crystal Creek drainage; potential camps exist near 4,500 ft, 4,800 ft, and a recommended ~6,000 ft basin below Crystal Lake, reported as the best camp option. From the basin, make a rising traverse toward the saddle below Crystal Lake and continue up onto McClellan's southwest face to reach the summit ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the southwest-scramble line back to the Crystal Creek basin and out the Ingalls Creek Trail; alternatively, continue north over the ridge into the Enchantments and exit via Snow Lakes or Stuart/Colchuck Lake as a one-way traverse (requires a shuttle and, if overnighting in the Core Zone, a wilderness permit).
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the southwest-face scramble to the saddle below Crystal Lake, then descend the rising traverse back into the Crystal Creek drainage to regain camp and the Ingalls Creek Trail for the roughly 7.5-mile hike out. Loose talus and occasional snow patches slow the descent relative to the ascent. Parties combining McClellan with a full Enchantments traverse instead continue north over the basin's south rim and exit via Snow Lakes (substantially longer) or the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake/Aasgard Pass trail; both alternate exits involve additional scrambling and, if overnighting inside the Core Enchantment Zone, require a wilderness permit.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as an overnight, or as a long single push by fit parties: Day 1 - hike the Ingalls Creek Trail to a camp in the Crystal Creek basin (~7.5+ miles, camp near 4,500-6,000 ft); Day 2 - climb the southwest scramble to McClellan's summit, return to camp, and hike out (or continue over the ridge into the Enchantments to exit via Snow Lakes/Colchuck as a traverse).
routes.bail: There are no fixed rappel anchors on this route; retreat is by downclimbing the same southwest-face scramble line back to the saddle below Crystal Lake and the Crystal Creek basin. There is no quick technical bail option, so turn back early if weather or party pace makes the class 2-3 terrain unsafe.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time based on the slowest party member's pace back to camp/trailhead well before dark, with margin for common summer afternoon thunderstorms over the Enchantments and for the fact that downclimbing loose, exposed class 2-3 terrain is markedly slower than ascending it.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed roughly July through September, once the Ingalls Creek Trail and Crystal Creek basin lose their snow; trip reports note lingering, occasionally icy snow patches on the approach even into July. Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Enchantments in summer, favoring an early start.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack is needed for the standard class 2-3 scramble. Some parties carry a rope with a few slings and a light rack (small cams/nuts) for the short low class-3 sections or as a safety margin, but most climb it unroped in good conditions.
whatToBring: Map and compass/GPS (route is cross-country above Crystal Creek)
Extra water (dry stretches along the Ingalls Creek Trail)
Layers for the exposed ridge and summit
Self-issue wilderness permit for Ingalls Creek Trail camping
Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
proTips: The Ingalls Creek/Crystal Creek approach avoids the Core Enchantment Zone quota permit that governs the more popular Snow Lakes side.
The roughly 6,000 ft basin below Crystal Lake is reported as the best of the available camps and shortens summit day.
McClellan is often combined with nearby Little Annapurna (about 1.3 miles west) for an efficient two-summit outing when approaching via the Enchantments.
watchOut: Loose talus and blocky rock on the southwest-face scramble.
Snow patches can linger on the approach and route into midsummer and may be icy.
Water can be scarce along stretches of the Ingalls Creek Trail; plan resupply points.
Cross-country route-finding above Crystal Creek — no maintained trail or consistent cairns.
knownHazards: Rockfall/loose rock on class 2-3 terrain, especially with multiple parties on route.
Residual snow/ice on approach slopes into early summer.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common over the Enchantments basin in summer.
Exposure on the final ridge scramble to the summit blocks.

pitchDetail.notes: Ingalls Creek Trail to the Crystal Creek crossing, ~7.5 miles of gentle forested trail.
Cross-country ascent through forest and talus to a high camp near the 6,000 ft basin below Crystal Lake.
Rising traverse to the saddle below Crystal Lake, then up McClellan's southwest face to the summit ridge.
Short final scramble along the ridge to the summit blocks.

road.name: Ingalls Creek Road
road.driveNote: From US-97 near Blewett Pass (milepost 178), turn onto Ingalls Creek Road and follow it about a mile to the trailhead; also reachable from Leavenworth via US-2 east then US-97 south.

climate.forecastZone: East Slopes Central Cascades / Stuart Range
climate.spring: Significant snowpack persists on approach slopes; upper Ingalls Creek/Crystal Creek terrain often still snow-covered.
climate.summer: Prime climbing season; warm days with common afternoon thunderstorm risk, trail generally dry by mid-summer.
climate.fall: Cooling, often stable weather can extend the season into September; early-season snow possible by October.
climate.winter: Heavy snowpack and avalanche terrain on approach slopes; not a standard winter objective.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Leavenworth Ranger District), Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required for Ingalls Creek Trailhead parking
access.permit: Self-issue wilderness permit at the trailhead; if the route is combined with a traverse and overnight stay inside the Core Enchantment Zone, a quota permit (May 15-Oct 31, lottery or daily walk-up) is required
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

timing.recommendedStart: Early/pre-dawn departure from high camp to summit and descend below treeline before typical afternoon thunderstorms build

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff via RiverCom Dispatch: 509-663-9911 (non-emergency); dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Leavenworth Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest: (509) 548-2550, 600 Sherbourne St, Leavenworth, WA 98826
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-5815
emergency.notes: Chelan County Volunteer Search & Rescue (CCVSAR) supports backcountry rescues in this area; cell coverage is unreliable to absent in the Crystal Creek drainage and Enchantments basins.

routes.corrections: The peak's given elevationFt (8,367 ft) is effectively confirmed by Wikipedia, Mountain Project, and Peakbagger-derived data, all citing 8,368 ft (2,551 m) — a 1 ft rounding difference, not a substantive conflict, so the sourced summit elevation (highPointFt 8368) is used here.
